Overall, Alan Wake is a solid title, But falls short when it comes to mechanics and fluidity on movement.

[h1] The Good [/h1]

- Overall, the story is super solid and feel like YOU are actually Alan Wake himself, not some random spectator.

- The Light mechanic is a very good gameplay element that assures you see your target before your target sees you. 

- The graphics look classic, and still holdup today when it comes to realism. 

[h1] The Meh [/h1]

- The gunplay is okay, I feel as if it gets delayed randomly when im shooting. (I could be shooting a juggernaut and out of nowhere my gun will stop shooting for a second and let me shoot my enemy again)

- The Fps is playable but I sorta want to be able to play at my monitor's refresh rate (100 fps)

- Overall the movement is okay, but the jumping has resulted in me dying a couple times from how RNG it is on working properly

[h1] The Bad [/h1]

- The Ear peircing sounds in every episode get so f%&&&$^ annoying when im in the middle of gameplay. It is so loud for absolutely no reason and has resulted in me getting a headache a couple times.

- Why is there absolutely no vaulting??? some obstacles require jumping and vaulting onto them would really help instead of me jumping for a minute just to get past it. 

- The difficulty balance is off, some levels you get not much ammo, but when there are less enemies, you get more ammo?? 

[h1] Overall [/h1]

[h2] 7.5/10 [/h2]

Alan Wake is a solid game when it comes to story, and graphics. However, where it falls short is in it's mechanics like movement and overall combat. 

I recommend buying if you have the money

225778817.review:
Alan Wake's writing is trash. His books sell, he is recognized wherever he goes, he's met by sincere fans of his work, but every time I picked up a page of his manuscript, every time he tried to narrate what was happening to him, the same thought entered my mind. Alan Wake is a bad writer. His prose is clunky and dull, purely describing the exact structures of events at a roughly 5th grade reading level and throwing in shoddy similes when he realizes he needs to spice it up. Maybe this makes him the worst possible candidate for a writer whose works are brought to terrifying reality. Maybe trash is exactly what we needed.

To be clear here--I adore how much I don't like Wake's writing. It feels really expertly crafted to be overly gritty pulp, and it crafts a really excellent vibe for the whole game. Unfortunately, what I think about [i]Alan Wake[/i]'s writing, the game itself, is... eh. It struggles to really grip me and while I think the comically obvious homages to [i]Twin Peaks[/i] are cute they make the game feel like it struggles to hold an identity all its own. When you can feel that identity, however, it really shines. The Old Gods of Asgard in particular are a really delightful set of characters that I'm excited to see again. Remedy was clearly working with a lot of great ideas here, and even if they don't all work as well as they could I can see how the much more beloved [i]Alan Wake II[/i] could go further with them.

If it seems I've been neglecting talking about gameplay in this review of a video game, that's for a reason: it's just pretty unmemorable. Remedy is great at gunplay, naturally, and I like the idea of the flashlight mechanic in concept, but it comes together to make something that's perfectly fine and definitely does not hold its ground for the entirety of a 12 hour campaign. It needs either more combat variety or more forms of interaction as a whole. In particular I really did not like dealing with the object-possessing poltergeists. I can imagine a lot of people trailing off of [i]Alan Wake[/i] after a few hours, and that's because I did when I first played it. As with everything, though, when it works it really works. This is a classic middling early 2010s shooter with a lot of potential, and I hope that the rumors I hear are correct, and its sequel truly does deliver on it.

[h1]Alan Wake: A 10/10 game about writer’s block. No seriously.[/h1]

A Stephen King simulator where your biggest enemies are:
- Darkness
- Batteries
- Cardio

The atmosphere is incredible. It feels like binge-watching a cursed late-night TV thriller while a forest personally hates you.

The gameplay loop is basically:
[quote]
flashlight → gun → reload → run for 7 seconds → Alan starts breathing like he smoked 4 packs a day since birth
[/quote]

Combat gets repetitive, but the story carries the entire game on its back like an exhausted novelist on a deadline.

Also shoutout to Remedy for making Energizer batteries feel like legendary loot.

[b]Pro tip:[/b]
Don’t think too hard about the story or you’ll end up looking like Charlie from It’s Always Sunny explaining conspiracy theories with red strings on a wall.

[b]10/10[/b]

225336222.review:
There's a good game in here somewhere, and I'm sure that place is spelt as Alan Wake 2. I played this game because I've heard such great things about the sequel, but man this game was really a slog to get through. 

The game is all about writing, which, funnily enough, is its greatest strength. The premise of his book coming to life and finding pages scattered foreshadowing events bound to happen was really engaging and memorable. I also loved some of the little flourishes this game has, like the Night Springs episodes that would play on the TV or watching Alan writing his manuscript that is now coming to life. Brightfalls is a nice location with some cool scenery, but the game never fully utilises its potential, opting instead to have bland forest followed by identical bland forest.

Where this game fails is arguably the most important thing - the gameplay. It's just so incredibly monotonous and unengaging (so much so that I dropped the game 2h in before finally finishing it purely so I could get a more optimal experience from the second one). The combat is unique at first with the emphasis on light and darkness, but it never explores it or expands it. The first combat encounter is identical to the last - shine torch to make enemy vulnerable, then shoot them, then they die. There is never a single enemy that shakes this up or forces the player to engage in the combat in different ways. It's just the same thing over and over and over; it's so boring. 

Also, ♥♥♥♥ this game for its annoying ass stamina bar. It runs out so quickly and yet the game is comprised so much of running from point A to B it's practically a walking simulator. Why they made it so measly is beyond me it was so grating. How is it fun to have to stop to let Mr Wake catch his breath every 2 seconds just to reach the next combat encounter identical to the last? It would've been so easy to solve also. Just do what Elden Ring does - in combat the stamina bar exists to increase difficulty and out of combat it doesn't exist to prevent this. The audio mixing was terrible also.

Unless you are really desperate for a game with a meta story or just want to own all the Remedy games, go for it. If you want a fun game or are like me and played this to play the sequel, just watch a YouTube playthrough.

Alan Wake has a really enjoyable and creative story. Alan is a great main character, and I was genuinely intrigued by the events unfolding from start to finish. I loved the concept of darkness being the enemy and using light as a weapon. 
There’s something incredibly satisfying about shining your flashlight on shadowy enemies to weaken them before finishing them off with gunfire. It feels fresh and ties perfectly into the game’s themes.
The atmosphere and suspense are fantastic throughout the whole game. The eerie forests, the small town mystery, and the constant sense of dread were perfect.
If I had one real complaint, it’s that there are simply too many enemies at times. The game occasionally throws massive waves of enemies at you back to back to back, which starts to hurt the immersion.
That small issue aside, everything else about Alan Wake is excellent. The story, the atmosphere, and the unique gameplay hook all come together to create a very special experience.
Highly recommended, especially if you enjoy story driven games with a strong sense of atmosphere and mystery.

224817976.review:
Alan Wake is not a perfect game, but it is a very memorable one.

The core idea is simple. You fight darkness with light. It sounds basic, but it works surprisingly well and gives the combat its own identity. The real highlight, though, is the story. It feels like stepping into a psychological thriller, with a constant sense of mystery that keeps pulling you forward.
The early game is rough. The loop is repetitive, and most of the time, you are just running through forests, fighting the same enemies again and again. It can feel boring at first.
However, if you stick with it, the game gets much better.
As you progress, later chapters introduce new mechanics at a steady pace. Driving sections, poltergeist objects, chase sequences, and more variety in encounters keep things fresh. Chapters 4 and 5 are where everything clicks. Combat also becomes more fun once you unlock tools like flashbangs and flares, especially when dealing with groups.
There are still issues. Combat can feel clunky. Alan is slower than his enemies, so you are often forced to fight. Enemy spawns can be annoying too, especially when you get hit from outside your view.
Even with those flaws, the game delivers something special. Strong atmosphere, memorable characters, and a story that feels different from most games.

If you like Max Payne or enjoy the vibe of Stephen King stories, this is an easy pick.

However, the combat is quite unique. Using a flashlight or light to shine on enemies, then shooting them with a firearm. The plot is quite interesting to follow, bringing scary and touching moments all at once.

I don't think it's a bad game, I just feel that it's not for everyone.

It's not as fun as I thought, however the story seems interesting, but a little confusing. There are some elements, like manuscript pages, radio transmissions and videos, which provides even more details to the story if you take the time to check them.

The main gameplay mechanic (flashlight + gun) it's somewhat cool, but does feel repetitive over time; basically you "stun and shoot" the enemies.

Something that ruined the experience (at least for me) was that the 3rd person camera perspective constantly moves to the left, leaving the character on the right. You can manually switch it back to the conventional position (camera on the right, character on the left), but eventually it returns to the uncomfortable position.

If you're interested on the story, I suggest to just watch an in-depth video about the lore of the game; playing it it's not worth the time.

Gameplay is solid, but the constant enemy waves can get a bit overwhelming at times. Sometimes it feels like the game doesn’t give you a breather and just keeps throwing enemies at you nonstop. 224277802.review:
Alan Wake doesn’t really feel like a typical game—it plays out more like a gripping web series. Each chapter follows a familiar but effective rhythm: it hooks you with a strong opening, slows things down to build tension, and then hits you with a solid peak by the end. That episodic structure makes it hard to put down, because you’re always curious about what the next “episode” will reveal.
The narration is easily one of its strongest aspects. It’s so well written that the line between reality and fiction constantly blurs. You’re never fully sure whether what you’re seeing is actually happening or just part of a dream or story unfolding in Alan’s mind. That sense of uncertainty sticks with you throughout the game and keeps you engaged until it finally decides to show its hand.
That said, the character relationships could’ve been developed a bit more. The bonding is there, and it works well enough for the story, but it never reaches the depth it feels like it could have. It’s just something that could’ve elevated the experience further.
Gameplay-wise, I can see why some players might find it repetitive, especially toward the later stages. But personally, it didn’t bother me much—the atmosphere and storytelling carried it through, and I was invested enough to keep going.
Overall, it’s a great game and a memorable experience. I really enjoyed my time with it, and it definitely leaves you wanting more. Now onto Alan Wake 2 , it sucks thats it is not available on steam.

224173230.review:
Type of game: Story and narrative action games
Gameplay: Okay-ish to poor

My personal opinions: 

[h1] Cons [/h1]

Credits where credits is due, this game is made in 2010, that was ages ago, so i will not say anything much other than this game has some bugs here and there in some areas. Lets start with the negatives, there is a place where if you dont know you cant go there, alan wake just dies. A short fall might also kill your character and going up the stairs will also make him stop for no reason and you have to wiggle around before you can even move. Not to mention the character you play as has the most worse kind of stamina i ever experience, you can run for about a few seconds and then he is winded and that also affects his dodge where he will touch the ground for about 1 seconds making him open to attack easily. How was the story? Mid, its nothing crazy but its also not that bad either, there is a lot of things that can be done in this kind of game. If the focus of such games is based around story, it needs to be focused more on that aspect, where you flesh out each character instead of having them appear for a short while where either they die or we dont see them afterwards anymore after their purpose is fulfilled until of course alan wake 2, which isnt on steam and i dont feel like playing alan 2 on a different platforms. The game can also reduce enemies spawn and not having an unlimited spawning on them in some dark areas, thats where you take away the experience of wanting a good story game to where it feels more like an action shooter survival game. And what i dont get is, why in the hell does alan wake KEEP LOSING HIS GOOD FLASHLIGHT AND ALL THE GUNS AND THINGS YOU SAVED UP EVEN IF WE ARE ON THE SAME CHAPTER?! I dont get it, all those stuff you saved up feels meaningless, making you feel as if whats the point of saving them, just waste those idoits with whatever you have. And this is personal for me, for a story based game and narrative, yapping is just natural, but juggling balls in the arse if its just too hard for me to just listen to what they wanted to say and replaying levels isnt as fun when you cant skip them, for example, the dr hartman area chapter, i really hate this with a passion and i mean it, this is the biggest negatives for me and is one of the pain in the arse moment when getting all the complete achievements. And in some areas, my fan just went overdrive like i am running the most extensive stress test on my GPU, which is weird and there is a frame drop for some places, but only in a few areas, its not really taking away the experience but its a negative for this game as well.

[h1] Pros [/h1]

Now for the plus positives, as said, its not a really good story game, but it is a good action shooting game but added with the two step verification where you got to burn away the emo on them before you can make them meet god. I enjoy the action too much where you got to survive being surrounded by the taken on the concert area and i wish there was more of that. One of the unforgettable moments in this game. What else... eh... thats all the positives really, its such a shame i cant think of whatever else that is great with the game, thats all there is to it that is on my mind. You can pick this game up if you care more about the shooting aspect more than the story, this game just isnt it for a good story game.

My final verdict: 4/10

224084165.review:
The story and the experience of Alan Wake is superior in gaming. It’s probably my favourite of all time. At least in my top 5 favourite games of all time. For the sake of transparency, I finished the game on the Xbox 360 not the PC Steam version. I did play the DLCs on Steam though which I will get to. Again, I did not play the PC version or the Remastered edition so I am commenting about the main game on the Xbox360. The game suffers from some extensive jankyness, not enough to sour your experience but enough to be noticeable. I am still not a fan of the combat but in all fairness, it is growing on me. The more you play the more you get used to it. Everything else about this game is brilliant and everyone should play it at least once. It goes from combat to story content seamlessly. All the rich depth and additional material that Remedy includes make the experience seem special and a pleasure to behold. The music and voice acting are brilliant. Amazing, I loved it all!

Alan Wake is so well balanced it’s not funny. They realized that if they didn’t end it when they did it would have almost worn out its welcome. So, it ends at exactly at the right time. I think people might have had a different opinion of it otherwise. 

Now I will talk about the 2 DLCs I played in the PC version on Steam. The Signal I did not like that much! In fact, I almost rage quit it. It felt like the wheels were starting to come off. I thought there was no purpose to it which was such a contrast to the main game. The combat is atrocious. Way too repetitive as all the enemies seemed the same old thing. It was a real grind! It is brutal! It suffers from unbalanced combat to story but the story you get helps you to try to forget that. It does introduce the “words” mechanics to the game which was good. Play if you are a completest and want to get the story elements. Otherwise, you can skip this DLC. I don’t think the additional story you get is that crucial to the overall Remedyverse. 

Now the second DLC The Writer is brilliant. I loved it!. It is perfectly balanced combat to story. It innovates by expanding the “word” mechanic. It introduces some great environmental puzzles and platforming. It’s still a bit janky in a few spots on the PC as well but not too bad. Maybe the remastered version fixes that? I would say that this is a worthy DLC for Alan Wake and a must play!

224040775.review:
I played this game not really knowing what to expect, and it ended up being one of those games that sticks with you after finishing it. At first it feels pretty simple, you arrive in a small town, things seem normal enough, then little by little everything starts becoming strange. The game is really good at building that uneasy feeling where you know something is wrong even when nothing major is happening yet. That was probably my favorite part of the whole experience. The atmosphere carries the game hard. Walking through dark forests, hearing random sounds in the distance, seeing lights through the fog, it all creates this constant tension. Even when I was just moving from one place to another, I felt on edge. The town itself also feels memorable, not huge or overly detailed, but it has personality. It feels like a place with secrets.

Combat was fun at first and stayed decent throughout. Using light as part of the gameplay is a cool mechanic and makes fights feel different from standard shooter stuff. It can get repetitive later on, especially during longer sections when enemies keep showing up, but I never hated it. It was more like something I pushed through because I wanted to know what happened next. That says a lot about how strong the story is. I liked that the game keeps feeding you small mysteries instead of dumping everything at once. You find things along the way that make you question what is real and what is not. I will avoid spoilers, but there were a few moments where I genuinely stopped and thought okay, now I need answers.

Alan himself is also more interesting than I expected. He feels flawed and stressed in a believable way, which fits the situation. He is not some fearless hero pretending everything is fine. The game does have some rough edges. Movement can feel a bit clunky, and a few sections drag longer than they need to. But honestly, I stopped caring about that because the mood and story kept pulling me forward. By the end, I just sat there for a minute thinking about everything that happened. For me, Alan Wake is one of those games where the experience matters more than perfect mechanics. If you like getting pulled into a strange world and slowly uncovering what is going on, it is absolutely worth playing.

224023338.review: alan wake more like alan sleep

224022158.review:
its very, very easy to argue how this is a step down from the max payne games, especially if you atomize it to its individual components (does it have "better gameplay" a "better plot", ect), but esp after soaking on it for a while i just cant help but like it even more. it is in some ways the most surface-level remedy story so far, consistently dredging ideas that were confidentially understated in max payne 2 right up to the level of unmissable text and flashy literal narrative mechanics. so when i sit back to consider the whole thing, esp after playing the dlc, its amazing how many strange little corners there still are,,,the game is explaining things to you so often that sometimes u dont catch when something has been left vague or subtextual until youre scrubbing over it later. its a great effort that still manages to contain the comedically mediocre sledgehammer prose and story written by alan himself. i still giggle at "a gruesome slushie"

obviously i am a soft mark for this kind of subjective reality, dream logic, twin peaks references joint. but there are distinctive qualities to alan wake as a story and a game that make it coalesce in my mind way more vividly than i assumed it would even a few hours before it was over. the core gameplay is, once you get a grasp on it, a surprisingly sturdy remix of some remaining max payne elements...those games are based around scrappy, improvisational, often trial-and-error action setpieces, and it is much the same here. its not as smooth as the max paynes, sometimes in ways that feel compromised by the technology leaps (like the cinematic Autododges) but sometimes in ways where alan's clunky powerlessness really does create something visceral and involving. its a really nice step up in enemy variety and increased distinctive uses for every weapon, both are still quite minimalist by most standards but they do well to strengthen the individuality of the encounter design beyond that of even max payne 1. and as this encounter driven clumsy action game, there is something really fun about it honestly, far more than i expected considering many people who like this game basically apologize for its gameplay! where the game really clicked for me was in the second chapter or so, when it became clear that the focus was Group Encounters that need to be either crowd controlled or physically circumvented or some combination of the two....there is a lot of enjoyable, frantic adrenaline to be found there, from swiping around to make sure youre not getting flanked to finding the exact right time and place to deploy your flare and just make a break for the nearest light. ive never played anything quite like it in either action or survival horror...the way you start each battle powerless and have to arduously cut a successful path through it remained a unique pleasure through all of the base game. while far from a smooth action exciting, there is a lot of memorably exciting moments, and while far from a punishing Survival Horror Resource Economy, you are still asked to make choices that at least Feel like something while you make them

but i think what really sealed it for me was that, while the concept seems ripe for pure solipsistic dream reality, the unique feel of the story does genuinely come down to the fact that ultimately it is taking place in a Real World with Real Other People. they are beholden to the magic of alan's story, but one of the rules of the magic is that it must be consistent and true to itself, which helps everyones behavior still feel like an authentic thing for alan to bounce off of. its slightly horrific in that everyone, including himself, is an unwitting pawn to one man's ideas, but it is also beautiful in that it is a testament to how art is not cut wholesale from a singular genius stuck in a room somewhere. something i appreciate about the interviews ive seen with sam lake is how good he is at emphasizing remedy as a Team, the games as products of a creative collective rather than just a vehicle for pure autuerism, and i think that mindset carries through to alan wake's view of what it means to write. its not all about individual whims, otherwise he could just write an instant happily ever after. he must write something that feels True...true to itself, to all of its moving parts, to its participants. rather than a vehicle for ego, he carries Responsibility to his audience and his collaborators. there is nuance about this...its hard to read heartman and the darkness wearing barbras face as anything but condemnations of the unproductive ways that outside forces can clamp down on creativity...and ofc the game as a whole is a statement of creative defiance, trying to make something vastly different than max payne. but it never feels like a one-note celebration of the individual Supergenius...if it was, it would have picked a better writer than alan wake.

all of these idiosyncrasies are really honed and clarified by the dlc. the first in particular shapes the unique combat encounters into something infuriatingly bitter and masochistic and player-hostile, i hated it while i actually played it but it didnt take me long afterwards to turn around on it...its a productive, narratively resonant bitterness, playable black coffee. the second dlc throws more spectacle and power fantasy at you in a way that might feel cheap on its own but does feel meaningful in contrast to the first dlc...but in all of it, alan is truly Trapped In His Own Head for the first time, and the sense of emotional claustrophobia this creates is beautifully crushing. it makes you realize how comforting the presence of (actual) outside humans was, even the antagonistic ones.

its an easy game to nitpick, or call foul on its large structural decisions. there are times both in its narrative and its gameplay where, despite all the idiosyncrasies, its perhaps a bit too conventional for its own good...and ofc, with the highly acclaimed followup, im sure its also easy to write this off as essentially Necessary Groundwork for a later more ambitious game, but that on its own is a bit un-glamorous in its narrative tablesetting. but even if i agreed with all of that, i find the sheer Personality of the production every bit as irresistible as i did in the max paynes....these games feel, more than most of their relative budgets, like they were made by People, and perhaps thats why some sort of truth about humans or artmaking or destiny is bound to jump out at you at least once. with so much explanation of its own subtexts, its perhaps not Deep, but it is True To Itself...strong magic!

223953966.review:
Alan Wake is a gripping, atmospheric thriller that feels like a lost season of Twin Peaks turned into a video game.  It masterfully blends psychological horror, cinematic storytelling, and light-based combat into a uniquely intense experience. 

Every shadow pulses with danger—your flashlight isn’t just for seeing, it’s your weapon. Shine to weaken, then shoot. It’s tense, smart, and totally original. 

With a haunting Pacific Northwest setting, a mind-bending plot, and a killer soundtrack, Alan Wake doesn’t just scare you—it gets under your skin. 

"Pure nightmare fuel—brilliant, bold, and unforgettable."

223886712.review:
This is one of the best games I have ever played in my life, if not THE best game I've ever played. All my life of playing games, which I haven't played too many games but enough to have a taste for certain games.. I suppose.. but so many games just never spoke to me the way that the Alan Wake games have spoken to me. There is actual depth behind the character's, the atmosphere is beautiful and amazing, the character's are interesting and lovable, the music is top notch, and the gameplay is simple but enjoyable even if it's not necessarily the most stimulating gameplay as a multiplayer shooter might be which is COMPLETELY fine with me because I prefer laid back story games over multiplayer overstimulation nonsense. The character's in this game actually speak to me where as other character's I've played as, sure I liked them a lot-- but I could never really relate to them. I mean I might've been able to relate to some emotions they experienced, but it just isn't the same as actually feeling like you've experienced exactly what the character you're playing as has experienced. It makes you actually sympathize for everyone involved in this series and I'm definitely not saying I've experienced all that Alan Wake has experienced but I have experienced some of what he's gone through and thinks about himself which is so... just meaningful and amazing to me. All of these character's feel so human, especially in the second game as well as American Nightmare but completely enhanced in Alan Wake II, let alone the first Alan Wake. I actually worry about what every character is going through and feel so much for what they're experiencing and I love when games and media makes me feel that way. Human.

I love what this game was inspired by and I love every little detail, love, and care that was put into it. I have always loved the atmosphere that the games of Alan Wake puts you through.. whether it be a little cozy town out in the woods of Washington, the twilight zone of the Arizona desert with a motel and a little 1950s inspired diner, or the dark and gritty city of New York. The way that this game's atmosphere is portrayed is just absolutely stunning to me and I wish I could experience it in real life, not to mention it is just so fitting for the story that is given to us in each game. It completely sets the mood perfectly for each game even if some of it has it's silly moments. It just shows what Alan Wake is dealing with and what ideas he has gone through just to get where he is in the series now. The world building in this game is just... RAAAH.. I love it so much and I like most things that are introduced into this "universe" despite some skepticisms.. I still watch and listen with open ears and burning eyes as my retinas burn into flames after staring at my laptop or tv screen after having played the games for hours on end.

The music is original and amazing even with the added music from other artists, the soundtrack portions are just as magnificent as the songs sung by people that were placed in the games. There's not many songs in the Alan Wake series that I dislike. They are all just such fitting pieces and I love how they tie everything together.. and how much they so beautifully and emotionally do it as well. I love listening to the soundtrack of this game whenever I can. It is so beautiful or just hard af.. to put it in a more silly way but genuinely, you cannot tell me in any of the games you didn't rock out to any of The Poets of the Fall's music or even the other added artist's music when you play Alan Wake II. My favorite piece from the game is "The Clicker" in the main game but especially Alice's version in the DLCs of the first game. It is so pretty and it speaks to me emotionally. Not to sound like I'm harping the game or anything but I genuinely just really do love this game and what has been put into it.

I know this is a review for the first game but considering that Alan Wake II isn't on here, I figured I would just write a review about how I felt about all three of the games in this review. I don't care if this game isn't in other people's tastes, it is in mine and I love this game series so much and it makes me so happy even if it has it's flaws or other tiny things I might've not completely enjoyed. The things I disliked are so insignificant compared to the things that I do like and love about the games. I'm so glad to have played these game's in my lifetime and yes, I EXTREMELY and WHOLEHEARTEDLY recommend this game to EVERYONE! I think everyone should try this game out and get to experience the world of Alan Wake even if you don't end up liking it-- I still think it's a story worth seeing!

I love you, Alan Wake games, Sam Lake, and Remedy for blessing me with the existence of this game as well as the friend who introduced me back when they did.. you know who you are if you're reading this, lol. I hope to be blessed with more amazing games like this in my lifetime that actually make me feel something and enjoy playing it in ways I never had enjoyed playing any other game before.

I LOVE THIS GAME!!!!

223836887.review:
Alan Wake: A Masterclass in Atmospheric Horror
Alan Wake, developed by Remedy Entertainment and released in 2010, is a groundbreaking psychological horror game that blends compelling storytelling with innovative gameplay mechanics.
What stands out:
Narrative depth. The game follows Alan Wake, a struggling writer who finds himself trapped in a mysterious town where his worst nightmares come to life. The plot is rich with literary references, surreal imagery, and a twisty mystery that keeps you hooked until the very end.
Unique gameplay. Instead of traditional ammo, players use light (flashlights, spotlights) to combat dark beings. This mechanic adds a strategic layer — you must constantly manage light sources while fighting enemies. It’s both challenging and satisfying.
Atmosphere. The eerie, dreamlike environment of Bright Falls is masterfully crafted. The lighting, sound design, and ambient music create an unsettling yet captivating moo

223825496.review:
Alan Wake is a game that is made with love.

Love and wits to be honest.

It’s not a perfect game, its gameplay can be repetitive and full of what feels like elongated unnecessary obstacles, but it is a game that otherwise moves to its own rhythm.

Throughout its six episodes this Lynchian Twilight Zone descent into madness pulls the likes of moments and mechanics that I haven’t seen in any other game.

From the episodic structure, to the manuscript discovery drip feeding of narrative, the splashy combat system, the third person narration - key moments that swing for the fences.

Hell, the tapping R to reload (your reload speed determined by this) is a brilliant mechanic that upends such a simple action and I’m shocked other survival horror games haven’t pulled this off since.

And to even call Alan Wake a survival horror is reductive. It’s in all ways its own beast as it blends a world of mystery and self awareness.

It’s a delicate dance, begging you to engage with its twisting tale while dazzling you with meta spectacle.

I call it a “waltz” - a back and forth with the player as you lean in to its shadows and sway back to its bright moments of snide jest.

It’s this looping flow that creates the beauty of its experience, I found myself constantly engaged and smiling, and that smiling is what opened me up to its engagement.

It’s hard to explain, but it’s the games secret weapon. It’s the thing that transforms Al’s story from a convoluted collection of ideas into a dive of the abyss.

Its safety built up patiently, wearing down your guard with a smile. A needle drop at the end of an episode, the following “previously on” bit, a best friend named Berry, the third person narration, the witty live action dubbed over Night Springs episodes, voyeuristically observing your past self, two comedic old brothers, the manuscript pages giving you info you don’t particularly need - but expand nonetheless.

It’s an incredible balance that I truly have never seen in another title.

It’s wholly its own thing, full of its own significant choices that make me question its reality and build theories - which is something I haven’t done with a piece of media in a long time. Churning my mental energy over ideas that feel on the precipice of truth, hanging on the ambiguity left behind with a strong sense of conviction by the artists hand.

And it’s why this is such a staple in video game recommendation.

Because games are expensive, and especially now, games are safe, and because of this we get the same game with all the same mechanics and concepts and ideas under different skin - and that’s not always a bad thing.

But, when a game does something so particularly different, so brilliantly risky, a complete swing for the fences - and pulls it off to such a particular outcome -

it demands eyes.

223663658.review:
I desperately want to love this game because the atmosphere and story are fantastic, and I'm intrigued by how all the Remedy games fit together.  Unfortunately, the gameplay itself is just...really bad.   I honestly do not understand all the positive reviews (and critical acclaim) this game has received.  I am VERY easily amused, but after four hours, I'm tapping out.  This has the worst combat of any AAA game I have ever played.  :(

Resident Evil's ammo scarcity works because there are ways around it (melee, hiding, running) but Alan Wake is literally just scripted enemy encounters with waves of bullet sponge enemies.  And I mean waves...this game is clearly focused on combat.  Also, the flashlight mechanic is kind of dopey.

Story is great!  Guess I'll watch a summary on youtube though.

223576680.review:
You know how video games have great shooting mechanic or movement, graphics, controls? Well, this game has almost none of that (except lighting maybe) , but what it has is one of the best story telling and atmospheres i have ever seen in a video game, most probably in the top 10 in those 2 categories. If you have good attention to detail, only then can you come to fully appreciate this game. Manuscripts in the game when combined with the story and all little radio and tv details spread out throughout the game give the player so much satisfaction  because once you start to piece together all the things and that moment when the story becomes clearer  in front of you, that feeling alone makes this a 9/10 game.  
         Be aware, that you can run through this game without knowing much about the story if you're not paying attention ,so , an advice: play it when well rested if possible.
          Negatives: the game shows it's age with clunky movement, medium shooting, not that much great graphics
        Positives: lighting accompanied with atmospheric fog, dark tone of the color palete, shadows very well placed make up for negatives. Story is top notch and honestly , if you have time, taking some notes on the side will make you understand everything extremely well, or at least much better. Worth a couple of replays cause of story alone, cannot stress that enough.
            Be ready for God knows how many , well placed, jumpscares.

223497660.review: Alan Wake is not just a game, it’s a dark and intense psychological journey. It follows a writer trapped in a mysterious nightmare, where reality and fiction start to blur. The game leans heavily into its story, showing how fear and isolation can slowly break someone down. Every step deeper into the darkness feels more unsettling, and it keeps you hooked on what’s real and what isn’t. The atmosphere is what really makes it stand out. The world feels eerie and unpredictable, and the flashlight-based combat adds constant tension, making even simple encounters feel stressful. The story being told like episodes makes it hard to stop playing, and the music at the end of every episode is a chef's kiss and sound design hit perfectly to build suspense. The only downside is that the gameplay can get repetitive, and movement can feel a bit slow at times. But honestly, the experience is so immersive that it doesn’t matter much. By the end of the game, you just sit there thinking about everything that happened. It sticks with you.

223015096.review:
Alan Wake and honestly it’s one of those games that sticks with you after you close it

The best part is the storytelling. It feels like you’re playing through a psychological thriller series instead of a typical game. The atmosphere is amazing dark forests, creepy silence. The light vs darkness mechanic is simple but really satisfying.

The gameplay can feel a bit repetitive at times. You’ll be doing similar combat encounters again and again, and it’s not as smooth as modern games but the story and vibe easily carry it through those moments.

The DLCs get even more weird and mind-bending, which I actually liked. It makes you think rather than just giving clear answers.

Overall, Alan Wake isn’t perfect, but it’s special. If you like story-driven games with a strong atmosphere, it’s definitely worth playing.

8.5/10

222439777.review: It´s quite a good game. The writing is great, and the whole world is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ incredible in terms of ideas and lore: It has so much potential in terms of gameplay and in storytelling. The characters are well written (The relevant ones, the rest are kinda forgettable), the plot develops in an interesting way and that makes you wonder what to expect next, and Im a big fan of the concept of the light as literal guidance and saviour. Besides, you can feel that Remedy vibe to the game; so if you were into Max Payne, you're gonna like this one: Fair length of the missions, gloomy, main character monologues about deep stuff, etc. I think that the years have taking a toll on how repetitive the mission structure is, or their scenarios, but the gunplay is well executed and the flashlight is real fun to use. I understand why many hold this title dear to their hearts: It is flawed, but the concept, story and world shines way above such issues.

222294562.review:
Starting off with some heated points..
- Even tho the main character we play as is called Alan Wake, several times during the game he sleeps or faints for several minutes/hours. Very unprofessional of him! 😤 maybe consider adding a "," so it can be "Alan, wake"..

- Even tho his wife is kind of pressuring him just like mostly everyone else for him to write, while Alan just wants a bit of a break of work and a romantic vacation. He still goes after her all alone against monsters.


Now let's calm down a bit and get to the good stuff 😌
- Alan Wake besides committing a lot of mistakes on his relationship, he recognizes it and does his best to fix them. While also looking out for his wife all alone in a dark forest full of monsters and coffee thermoses, all to find her and get her back. This is true love! something that would most likely never happen in this generation of brainless hook-ups. So props for Alan Wake for being a true man! 👏🏻👏🏻

- Even tho he did sleep and faint a lot of times throughout the game... he did still hold a big record of non sleeping days, so I gotta give him props 😒🙄

- The soundtrack for this game is absolutely amazing! Old Gods of Asgard 🖤🖤🖤

- The graphics are amazing for a 2010 game, the model for the characters are also very detailed.

- Besides the game not being "fully" horror, it sticks a fine line between horror + sillyness, which I kind of love. It manages to set the scary atmosphere well and the ambience sound is great as well!

- There is gonna be lots of time where you'll be unexpectedly scared by some monster jumping up on you or something, even when you are expecting it you may as well be scared because Idk man, they're just high IQ beings. I really wasn't expecting they to be much of a challenge in the beggining.

But anyways, that is all, I definitely recommend Alan Wake!

A little janky in spots, but still holds up, even in 2026. 

+ Visually, it is a little dated, but looks good enough to get immersed in as you play.
+ Combat at first can feel a bit strange, but it grows on you. Never gets too complex, but does get hectic.
+ Acting was fairly solid the whole way through.

- No real progression and no reason to find the collectables other than additional context from story pages.
- Not much replayability outside challenges, a bit of story added via additional pages, and dev commentary. Not my thing, though it's not a long game, so maybe worth at least one more go if you're a collector.

* Fairly short. Took 13 hours to do a fairly complete run while doing hot-seat coop with my wife. We didn't mind, but if you're looking for a longer experience, this may not do it for you.

221396918.review:
I recently played and completed Alan Wake, and it’s one of those rare games that feels more like an interactive novel than just a typical action title. The storytelling is its strongest point—structured like episodes of a TV series, it keeps you hooked with suspense, mystery, and a constant sense of unease.

The atmosphere is incredible. Bright Falls feels alive, and the way light and darkness are used both visually and mechanically is brilliant. Combat is simple but satisfying—using light as a weapon against the Taken is a unique twist, though it can feel repetitive over time.

Where the game really shines is in its narrative depth. Inspired by writers like Stephen King, the story explores themes of creativity, fear, and reality vs imagination. The pacing is solid, though some sections drag slightly due to repeated enemy encounters.

The soundtrack and sound design deserve special mention—they elevate the emotional tone of the game and make key moments much more impactful.

Overall, Alan Wake is a must-play if you enjoy story-driven games with a dark, psychological edge. It may not have the most complex gameplay, but its narrative and atmosphere more than make up for it.

**Rating: 8.5/10**

220752697.review:
A great game and entry into the Remedy connected universe. The game has obviously aged since its released in 2012 but the story and content still hold strong. There is one chapter whose pacing feels a bit off (i.e. drawn out, felt like you're just running around the map) but it was a minor issue compared to the overall game. Gameplay mechanics are not overly complex - primarily focused on exploration of the map and surviving enemies. There are collectibles and item encounters, some of which are tied directly to the story (i.e. Night Springs TV episodes, manuscript pages). The game (with the two extra chapters included) wraps up the story nicely while also hinting at an eventual sequel (which is, frankly, a masterpiece).

While I certainly wouldn't NOT recommend this game, if you are someone who finds reading tedious during video games or is just looking to play a game where you can zone out and not be interrupted with another manuscript page to read, this is not the game for you. You may still find enjoyment in the game (and if so, rock on) but this will be a game best enjoyed when be able to commit to the experience.

I've seen several reviews elsewhere of people who struggled with the remastered version published by Epic, like game stability and crashing. I would recommend this version if you're not concerned with the new set of polish on the graphics and are seeking a stable port to the PC.

220611732.review:
I finally finished Alan Wake and honestly it felt more like experiencing a psychological thriller than just playing a game.

The atmosphere in Bright Falls is incredible. The forests at night, the constant feeling that something is wrong, and the way the story slowly reveals itself kept me hooked the entire time. I really liked the episode style structure too. It actually feels like you are playing through a TV series.

One thing that stood out to me was the manuscript pages. Finding pieces of a story that predict what is about to happen creates a really cool sense of tension. You know something bad is coming, but you still don’t know exactly when or how.

The combat is pretty simple, but the flashlight mechanic makes it interesting enough. First you burn away the darkness, then you can finally damage the enemies. It’s straightforward but fits the theme perfectly.

Overall this game is not about complex gameplay. It’s about atmosphere, storytelling and mystery. If you enjoy story driven games and psychological horror, this is definitely worth playing.

220608860.review:
[h3][b]The best tension is the kind that makes you look into the dark with respect[/b][/h3]

Alan Wake is an action thriller with a TV series vibe, where light is not just decoration but a real tool to survive and move forward. What stands out most is how the story and atmosphere hook me early, with a mystery that stays clear enough to follow while still keeping me curious. It definitely feels like a game from another era, but it has not aged badly. Still, I would not call it a must play today.

I could not play it back in 2012 when it released, and I am sure it would have hit harder back then. I enjoyed almost everything, except the damn collectibles, that early 2010s trend and its obsession with padding playtime. If you are a trophy hunter like me, you end up following a step by step guide, and that kills part of the magic. I recommend it to players who value story and atmosphere over pace, but not to completionists who get bored hunting everything down.

220602692.review:
The game is clunky. The combat suuuuuuuuuuuuuuucks and makes up almost the entirety of the game, when you're not watching cutscenes or walking through the woods doing nothing. The entire flashlight gimmick easily doubles the length of every fight. EVERY fight. There's virtually no combat variety: you're almost always ambushed by three dudes who are either already flanking you or immediately try to flank you. The dodge doesn't always work. You can't outrun the enemies to create space or just avoid combat encounters. You have next to zero stamina for the sprint. You don't have any kind of melee attack. There's no real variety in any of the fights since there are like three enemy types (including crows) and all of four gun types. I was so bored to tears by the combat by chapter six that I was begging the game to just STOP and let me finish the game. I had all the ammo I could ask for but the combat sections just kept appearing, one after the other, with the SAME ENCOUNTERS in every fight.

All of your weapons get taken away whenever the game feels like it, meaning anything fun (flare gun) or useful (flashbangs) that you have will just be taken away periodically.... along with whatever upgraded flashlight you've picked up.

You will get stuck on surfaces: walls, objects, small mounds of ground. At one point, I was trying to see if I could climb up a truck and fell between the truck and the car right next to it. I could not jump or run or attack. The game was, in effect, soft locked. In episode 5, there's a section where you have to run away from [spoiler]the Dark Presence[/spoiler] [i]while doing platforming[/i] over instant death drops. I missed this one jump multiple times because the game's pseudo physics engine was eating my jumps. WHO THOUGHT PUTTING TIMED PLATFORMING IN A SURVIVAL HORROR GAME WITH SEMI-MODELED PHYSICS WAS A GOOD IDEA?

The story sucks, too. The first three episodes don't tell you anything. You have no reason to think anything happening is actually real / trustworthy / believable. Then episode four comes along and spells out the whole story... which would be somewhat acceptable, except there are still two and a half chapters left to play! Everything after episode four is just ironing out minor details. Alan, as a character, is a piece of work with no real personality or backstory. In fact, it's implied by the story that Wake himself could be [spoiler]a creation of another character (Thomas Zane), as Zane wrote the Clicker into existence. How else would Zane know Wake existed as a child?[/spoiler] This is hinted at and never addressed again by the game. Actually, the end of the game as a whole is a massive damp squib. Virtually nothing is resolved. The game just ends with a cutscene that just stops the story for now.

One more thing about the writing: the entire plot is about finding Wake's wife, Alice... except I, as the player, have [i]zero[/i] reason to care about Alice since the only interactions I have with her are annoying (the photography thing on the ferry at the start of the game) and Wake being either angry at or dismissive of her. Wake's a jerk to her.

The writers making her afraid of the dark... as an adult... an adult who [i]voluntarily[/i] chose to go on vacation in the remote Pacific northwest... was moronic. WHY WOULD SOMEONE TERRIFIED OF THE DARK WANT TO GO TO A CABIN IN THE WOODS OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST? Is she STOOPID? I honestly thought her being afraid of the dark was a joke at first, considering the vacation location that SHE DECIDED ON... but no! Her intent was to get Alan into a psychiatric treatment program in Bright Falls, [b]as a surprise.[/b]

Let me repeat that: Ms. Afraid-of-the-dark's grand plan for this "vacation" was to either force her husband into working, knowing full well he's been suffering from writer's block for two full years... or pressure him into a psychiatric program to cure his writer's block... in an area of the country known for not being particularly well lit at night... without telling him any of this before they arrived. And where were they supposed to be staying? In a cabin in the woods. Just... genius writing, guys. Masterful. It's both contrived, and it makes your main female character look like a complete dolt.

I can only assume this game got its reputation as a "great" game from being a console exclusive to the Xbox 360. After all, they love repetitive combat encounters and cutscenes over there. Just look at how popular they made Halo...

220598792.review:
I started playing Alan Wake because I kept hearing amazing things about Alan Wake 2, and a lot of people recommended finishing the first game before jumping into the sequel. I played the original version (not the remaster, because there is no one on Steam), and honestly it still looks pretty decent even in 2026.

I’m not a huge fan of psychological thrillers or horror games, but the story really pulled me in. It’s rich, mysterious, and makes you curious about what will happen next. At times it honestly felt like a Stephen King story coming to life.

Unfortunately, the gameplay was the weakest part for me. The limited tools and mechanics made combat feel repetitive pretty quickly, and after a while it started to get a bit boring. Some of the scripted encounters were also frustrating in a bad way - it felt like the developers tried to create difficulty in a very artificial way. Because your options are so limited, you can’t really improvise or approach situations differently (though maybe that’s just my skill issue). A few episodes also felt longer than they needed to be, especially episode 3 and 5.

On the positive side, the music was great. Some songs, like "The Poet and the Muse", were fantastic and I even added them in my Spotify playlist.

Overall, I liked the game more than I disliked it. It’s definitely a classic and worth playing at least once - especially if you enjoy survival horror or stories with a strong Stephen King vibe.

220195855.review:
I picked this game up as a bit of a lark, as part of a sale bundle with Remedy's hit game 'Control'.  There is a side quest in Control and some other lore that references Alan Wake and implies they are in the same universe, so naturally I had to see for myself. I had a blast, despite this title being over a decade old and showing all of that age. 

The combat is terrible, the outdoor maps are basically linear, and the narrative is down a track. If you can get past all that, you barely notice because that rail car is such a wild ride you keep coming back for more. It isn't quite bad enough to suggest watching a playthrough, but I am probably not going back for achievements.

This is a horror game in a way similar to how Stephen King is a horror writer, with all the camp and comedy that entails. If you know your tropes you will enjoy seeing them revisited as something familiar to hang on to as you plunge into the icy darkness. The writing is phenomenal, layered but accessible in a way that would have worked as a book. I am grateful they chose a game as the storytelling medium though, as I may never have taken it off the bookshelf otherwise.

You should be aware there is a remake, but I think the original is a better choice for both price point and aesthetic.  The older models and shading have a retro appeal that seem to be absent in footage seen of the remastered version, and this version will run on whatever potato you own.

TLDR Get it.  Look for a sale or bundle perhaps, but don't forget about it because you need to hear about what happened in that sleepy mountain town back in the day.

219985411.review:
The flashlight and pistol combination are interesting and the gameplay is clever, you get no true crosshair but rather you aim by using the flashlight to make sure you're on target. When on target there's a shrinking circle that acts as a health bar/shield indicator to let you know when the enemy will be vulnerable. The flashlight also highlights arrows similarly to a blacklight effect that direct you towards secrets. The guns are varied enough to be fun and they give you variations of light sources beyond the flashlight so you have plenty of options for fighting to keep things interesting, even if it boils down to a simple 'break shield, then shoot until enemy is kill'. Not to spoil anything later, but there are other methods of killing the foes and it gets even more creative. Some of the later enemies are a bit boring to me in terms of design but not a deal breaker. Remedy has great game design, there was a fight where I realized they intentionally gave me objects in the environment to shield myself with so I kept the enemy on the other side as intended, as otherwise the fight would be a lot harder. As far as the guns in the game, there's not a ton but they're all balanced and useful, I found myself switching them often and ammo is limited so you're incentivized to do so.

This game is a breath of fresh air, it uses strong narrative elements as the author Alan Wake talks you through his visions and nightmares in the universe as you solve mysteries and fight. There's some light horror elements and mild jumpscares, but nothing is very frightening and it all serves well to the theme of the game. Not a huge fan of the Energizer™ branded batteries and Verizon wireless billboards/TV ads but outside of the obnoxious ad plugs this is a unique game worth the time investment. I found myself listening to the radio shows and watching the TV segments as they're actually good, Quantum Suicide was one of the first I saw and it was interesting. Most of the side content in games is usually boring, but not here. My only complaint is that the manuscript collectibles drove me nuts because they're interesting and I wanted to read the full story, but can't actually invest myself into the story as I'm going to inevitably miss some of the hidden pages and then lack the full context of what I'm reading.

Graphics and performance were good, only some light bugs (spinning chalkboard nearly killed me lmao). The game is a bit dated and while it generally looks pretty good, the character models, ground/rock textures, and the TV shows are the weakest points as the resolution/quality is quite low. I honestly wouldn't mind a remake of this and sadly Alan Wake 2 is locked behind Epic Games Store.

Unfortunately Alan Wake's story loses some steam towards the end and I kept thinking 'alright get on with it, just end it' in the final chapter that could have been cut in my opinion. Remedy has shown that they know how to competently make a single player experience around the supernatural and I'm happy to fight shadow demons in whatever they put out.

Still a great game, 8/10

219816051.review:
My overall experience for Alan Wake would be... 
[h1] If it wasn’t for the glaze on top of the donut, this would’ve been an uncut bland bagel [/h1]
.
I find every aspect of this title from mid to decent with rare Hype Moments here and there. Like the “puzzles” and level designs are ok. The driving is serviceable and functional but not ideal. The combat is the least interesting part of the game, but it’s certainly unique for its use of multiple forms of light( flashlight, flares, flare-guns, headlights, etc… ). The game has a lot of plot points where they take away your weapons+ ammunition, this is done to maintain suspense/tension, but also frustrating for someone who explored and scraped through the area
. 
I HATE that I cannot turn-off motion blur. And I DESPISE the multiple Walk-n-Talk sequences, the dialogue in these sections are intriguing but not enough to distract me from this hiccup( also killed my interest in replaying the game w/ commentary mode )
.
[h3] So what is the glaze of the donut? [/h3]
THAT would be [spoiler] the Anderson’s Farm section , the competent NPC( *cough SARAH BREAKER *cough ) that joins the fight with Alan [/spoiler] , and primarily… the Writing. The internal explanation of the item placements all around Bright Fall is obscure and interesting, the Night Spring episodes are intriguing, Alan’s manuscripts that foreshadows past,present,future events were cool, the light-vs-darkness theme being well implemented in lore+gameplay is appreciated, and the events coming full circle and convincingly explained was nice. Also, the whole cast is pretty good: Barry is charming, Pat is likeable, Sarah is admirable, Thomas + Anderson Brothers reliable, and Alan relatable.
Alan’s writer’s block and struggle to write is echoed when I was writing this post, and when he’s winded-out after sprinting was hilarious.
Also, love the episodic structure of the game and how every episode ends with a uniquely relevant song
.
In short, the same way the glaze is what separates donuts from bagels… the hype small details in-between is what elevated this overly average (5/10) game into “OK”(6.5/10) for me

the story was quite good and memorable with some pretty interesting characters and a quite unique concept, all of which is carried by a very good and strong atmosphere. Alan himself also isn't the most likable person at first however watching him slowly lose his grip on reality is interesting enough to carry the experience and by the end of it, after experiencing all that beautiful gameplay, you'll most likely share the same amount of insanity as him. really my only complaint is that the ending just feels kinda underwhelming.

the gameplay was alright however it has a ♥♥♥♥ ton of jank to the point where it kinda gets infuriating. the movement feels alright until you're either put in a section / situation where you need to run which is when you'll see that Alan has the lung capacity of an 85 year old grandfather that smoked for more than half his existence or in a section where you need to do parkour which is when you'll also not only see Alan's inability to both vault over ♥♥♥♥ and jump properly which made me restart the special episodes so many times for those ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ "no checkpoints" achievements to the point where I genuinely went outside and contemplated the life choices that led me to this moment. the combat is actually quite alright and quite fun although it's also pretty jank and doesn't really have much weapon variety outside of your standard revolver, a double barrel & pump shotgun, a flare gun which I have to add is the most fun & satisfying weapon in this entire game (especially when shooting it into a crowd of 5+ enemies) combined with throwables like flares & flashbangs which saved my ass more than the actual guns in this game. however one thing that won't save your ass and (will more than likely) actively work against you is the game's camera in a lot of scenarios. for example, I've had countless times where I was close to the edge of something, a group of crows decided to attack and I either straight up died or fell, got stuck and had to reload a checkpoint. there's also the 360 shots that the game decides to do whenever you pull out a flare while you're surrounded by a group of enemies which albeit looks cool the first couple of times but it genuinely gets annoying after a while to the point where I wish it had a toggle.

the graphics were genuinely still really good for a 2010 game and the performance was absolutely flawless. not only that but somehow I've had less crashes with this original release than with the 2021 Epic-exclusive remaster that crashed every single ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ time I alt-tabbed outside of the game then back into the game to the point where I just said "f*ck it" and bought this instead.

overall, this was the most 7/10 game that I've experienced in my entire life and even with its issues I still enjoyed my time with it somehow to the point where I'm still asking myself how and the experience of 100%'ing this absolute masterpiece of a game felt exactly like pushing a boulder up a mountain while actively getting rocks thrown at me.

219301188.review:
Alan Wake is a classic third-person action horror adventure developed by Remedy.
You play as Alan Wake, a best-selling writer trying to rescue his wife from a Dark Presence in a small American town and its surrounding areas. It has a decent story and characters, and for some reason is presented in an episodic format, even though the game wasn’t released episodically.
Combat is a bit unique, since enemies have a darkness shield that must be removed before they can take regular damage. The standard way to remove it is by using your flashlight, which requires batteries to operate. For weapons, you have a small selection, including a revolver, shotgun, and hunting rifle. Like batteries, ammo isn’t scarce, but it isn’t abundant either, requiring some resource management and scavenging.
However, I wasn’t a big fan of it. It’s different, but that doesn’t mean it’s better. Needing to remove the shield first is annoying and makes combat slower, although encounters are balanced around it. Coupled with janky controls, combat feels mediocre. And that jankiness extends to general movement and the driving sections.
The graphics are alright, considering its age, and it has a decent soundtrack. The PC version includes two DLC episodes that serve as an epilogue to the story. The main story lasts around a dozen hours, plus achievements that include collecting a ton of collectibles.
It’s a decent game, although I was a bit disappointed by it. The story is alright, but the gameplay felt mediocre. Maybe it has aged. But I can see why it’s a classic, and in the end, I can recommend it on sale.

219202316.review:
This game is build with movie tropes and clichés so recognizable, that I often smiled under my nose, instead of shaking in fear ;p

Twin Peaks inspirations? Sure, but also:
 
 Just in the first chapter you will see the famous and beloved "Axe through the door" scene from "The Shining", even using the same camera angle - and Alan verbally acknowledges that reference himself!
 "wow, that guy swings like Nicholson in The Shining" - uttered Mr Wake, mildly unphased after barely escaping death, just to let everybody know - he gets it XD 
  Guys, this is top tier comedy material, almost as much parody vibes as Deadly Premonition, without even trying XD

 This scene also implies that the main character exists in the same pop-culture realm as we do - he knows the same movies we know... he even calls the famous actor by name... 
And yet, he's somehow unable to spot so many other obvious references - all while being a writer for a living. 

 It's a shame that he doesn't have any smug one-liners about the possessed bulldozer, which is a pretty obvious reference to one of S.King novels. Maybe, as a writer, he doesn't want to give any credit to his competition? ;p 

 Black birds follow him everywhere, and yet, he never finds it to be at least... suspicious :) C'mon this black crows horror trope is sooo old and recognizable, that it's hard to see it as anything else than a tribute to the classics... And you don't have to be a pop-culture big-brain (let alone - writer) to spot it :) 

In general I found it a bit silly that a pop-culture creator/writer like Alan, is just swimming so deep in these horror references and tropes, and somehow doesn't recognize any of it, besides this one time with Nicolson and Shining ;p 

To put it bluntly - he's not the brightest... flashlight in town XD

...
 
 The main enemies are... umm... shadow zombies?

 Interestingly the first one we meet breaks the 4th wall (at least to some degree), and accuses Alan of being a "lousy writer - cheap, pretentious, zero artistic merit"... and that's why he deserves to die XD 
 Also, the shadow zombie signals its own self-awareness, by announcing itself as Wake's creation.
 
 For a while it felt like the game is interested in playing around with all these popular story-tropes a lot more, than in actually telling a story. Which was a major surprise, since I've heard so much praise for this story before. 

 Other zombies we met, aren't as talkative, but they aren't silent either. 
They shout one-liners thematically related to their line of work...
 Eg. the forest-guard-zombie will scream something about safety on the forest trips and health benefits of nordic walking. 
 As funny as it sounds I thought It was pretty creepy. It sounds like their human personalities resurface for some brief moments, but are distorted and twisted by the mysterious dark force.

 It reminded me about the Zombies in Stalker, when they cry and growl "My dear daughter... Dad will be back soon..." and you can hear the last remains of their consciousness waging a losing fight for a semblance of humanity... it's creepy, but also incredibly sad and tragic- all at the same time... 

  Yeah, Stalker did it much better, but I respect Alan Wake for genuinely trying :) 

...

 The combat gameplay is... pretty shallow, very gimmicky and gets old quickly. 

- Light the enemy with your flashlight and hold... hold... now shot him! 

- Ok, here comes a bigger one... light him up and hold... hold... hold... ahh battery depleted - swap it quickly - and hold... hold.. yeah finally you can blast him, but this one takes up to six bullets! 

 Reload, repeat.

 Instead of a crosshair you have to aim with your flashlight beam. Forget about limb detection or even headshots - Alan just aims in the general direction of the highlighted enemy, its very rudimentary.

 To be fair - sometimes the game will mix things up a little: 
- One section where you have to use flares, and your temporary friend will (very slowly) shoot enemies for you
- Sometimes instead of the flashlight you can use flashbangs. And I got to admit, getting some of these was always a huge relief, bc one well thrown flashbang can spare you the tedium of picking apart five shadow zombies one by one :) 
- In some sections, you have to fight against static objects that suddenly levitate and launch themselves at you. So you 'fight' against some rusted beams, crates, barrels etc. From the story-and-atmosphere perspective - that is just silly. From a gameplay perspective - feels cheap and annoying. 

I could nitpick and complain about this game for hours, but at the end of the day - this review is positive... and why is that? 
 
 I thought this game is very... likable :p

I loved exploring the environments, so many levels and scenes are imo so well put together its hard to not be impressed by it. It feels like a labor of love at times, like the dev team is so proud to show off the features and toys they build for this project, even if it doesn't fit well into the overall thriller/horror direction. OR DOES IT? XD

 For example there's a special level dedicated for car driving. Not a race, not a chase, not an action sequence - just a bit of random free-roaming that probably would've been cut-out - if this project wasn't a labor of love :) 
 You have a bit of open space to your disposal, and four different cars to try out. Nobody attacks you, there's no time constraint, you don't have to check any mandatory activities. The game just suddenly decides to take a little break and lets you mess around with car physics and even do a little bit of destruction. 
 And that was soo cool :) A little insight into the development process, a little show-off, like "look what we made here - cool huh?!" 
 Yeah, it was cool, and great breath of air between long combat sections. 

...

 The story is served to you in chapters, even with quick TV-like montages of 'what happened in previous episode'. I guess that's bc in the early 2000s many very successful TV shows were divided into shorter and easier to digest seasons, each one introducing its own story arc and theme, which is a basic standard now, but was a novelty at the time. 
 Imo that idea worked here very well, especially the quick edits "previously in..." introduce that special early 2000s media vibe, which I'm very nostalgic about. 
 
 Unfortunately, the dialogues are imo pretty predictable, too long, a bit too pretentious and formulaic, like some Turkish soap opera writer was hired to give his best here XD
Especially when Wake loudly "thinks" to himself and we're all blessed to hear his "bright" observations like:
 "something made a hole in the wall the size of a mammoth!" - Wake thought that loudly after something indeed broke a big hole in the wall near him. Wow. 
He will often make the most surface-level observations like that, and try hard to sound profound when he does it... So, just like in these Turkish soap operas, but even simpler. 
 
No offence to Turkish soap operas or their writers :D 
 
Imo, it just doesn't click together with the atmosphere the game tries to build with the gameplay bits. Often the game can get you really immersed into some really well build scene, it lets you explore a bit and sink into this universe without even noticing it happened... And then booom! Soap-opera-grade dialogue makes you think everybody in this universe is some  pretentious half-brain lol

Anyway, I still liked this game. It's a bit wacky and a bit all over the place, but has it's own charm, and lots of that special "honest effort" kind of energy that I just love to see in games :)

219023248.review:
Alan Wake felt like playing through a novel that’s actively rewriting itself around you. Set in the haunting calm of Bright Falls, it blends small-town Americana with creeping supernatural dread, turning forests, cabins, and empty roads into stages for something quietly wrong.

The combat’s light and gun loop is simple but distinctive, burn away the darkness with your flashlight, then strike while the enemy is vulnerable. It’s less about power and more about panic management, forcing you to hold your ground while the night closes in.

What truly defines the game, though, is its storytelling. Framed like a TV miniseries, complete with episodic structure and cliffhangers, it leans hard into psychological horror and writerly paranoia. Manuscript pages foretell events before they happen, creating the unsettling sense that fate is already written, and you’re just catching up.

Alan Wake isn’t the scariest horror game, nor the most mechanically complex, but its atmosphere is unmatched. It’s a slow, eerie descent into a story about creativity, obsession, and the terror of losing control of your own narrative. A cult classic that lingers like a half remembered nightmare long after the lights come back on.
