218839477.review:
A marriage of story and gameplay that is reminiscent of what Half-Life did for FPS but for TPS.


It's funny that Half-Life was all the rage when it came out and yet it never really interested me seeing it in the store (back when you went to physical stores to buy games).  I didn't play it for well over a decade after it came out, and while it didn't hit me as hard as it did people when it came out because I had played games that built on what it did, I was able to see why it was groundbreaking: it did a lot with the genre where you didn't just run around and shoot things like in DOOM or Wolfenstein but rather you had a mix of FPS, horror, storytelling, with a dash of puzzle-solving.  It was how it wasn't a run-and-gun but rather a think-and-gun that made it revolutionary for the genre.

Similarly, I think Alan Wake has that feel of making the gameplay, story, atmosphere, and characters come together in an amazing combination that sucks you into the world.  There are games that came before it that did that such as the aforementioned Half-Life as well as games like Final Fantasy, but this was a third-person shooter that took that similar approach where most of those games were run-and-gun and turned it into mystery-and-flashlight (you'll get the joke when you play).  It has psychological thriller, horror, action, and mystery elements, weaving them together nicely.


The Positives:
-It's definitely different.  You're not just running around shooting things and collecting power-ups and health kits.  You're exploring, thinking, learning, anticipating, and trying to stay safe.  I know there are ones that came before it like the Silent Hills and Resident Evils of the world, but this does have its own spin with it being more psychological horror than survival horror.

-I enjoyed going through the game.  The game felt well-paced for the most part, the story unfolded well, there were a lot of cute little easter eggs for folks who like the genre, and I kept progressing looking forward to the next part of the story.  I play a lot of games where grinding, puzzles, epic boss battles, and other things get in the way of the story and break it up, so what I liked about this was how the story didn't get blocked by too many of those kinds of things.  It unfolded continuously, which I really liked.

-They really did a good job with the world.  It really felt like a TV show where you knew all of the characters and got sucked into the game.  That's hard to do—you're usually an observer of other people's lives, but because of the psychological elements, you do feel like you're in the game unfolding in front of you, which I enjoyed.  It's not often where you get sucked in—many games are meant to be played for skill or progression, but this one was played for story and the experience.


The Negatives:
-No doubt that the combat was a little clunky and, in one part, I remember being a tad frustrated fighting a few enemies at the same time and not being able to see who was attacking from where, etc.  For that part, you kind of have to run around and spin to see what's going on without getting hit.  But it wasn't too bad.  I only had a couple of points where it became annoying where I wanted to get on with the story but had to get through it.  The enemy parts are very "staged" because the rest of the game involving narrow walkways and small areas for exploration won't give you the room for the combat mechanics, so it's kind of obvious when a battle is coming up.

-If you prefer action or a greater combination of gameplay and story, this leans more heavily on exploration and mood.  It's kind of like a stepping stone in the branching out of games from being games and instead being stories and experiences, this step being where the story trumped the combat...with latter steps after this being the genre known as the "walking simulator" where it's all story and environment and they dropped any pretense of there being traditional combat-oriented gameplay.

-It's not the best ever (or even of the time) in terms of graphics or originality of story if that's what you're looking for.  I don't consider this a terrible thing because the graphics do what they need to do and the similarity to other shows, movies, and books of the genre is what's necessary for the game and its easter eggs and such to work, but you may not like those elements, anyway.


Overall, I had fun with this.  I liked it so much that I played it's sequel as soon as it went on a decent sale (I have more games than I can play and so playing a sequel soon after the original is indicative of how much I liked the game and wanted to keep playing).  I think you might, too, if you like the genre.  There are going to be more original stories and better graphics out there, but if you're looking for something that scratches that mystery/thriller itch, this does it.  It's a lot of fun.

218526772.review:
First, the core mechanic is conceptually elegant. Light is your weapon. Darkness is the enemy. That sounds simple, but it creates a clean symbolic system. You’re not just shooting enemies; you’re burning away narrative corruption. Mechanically, you weaken enemies with a flashlight before you can even hurt them. It forces pacing. You can’t panic-fire your way out. You manage batteries like oxygen. That tension loop is tight.

Second, the structure mirrors psychological horror literature more than typical action games. The episodic format complete with recaps and needle drops leans into TV storytelling rhythms. It’s not accidental. The game is deliberately playing with the language of serialized mystery shows, closer to something like Twin Peaks than to a traditional shooter. Small-town eeriness, cryptic symbolism, mundane settings infected with something wrong.

Third, the meta-narrative layer. Alan is a writer trapped inside his own manuscript. The game isn’t subtle about it. Manuscript pages you collect foreshadow events before they happen. That’s clever because it weaponizes dramatic irony. You know something terrible is coming, but not when. That creates anticipatory dread instead of jump-scare dependency.

Now the uncomfortable truth: combat is not deep. It’s repetitive. Enemy variety is limited. If someone praises the game purely for gameplay depth, they’re lying to themselves. The strength is tone, cohesion, and thematic ambition. It understands what it wants to be.

It also benefits from restraint. Bright Falls feels grounded. The forest isn’t a gothic castle. It’s logging country. That contrast ordinary American Northwest versus creeping unreality anchors the horror. When reality bends, it matters more.

Finally, it respects mood over spectacle. It’s closer to a slow-burn novel than an adrenaline shooter. That means it will bore players who crave mechanical mastery. But if you value atmosphere, narrative integration, and symbolic design, it’s unusually focused.

The game isn’t good because it’s perfect. It’s good because it’s coherent. It knows its thesis: creative obsession can consume the creator. And it builds every mechanic, line of dialogue, and flashlight beam around that idea.

218261867.review:
Alan Wake is a very unique game with a simple yet clever premise that bears obvious resemblance with Control. The enemy design, clunky movement style, and mysterious hostile force interfering with reality all place it firmly in the same category as its spin off (and arguably in the same universe). While no all of my 10-12 hour playtime was counted, I did highly enjoy it with one caveat.

Alan Wake has truly beautiful visuals that hold up to this day. Though by now its attempt at "photorealism" is laughable compared to its sequel, its saved by a high contrast artstyle with deep colors and moody undertones. The art and story direction are perfectly in tune with its Washington state 
setting; the tall trees, small towns, and eccentric people are familiar to anyone who's visited America's arboreal Northwest or Northeast coast. What's more, the plot surrounding the (literally) creative powers of art is uniquely suited to the ad-hoc nature of videogames; no plot contrivance or on the nose reference feels out of place because it's meant to be almost a dream within a dream. 

However, it's clear Alan Wake was the beginning of a formula Remedy would later perfect; cutscene pacing, story beats, and even graphics were good for their time and undeniably creative, but its easy to see how they've since improved. Most aspects (like the graphics and game mechanics) hold up despite their Xbox 360-era lack of polish. On the other hand, its hard to imagine that even in its own day Alan Wake's combat could ever be described as "good."

Like Control, enemies can be tanky and combat becomes sluggish unless you come prepared. Unlike Control, however, ammo is limited and many re-supply points are disproportionate to the enemies you'll face ahead. In some areas, progression feels natural and intuitive and combat (or the ability to run away from combat) is clean. In others, I'd find myself scrounging for ammo in the complete opposite direction of my path in order to prepare for a particularly tedious slog. Can I call the combat innovative? Maybe. In-character? Definitely. The foundation of Control's well loved rhythm? Unquestionably. But good? No. Thankfully, with the help of the "-developermenu" launch command, free refills for all weapons are available at just a click of a button. Yet out of all the things I liked about the game (and there was a lot to like,) vanilla combat was NOT one of them.

218096001.review:
Alan ran through the woods, the darkness closing in on him. Alan could hear it calling him from the shadows of the trees. The warped voices of those the darkness possessed was an unwelcome, but familiar sound to Alan after the events of the previous nights. Reaching a lamppost, Alan stopped under the light, a haven. Doubled over, trying to catch his breath, Alan could see her, taunting him. Alan knew that he had to keep going; he had to save Alice. 

[h1] Background [/h1]

[b] Alan Wake [/b] was released in 2010, developed by Remedy Entertainment. Remedy was known for developing the critically acclaimed game, [b] Max Payne [/b], and its sequel, [b] Mac Payne 2: The Fall of Max Payne [/b]. After releasing the sequel, Remedy took some time to catch their breath and explored new concepts for different projects. Taking inspiration from iconic creatives, Stephen King and David Lynch, one of these projects was, what would later become, [b] Alan Wake [/b]. 

[b] Alan Wake [/b] would be released to rave reviews from critics. With heavy praise for the story, cast of characters, and gameplay. However, being released the same week as [b] Red Dead Redemption [/b] hurt the sales of the game. [b] Alan Wake [/b] had a slow start, with sales going up later through word-of-mouth. Despite this, the game would garner a cult following and would be followed up by spin-off, [b] Alan Wake’s American Nightmare [/b] in 2012, and sequel, [b] Alan Wake 2 [/b] in 2023. 

[h1] The Story [/h1] 

The story opens with Alan Wake and his wife, Alice, stepping off a ferry in the small town of Bright Falls, WA. Alan is a writer from New York City, hoping that this vacation will give him a break from his work and cure some of his writer’s block. But a change in plans for the couple’s lodging leads them to an island cabin in the middle of the lake. That night, Alan hears his wife scream and finds her missing from the cabin. Hearing a splash in the lake, Alan assumes the worst and dives in, looking for Alice.  

Alan then wakes up in his car a week later. With Alice nowhere to be found, Alan refuses to accept that she’s dead. Resolving to find her, Alan investigates the town of Bright Falls further. Some things, though, are turning out not to be as logical as he may have believed 

[h1] Gameplay [/h1] 

[b] Alan Wake [/b] is a slow-paced third-person shooter with elements of horror, comparable to the Resident Evil 2 remake. You control the titular protagonist Alan Wake, as he navigates through remote parts of the Pacific Northwest. The game is divided into six linear episodes. Most of these episodes will have a daytime section, where Alan will talk to the townsfolk and explore the area a bit. The bulk of the episode will take place during the nighttime section. During the night, Alan will be hunted by a Dark Presence that possesses various objects and townspeople to try and kill Alan. The people possessed by the Dark Presence are referred to as “Taken” by Alan. 

To defend himself, Alan is forced to rely on his flashlight and any weapons he can find through the night. The Taken are cloaked in shadow and normally invulnerable to any form of damage; Alan’s bullets will do nothing. This is where the flashlight comes into play. The flashlight is used to remove this shadow from the Taken. With the shadow gone, Alan can dispatch the enemies with no worries. Should an enemy get too close for comfort, Alan can dodge out of harm’s way to put some distance between him and the enemy.  

There are no health pick-ups in this game; Alan’s health regenerates slowly if he takes damage. That is, unless there is a lamppost nearby. Standing under these lights are safe havens for Alan. All enemies will disappear and Alan will regenerate all his health. They provide a much-needed rest between action encounters in the night and typically contain some supplies for Alan. 

The gameplay is certainly a product of the time. The first few action encounters will feel fun, but afterwards they can become draining. The player must constantly be aware of all enemy positions. They will not stop attacking if Alan focuses on one or can’t see them. The player must be ready to dodge at a moment’s notice. This isn’t bad, but it can be slightly frustrating getting hit by an attack that you couldn’t have possibly anticipated. 

There are a few moments in the game where Alan will have to hold a position against waves of Taken. These encounters were my favorite in the game. The game provides tons of supplies and allows the player to take full advantage of the environment to their advantage in killing the Taken. These are an absolute blast. 

[h1] Gamefeel [/h1] 

Aside from lacking areas in gameplay, every other facet of the game is incredible. The graphics are dated at this point; I played the original instead of the remaster. But they still look good for what they are. Living in the Pacific Northwest myself, I felt like I was on one of my hikes the entire time I was playing. 

The sound design in the game is more subdued, due to the game’s focus in the horror genre. But the ambiance still adds tension during gameplay. During quiet moments, there will always be a light breath in the background. When games like these give me a method to defend myself, I often lose all feelings of fear. But this breathing noise always kept me uneasy about the situation. I wasn’t sure when or if a Taken would show themselves. 

If you are interested in playing this game at all, I urge you to not reveal the spoiler block that follows this. [spoiler] The concert farm in Episode 4 is such an incredible moment in this game. Holding your position against waves of Taken while Old Gods of Asgard play and fireworks go off is spine-tingling. I loved the Ash Tray Maze in [b] Control [/b], and while the maze is better in my opinion, this is still a great moment. [/spoiler] 

[h1] Conclusion [/h1]

I really enjoyed my experience with this game. The story kept me very engaged during the experience. Every day at work, I was thinking about getting back home to play this game. I was itching to find out where the story was going to go and how Alan would make it through.  

I had some trouble with the gameplay, but I think that happened because I played Remedy’s [b] Control [/b] first. [b] Control [/b] gives much more freedom in its gameplay and feels incredible. Going to [b] Alan Wake [/b] made me feel more constrained with what I could do and made every combat encounter feel the same. Some episode can felt very draggy (i'm talking to you Chapter 3) and the pacing sometimes struggles to keep things engaging moment to moment. It had some horror elements too, though a few of the jumpscares feel a bit cheap rather than genuinely terrifying.

That said, what truly kept me going is how strong and intriguing the story is. The narrative constantly pulls you forward with its mystery, atmosphere, and psychological tension. The world feels surreal yet immersive, and uncovering what’s really happening to Alan makes the journey worth it. By the end, despite its repetitive gameplay and slow stretches, Alan Wake stands out to me as a memorable experience carried heavily by its storytelling, mood, and unique presentation.

Alan Wake is a game that really stands out for its story. It feels like playing through a psychological thriller that keeps the tension going and a mystery that slowly pulls you in. The writing is good, and the story keeps you wanting to see what happens next.
The atmosphere is great and really adds to the experience. The way light is used in gameplay fits the story really well, and the soundtrack and narration help make it memorable.
The controls can be a bit clunky tho. Jumping in particular can be frustrating because hit detection is not always accurate. It does not ruin the game, but it can break immersion occasionally.
After finishing the main story, make sure to play the special episodes (The Signal and The Writer). They add important context and help wrap up the ending.
TLDR; Even with some gameplay rough edges, Alan Wake is worth playing for its story and atmosphere. If you enjoy narrative-driven games with a unique feel, this one is easy to recommend.

218061603.review:
This game has a completion rate of 27.7% due to some odd design decisions, the biggest of which is the flashlight mechanic.

Let's fix that because this is truly a wonderful game, and holds up very well with a relatively modern control scheme:

You are the most dangerous entity in the dark, do not forget that, you can quite literally walk enemies down with the light.

Use your batteries often - in fact use everything you have often. This is a major hurdle for most people who play Alan Wake. You will get supplies at every checkpoint, and your batteries back.

Your flashlight charges without batteries, it just takes time.

Use your flashlight heavily in every fight.

Don't be afraid to spam the battery reload.

 Once you break the Taken's "shield" by focusing your light, un-focus your flashlight so you can get a few moments of recharge during a fight. Often times you can just run away from fights so long as a checkpoint is nearby, like a light pole or a lit building.

Save 4 flare-gun rounds for the final boss in chapter 6 - do not miss! Save a combination of flares, flashbangs, and flare-gun rounds (about 6 in total) for the final boss in The Signal DLC (The boss fight begins in your apartment). In the writer, during the tornado, jump on the spooled wire (large wood object). Also in the writer, don't try to skip through faster than necessary on the spinning wheel, you'll just die.

217474640.review:
Psychological thriller with strong narrative identity

Alan Wake is a story-driven action thriller that blends horror, mystery, and light-based combat. Set in the town of Bright Falls, the game focuses heavily on atmosphere and narrative rather than complex mechanics.

Pros:
Storytelling: The narrative is the core strength, structured like a TV series with episodic pacing.
Atmosphere: Lighting, sound design, and environmental storytelling create constant tension.
Unique Combat: Using light as a weapon adds identity and thematic consistency.
Writing and Voice Acting: Strong dialogue and narration enhance immersion.
Memorable Setting: Bright Falls feels distinctive and unsettling.

Cons:
Repetitive Combat: Enemy encounters can start to feel similar over time.
Pacing Issues: Some sections drag, especially during extended combat sequences.
Limited Enemy Variety: The focus is more on mood than mechanical diversity.
Aging Gameplay: Movement and gunplay feel dated by modern standards.

Verdict:
Do I recommend it? Yes, particularly for players who value story, mood, and psychological tension over action depth. Alan Wake is best experienced as a narrative journey rather than a traditional action game.

Rating: 8/10 ⭐

217414401.review:
I gotta say, Alan Wake is one of those rare games that pulled me out of my comfort zone. I’m all about fast-paced shooters like Counter-Strike and Battlefield but story driven games? not usually my thing. Yet somehow, Alan Wake had me glued to my screen from start to finish. I picked it up on sale for nothing . I think like 1.99$
The story is straight-up phenomenal. It’s dark, twisty, and atmospheric as hell, with that creepy Pacific Northwest vibe pulling you in deeper with every chapter. The writing, voice acting, and soundtrack? Chef’s kiss. It made me care about what happens next in a way no other narrative game has for someone like me.
Combat’s solid with the flashlight mechanics feeling fresh at first dodge, light ’em up, blast away—but yeah, it gets a bit repetitive after a while. I wished there were more enemy variety or bigger fights to keep things spicy, but it was still okay and never frustrating enough to quit.
Some sections of the game drag with repetition (driving, collecting stuff), but man, the story more than makes up for it. Every time I thought “eh, this again,” the story continued to keep me captivated and always wanting more.
8.5/10 recommend, especially on sale. If you’re on the fence about dipping into story games, start here. You won’t regret it!

217379483.review:
Even more than a decade later, the game looks and feels fantastic on modern hardware. The lighting, art direction, and environmental design create a haunting mood that few games manage to replicate. Played in high resolution and framerate, the visuals absolutely shine.

The narrative is the heart of the experience. Slow, unsettling, and expertly paced, it constantly pulls you forward with its episodic structure. It’s the kind of story that stays with you even after you shut the game down.

Sound design and music deserve special mention. With good headphones, the immersion is incredible and the world feels tense and alive at all times.

Combat can feel clunky and repetitive, but it serves its purpose and never overshadows what the game truly does best: atmosphere, story, and identity.

This is not just a good horror-thriller—it’s a reminder of how powerful focused, narrative-driven single-player games can be.

not a review of the game itself which i'm sure is just great. more of an informational piece of the game's performance on my particular system and some fixes that helped me.
first problem was the game just wouldn't go above ~40 fps. fix to this was downloading dxvk (x32) and putting it in the game's files. now the game ran like it should. 
second problem was audio had cracks and pops. fix was to set the audio format of my audio device to "16 bit, 32000 Hz (FM Radio Quality)".
my final problem, which appeared after the ferry, i have not found a fix for. when moving mouse my game frequently stutters and freezes and the game drops 10-15 frames (if not capped under 90). if capped at any framerate under 90, frames don't drop but game still freezes. common fix to this seems to be capping the game at 60 fps and disabling windows' fullscreen optimizations, however doing this did not fix my issues. [b]EDIT: found a fix. [/b] fixed by disabling motion blur with PCGW blur fix or by adding -noblur in the launch options.

216806240.review:
Truly might be one of the most overrated games I've played in a long time. A product of its time, for sure, and not one that has aged well. For many the atmosphere and story redeems its gameplay sins, but in a genre that's experienced several peaks since this game's 2010 release, you are better off looking elsewhere. It suffers from a phenomenon typical of quite a few games in the 2010-2015 era, in which you are treated to at times less than 60 seconds of interactive gameplay before sitting through a several minute long cutscene that looks to have been exported from an Alcatel flip phone. Do not kid yourself that this was a technological limitation, as this was the same year we saw Mass Effect 2, Halo Reach, and Fallout New Vegas.

Level design reeks like someone listed out the story beats, picked an intended runtime, and applied just the right amount of padding to make the math add up. That can be forgiven in extreme cases, but when that padding essentially consists of three enemy types and one combat mechanic, it is unforgivable.

The game simultaneously cannot decide if it wants to be a story driven narrative experience a la Telltale, Dontnod etc., or an immersive horror. In trying to do both, it does neither. Frustrating and unrewarding combat segments are interspersed with 360p compressed cutscenes for dialogue with all the grace of a Netflix original. Hiding within Alan Wake is an interesting character study and mystery story. This juice is not worth the squeeze.

Alan Wake deserves a remake and a revisit to the ideas in this game so that systems can be reworked and tuned to bring the best out of what could have been a phenomenal game that ages gracefully rather than a remaster locked into an inferior game launcher which does not fix any of the core issues of the game

216479439.review:
Remedy Studios really knows how to write stories that get under your skin. I mean, Alan Wake is not your typical horror game at all. It feels more like this psychological thing that just keeps twisting around in your head.

The main guy, Alan Wake, he comes off as pretty messed up from the start. The game makes you wonder all the time if what is happening is real or if it is just him losing it, mixing up paranoia with some made up stuff. That part pulls you in deep because you are not sure yourself. It seems like the line between what is actual and what is in his mind gets so blurry.

There are these crazy turns in the plot that throw you off. Sometimes it is confusing on purpose, and I kept second guessing my own ideas about the whole thing. Like, is this happening for real or not. The game does not just hand you everything explained. It lets you figure it out as you go, which I think works well even if it leaves you hanging a bit.

The atmosphere stands out too, with all that foggy woods and weird lights that make everything feel off. The sounds and music add to the tension in a way that fits the mind games perfectly. And the fighting, using light against the dark, it is kind of fresh and ties right into the story theme.

I like how it is set up in episodes, keeps things moving without dragging. The characters stick in your memory, and that narration voice, it haunts you even after you stop playing. Some people might find the confusion too much, but for me it makes it stick.

Overall it is worth trying if you are into games where the story drives everything and messes with how you see things. It leaves you unsettled in a good way, I guess, though not everything ties up neat.

216409534.review:
I really love the atmosphere and story of this game, and the graphics still hold up well for a 2012 release. The combat can be tedious and the movement feels clunky, especially on keyboard and mouse, and it takes a while to get used to.

The main game has six chapters, each taking about two hours to finish, so the full story runs around 12–13 hours on normal difficulty.

There are three difficulty modes: easy, normal, and nightmare. Unless you’re a completionist, play on easy to get through the story faster. If you are a completionist, nightmare is probably the way to go. If I ever replay the game, I’ll definitely play it on easy to reduce the combat time.

Overall, I’d recommend this game mainly for the story and atmosphere, and playing it on easy mode.

216167171.review:
[h1] My name is Alan. I wrote the manuscript. It was difficult to put the pages together. [/h1]

[b] Alan Wake [/b] is, as the title of a YouTube video I saw in my suggested put it, the greatest 6/10 game ever made, though maybe I'd bump it up to a 7. It's got some unique concepts, and the game is pretty cool, but also damn it's B-movie stuff at times.

shoutout to steam preventing me from posting this review when I wrote it two days prior (as of writing) because I supposedly had to wait for the game to be released, even though it's been available on Steam for 13 years already

[h2] Gameplay [/h2]
Alan Wake is a third person shooter that flips some standard conventions on its head, mainly the lack of an actual crosshair. Instead, the light beam of your flashlight is used, which isn't that crazy as it sounds, you quickly become accustomed to using it and it becomes natural pretty soon.

The flashlight isn't just there for a crosshair though. Most enemies are coated in darkness, which is burnt away as you keep your flashlight on them. However, this takes 3-5 business days normally, so you can "boost" to speed it up at the cost of draining your flashlights battery, which replenishes either over time or immediately via loading a battery. Once the shield is out, you can actually damage enemies with your guns (there is no melee, if you have no ammo then ggs), which usually consist of a revolver, a flare gun and two handed weapon (of which there are four), alongside throwable flares and flashbangs. Each have different purposes, with flares repelling enemies while flashbangs are immediate damage, so there's a bit of strategising as to what to use at a given situation, but it's not that involved, since the gameplay isn't that deep or anything. Your main defensive ability is a dodge manuever, but be prepared for five bazillion cinematic slow mo shots for every time you successfully dodge.

At certain moments you drive cars but there's not much to really talk about here, it's standard fare car driving stuff, you can run over enemies and ♥♥♥♥.

A minor note, the game allows you to switch camera perspective from right to left by pressing a button. I always played it with Alan on the left side of the screen, as right side third person is just pretty cursed ngl. Also the sensitivity was ♥♥♥♥♥♥ and way too fast, so I had to lower it a bit.

[h2] Story [/h2]
As the titular writer, you go to some town in Washington with your wife, your wife gets snatched by t h e h o r r o r s and ♥♥♥♥ happens afterwards in which you end up blasting about half the towns population (but they're possessed so it's completely ok).

The game is split into six episodes (plus two "Specials", which are additional ones), and as Wikipedia put it, is presented like a TV program, with recap sections at the start of each episode that play immediately after you beat one summarising the episode you just beat moments ago. Truly a game ahead of its time.

[h2] Graphics [/h2]
[i] Played on a laptop with a GTX 1060, i7 something, 8GB of RAM and a HDD [/i] 

Alan Wake isn't gonna blow your mind visually, but it still looks pretty good of its time, though part of that may be boosted by nostalgia of seeing Alans model in old Gmod videos way before I knew this game existed. I will say that I do like the expansive backdrops, with them being fully modelled rather than just basic images.

For performance, the game ran smoothly, however, it defaulted to low, so I had to manually max out the settings.

[h2] Verdict [/h2]
I hereby declare [b] Alan Wake [/b] as 

[h3] REALLY GOOD IF KINDA STUPID [/h3]

Fighting the forces of darkness on a music stage while some damn good music is blasting is peak 2010 gaming

216120245.review:
[h1] Rinne Reviews: Alan Wake [/h1]

I know Remedy Entertainment from my wonderful time with Control, one of their other games. I had high hopes to see where Remedy would go here and I ended up being mostly content with my time with Alan Wake, but at the same time also disappointed. 

One of the strongest aspects of this game is the story. It wants you to see the game as some sort of TV series and it does such a good job with it! The storytelling's very compelling and on top of Alan Wake as a character who is really interesting to explore and really get to know more about and the art direction that I thought was pretty nice!  The game's unique with this aspect and it shines. 

The one thing I cannot stand about this game is the gameplay. I found it to be incredibly one note and gets boring very fast. The combat feels fine at the start, but in my personal experience I got bored of it by chapter 3. It introduces mechanics that are somewhat interesting like clearing the shroud before you take enemies down, but that's all it does. It doesn't explore much else and it doesn't introduce anything new later on. All you do is shine lights on the enemies ad shoot them, that's it. It really dampened my experience with it and by the end of the game, it felt awful to slog through. 

Alan Wake brings on an interesting story and dampens it with a combat system that is repetitive. If I can give this game a sort of neutral rating, I would. I really only recommend it if you're looking for a good psychological-thriller story and are willing to not get bored with the game.

216052094.review:
Alan Wake – Finished Playthrough Thoughts
Overall it’s a solid experience with an incredible atmosphere. The tone, the setting, and the constant sense of unease are easily the game’s strongest points.

That said, a few things held it back for me. Combat gets noticeably repetitive, especially in the later chapters, and the story starts to drag as it approaches the finale. The controls also feel clunky — the stamina is rough, the dodge is unreliable, and the game loves throwing enemies and assets at you while giving you very little mobility to deal with them.

Despite the frustrations, the world and mood kept me going. I’d put it at a 7/10. A memorable ride, even if it stumbles along the way.

215813418.review:
Alan Wake is what happens when a Stephen King novel gets trapped inside a flashlight and forced to do wind sprints through the woods of Washington. You play as the titular character who is a grumpy best selling author suffering from a massive case of writer block. He takes a vacation to the town of Bright Falls to clear his head but instead he loses his wife and finds pages of a horror novel he does not remember writing. Apparently his imagination is so powerful that it creates literal shadow monsters that hate bright lights and basic human personal space.

The combat is basically an aggressive form of home security. Since the enemies are protected by darkness you have to aim your flashlight at them until they sizzle like a piece of bacon before you can actually shoot them. I found the constant hunt for batteries to be the most stressful part because Alan has the athletic stamina of a middle aged man who has never seen the inside of a gym. He runs for five seconds and then starts gasping for air like he is trying to breathe through a straw while a lumberjack with an axe is teleporting behind him. It is a terrifying dance of shining a light and then blasting away with a revolver while praying you find a flare gun soon.

The atmosphere is thicker than a Pacific Northwest fog and twice as creepy. The game is structured like a television show complete with previously on segments that remind you exactly how much trouble you are in. Between the creepy radio broadcasts and the Twin Peaks style locals you really get the feeling that this town needs a serious intervention from an electrician. It is a classic piece of action horror that manages to make a simple flashlight feel more powerful than a rocket launcher.

9 out of 10. It is a brilliant and moody thriller that proves the pen is mightier than the sword but a high powered tactical flashlight is mightier than both. If you enjoy being chased through a forest by shadows while a man yells about his missing manuscript then this is your dream vacation.

Stock up on Energizer batteries and keep your back to the light and try not to let the possessed combine harvester ruin your evening.

215690083.review:
Alan Wake is my second Remedy game after Control, and while I didn't like it as much as that game, it was still a fun ride.  The main selling points of this game are the story and the combat.

The story and writing of this game are quite fun and well-realized. Without getting into spoilers, I like the way that this game frames the fear of the dark, and the dichotomy between safety and danger that comes from the light/dark theme. Alan isn't the most interesting character in the world, but I was invested in the story because of the way the writing gets you in his head and frames the struggles he's up against. 

The combat is quite fun, maybe not as much has something like Resident Evil, but I like the concept of having to burn the protective darkness off enemies with your flashlight. The game has a small arsenal where each weapon and especially throwable gets its chance to shine. Enemies aren't too tanky, but hit very hard, and have a tendency to swarm you fi you aren't careful.

Overall, great game, and I hope to play Alan Wake 2 someday.

215446711.review:
If you're expecting this game to be a psychological thriller with action elements, then you've been mislead. This game is a supernatural action adventure with linear story progression using bait and switch tactics to give the player a false sense of psychological narrative conventions. Without spoiling the game's story, the developers utilize suggestions of psychological distress from the main character, but abandons that narrative pretty quickly. They tried to build the game on ambiguity, but they constantly contradict themselves by explaining everything via narration from the main character. I get that he is a writer, narrating his story, but there were plenty of moments where the reader did not need exposition dumping. And honestly, if your story, game, or show needs someone to explain what is happening all the time... Then it probably is not a good narrative.

Alan Wake is a short narrative built into an unnecessarily long game with exposition dumping, gameplay padding, poor narrative scaffolding, blaring plot holes, and constantly closes interpretive space for the player. 215374867.review:
Awesome. Largely succeeds at doing something different and original, despite being a late 2000s cinematic action game, and more so than you'd expect for something so obviously a fan of its influences. The combat is something you get through as opposed to something you look forward to, but they do find a few ways to make it interesting. You play it for the largely excellent plotting, atmosphere, and sense of personality.

There are a few moments where the relatively weak gameplay gets in the way of the story's pacing, particularly in the last chapter. Alice is also boring and underdeveloped compared to the game's many memorable and charismatic characters, which ends up undermining the drama somewhat. These are nitpicks. Glad this game stuck around.

Remedy finances great original games by working directly with greedy, cynical publishers. The mark of this Faustian bargain in Alan Wake is the rampant product placement. You can avoid it by playing the remaster, otherwise not much of an upgrade on an already pretty game, at the cost of having to install the EGS. Pick your poison.

I am not a fan of Alan wake.

I tired I gave it an honest shot. Honest enough to get every single achievement and beat all of the extra story content, And i didn't enjoy it at all. I understand that this game is from the early twenty tens but thats not an excuse, games like borderlands 2, dishonered and a ton of other fantastic games, that both look and play a thousand times better came out in the twenty tens.

Not to mention that this game isnt the best when it comes to optimization, now this isnt the remaster the remaster is terrible. but this game does crash every now and again whilst running in full screen. It only happened once for me in my enter 25 hours but it still killed one of my very last achievement attempts no punctuation and it royaly pissed me off.

GAMEPLAY:
Its whatever, most if not everything you will be doing in alan wake 1 is shooting a gun at three different types of enemies with occasional fourth. One big guy that takes 3 shots on easy, one medium that takes 2, one little that takes 1. and the occasional chainsaw guy that gets used like 3 times in the entire game? and he gets shot 4 times before they die. other then shooting its a lot of running, and then some collectible grabbing. 214935218.review:
[h1][b]Alan Wake[/b][/h1]

[h3][b]Time Played:[/b][/h3] 

44 Hours

[h3][b]Last Played:[/b][/h3]

19th of September, 2022

[hr][/hr]

While my wait for this game was far less agonising than others, I first found out about it after its long development cycle was nearing its end, then summarily took over twice as long as said cycle was to finally play it. 

[h3][b]Yay:[/b][/h3]

+ Was thrilling from start to finish.

+ Story keeps you guessing, and in my case, unfolded in a completely-unexpected way.

+ Characters feel like actual people.

+ Gameplay uses light in very interesting ways.

+ The Taken are both creepy, and tragic to me.

+ Nightmare difficulty necessitated I play completely-differently to how I did on Normal, and further-emphasised how well-designed the game was.

+ Varied environments, and “Episodes”, in spite of them all being set in the same place, for the most part.

+ Graphics hold up very well to this day, to the point I preferred the original game's look to the “remaster” they did.

[h3][b]Meh:[/b][/h3]

~ DLC is not as good as the main game, though, I still remember it being favoured.

~ Night Springs episodes being live-action may have been done to add to the unsettling nature of the game, but I found them to slightly detract from the immersion...

[h3][b]Nay:[/b][/h3]

- Unremembered

[hr][/hr]

[h3][b]Verdict:[/b][/h3]

Simply put, this is one of the absolute best things I've ever played, and I am very glad I took as long as I did to play it, as I'm very certain numerous things would not have been picked up had I played it as a minor the year it came out.

I can only hope the sequel is as-good, if not better.

[h3][b]Placed in:[/b][/h3]

1: Fantastically-favoured.

214769640.review:
I know I'm late to the party, but I walked away from this game after beating it unsure of whether I loved it or hated it...I loved it for the first 6 hours at least. The gameplay concept is intriguing, with the use of light throughout the game leading to a gameplay loop I enjoyed, even though it gets old fast. I personally am in the minority (at least from what I've heard), in that I largely enjoy the gameplay, flawed as it is.

The story: Without any spoilers, you visit a town where everything is not as it seems, and you have to fight hordes of creatures to get your wife back. I personally felt that the story had some cool moments, and I hadn't predicted the ending at all, so that was great. The manuscript pages lead to some incredible "that makes so much sense now" moments if you take the time to read them. There were also many twists that could be predicted, but several caught me off guard and kept me guessing, and the only loose ends left felt very intentional, with no major plot holes of note, at least that I noticed. However, I feel this level of story needs to be backed by very strong gameplay, and as much as I enjoyed it at times...

The gameplay: ...it just fell flat a large part of the time. There is a pretty small arsenal, with only flares, flash bangs (basically lamer flare gun to use in case of emergency), a flare gun, one pistol, one rifle, and 2 shotguns. The light mechanic feels great, and the flare gun may be one of the most satisfying weapons I've ever used in a game, but the enemies hardly vary, you either fight small humanoids, or big humanoids, or birds. That's it. It's true, there are small humanoids that run really fast, and big ones with chainsaws, but those make the game slightly more challenging while still managing to feel repetitive. Compared to other games by Remedy like Control, or even Max Payne, these enemies feel very bland, with the only thing that seems to change throughout the game being how many you fight. There are also poltergeists, which are random inanimate objects that fly at you, but those are just dissolved with light and a lot of patience.

The atmosphere: As much as I dig on this game, I have to say, the atmosphere is incredible. There were several times I just stopped and looked around, Bright Falls really is gorgeous in this game. Although I'm not a huge fan of the light mechanic requiring every level to be swamped in darkness for enemies to exist, the dark forests feel very much like real forests, the nature and vegetation is beautiful, even at night, and because most of the game takes place in the dark, the juxtaposition when the sun comes out makes the world feel that much more beautiful. I frequently found myself exploring my surroundings, not for collectables, but just to see it, and that speaks volumes for the quality of the environments.

Sound Design: I'm no expert at sound, and don't claim to be one, the guns felt punchy, the ambience immersive, and the voice acting solid...that's all I can really say on this.

Conclusion: Buy this game, you may hate it, or you may love it, but I think it's a story worth experiencing, and a novel idea worth trying out. If you hate the gameplay, the story may still be enough for you to continue playing anyways. For the low price of $1.49 on sale, I think it's absolutely worth trying out. (Also, play the special DLC's, they're so wacky and different but I loved them more than the base game honestly). Buy it on sale, play it for 2 hours, and then decide if you want to continue or refund (although I don't see a universe where this game isn't worth $1.49)

214477796.review:
Alan Wake has a very strong and interesting concept. The light vs. darkness mechanic makes the game stand out from other action-adventure titles. However, the flashlight runs out quite quickly, which can be frustrating at times. Still, the story helps balance this issue — it’s not extremely deep, but it’s engaging and keeps you curious.

The gameplay is solid overall. Combat feels good, and the light-based mechanics are satisfying. That said, the checkpoint system is very outdated. Like many older games, having to replay long sections after dying can be annoying, especially during difficult moments.

There are vehicle driving sequences throughout the story, which are a nice change of pace. The fact that different vehicle models are used is a small but noticeable plus and adds to the experience.

After a while, the axe-wielding enemies become quite annoying. When combined with the poor checkpoint system and long cutscenes, these sections can feel unnecessarily frustrating.

Despite its flaws, Alan Wake 1 is definitely worth recommending. The story can be a bit confusing, but it’s entertaining and tells a unique, memorable narrative. The atmosphere and concept make it a game worth playing.

214331823.review:
Alan Wake is a fairly interesting horror movie, painfully stretched out into a 12 hour video game. The gameplay is novel at best, but mostly fluctuates between tedious and aggravating. It’s part third-person survival horror, part 7th generation action game, but it fails at both; the controls aren’t nearly tight enough for the action, the game undercuts the resource management of a survival game, and it’s not even very scary.

You’ll be fighting the same 3 or so enemy types with the same small arsenal for the entire game, with no real upgrades or progression to speak of, and the combat lacks any meaningful strategy; every enemy is killed the same way, and every enemy can easily wreck your ♥♥♥♥ in almost unavoidable fashion thanks to the clunky controls.

It’s a shame, because the story is pretty interesting, but it’s one of the rare examples of a game that suffers greatly from being a game and having gameplay. All the interesting story parts are separated by far too long sections of running through uninteresting areas at a snail’s pace, fighting waves of enemies over and over. The collectables don’t offer anything to make them worth going out of the way, either, so by the end of it, you’ll just be trying to rush through the encounters as fast as possible.

All this is made worse by the fact that, for some insane reason, the PC version renames ‘Normal’ to ‘Easy’ and ‘Hard’ to Normal. So, assuming you’re like me and want to play the game on the intended difficulty, you’ll end up playing what was originally Hard mode. Maybe the game is more tolerable on Easy, but I can’t imagine it would be any more fun.

214272849.review:
This game stands out as a psychological thriller where the atmosphere does most of the heavy lifting, and it does it exceptionally well. The game isn't outright scary, but it constantly maintains tension. The core mechanic of flashing darkness off enemies with a flashlight before you can hurt them is simple yet effective, especially when multiple enemies are closing in. Combined with limited batteries and ammo, the game frequently puts you into panic mode, which strengthens it's survival-horror identity.
Playing the original (non-remastered) version never felt like a drawback. The visuals didn't distract from  experience at all. The immersion and mood are strong enough that technical age fades into the background once you're invested.
The story is engaging, even when it's confusing. Much like following a complex TV series (Lost comes to mind), being ensure is part of the experience. The episodic structure, complete with recaps, reinforces the feeling that you're watching and playing a psychological TV show. It's clear the devs had a strong and deliberate vision for how the game should feel and unfold. 
That said, Alan himself doesn't always live up to his potential as a character. After seeing more expressive and unrestrained performances elsewhere in the add-ons, his portrayal here can feel somewhat restrained, as if the game never fully allows him to reach the heights he's capable of.
Gameplay-wise, item management works in the game's favor and reinforces the tension beautifully. Enemy encounters, how ever, are less impressive. Regular enemies do their job, but boss fights are a weak point. Unlike classic survival-horror bosses (such as those in early Resident Evil games, which feel like unstoppable forces of nature), Alan Wake's bosses are largely forgettable and lack presence or lasting impact.
Despite its flaws, this game remains a solid entry in the genre, especially at a time when games like this are increasingly rare. Its atmosphere, storytelling style, and identity leave a lasting impression, making it a game worth remembering and revisiting after the credits roll.

213847654.review:
It’s a linear third-person action shooter, heavy on horror-story, with scripted animations and cutscenes. The 1st playthrough has to be on Normal difficulty: it took me around 25h (main game only) to thoroughly explore every nook and cranny. If you stray from the paths, you’ll mostly find otherwise empty maps with only 100 pointless collectibles; scattered story pages play some part, but you stumble upon them naturally.
The story is not bad in essence, but the mix of humorous lines, bad-ass lines, horror elements, action scenes, romance, insanity portraying environments, and local characters’ mini-stories did nothing for my immersion—animations don’t help either, in both cutscenes and gameplay. Graphics are weird too: open areas look fine as a background but odd up-close; interiors were fine, caves looked really bad. Shadow auras and some explosions look great, while breaking wood looks ridiculous. Still, the remote mountain setting is cool, with deliberate viewpoints where you can see your destination in the distance, between long forest hikes and car drives.
Sound and voice acting is great. For some reason, a whole season of horror TV-series’ mini-episodes plays on random TVs, and songs can be listened to during gameplay or after each chapter. There’s forced aim-assist and lots of utility, and no point hoarding ammo since your inventory constantly resets—combat is generally easy unless you don’t kite and get surrounded.
On a 2nd playthrough, Nightmare difficulty took me 12h, including the 2 DLC “episodes” (which don’t really further the story), and it felt much better balanced and paced. If you want to try it, stick to the paths and search online for instructions to hack Nightmare difficulty for 1st playthrough—looks complicated, but it’s worth it.

213764605.review:
Alan Wake – A Stylish Psychological Thriller with Rough Edges

Developer: Remedy Entertainment
Release Date: May 14, 2010
Platforms: Xbox 360, Xbox One, Xbox Series X/S, PC, PS4, PS5 (Remastered)


Overview

Alan Wake is a psychological action-thriller that blends survival horror with episodic storytelling, heavily inspired by Twin Peaks and Stephen King’s works. You play as Alan Wake, a troubled writer suffering from writer’s block, who finds himself trapped in the eerie town of Bright Falls as reality begins to unravel around him. What starts as a search for his missing wife slowly turns into a descent into madness, darkness, and a story that seems to be writing itself.
Playing Alan Wake today, it’s clear why the game is remembered fondly for its atmosphere and narrative ambition. However, it’s also impossible to ignore how dated some of its gameplay systems feel. While the story and presentation still shine, the moment-to-moment gameplay often struggles to keep pace with the narrative’s brilliance.


What Makes It Good?

✅ Exceptional Atmosphere and Storytelling
The biggest strength of Alan Wake is its atmosphere. Bright Falls feels unsettling from the moment you arrive—quiet forests, flickering lights, and a constant sense that something is watching you. The episodic structure, complete with “Previously on Alan Wake” recaps, makes the story feel like a TV series, pulling you deeper into its mystery.
The narrative is layered, surreal, and intentionally ambiguous. Themes of creativity, mental illness, and loss are woven into the plot, making it more than just a standard horror story. It’s a slow burn, but one that sticks with you long after it ends.

✅ Memorable Sound Design and Music
Sound is absolutely crucial to Alan Wake’s identity. The ambient noises, distant whispers, and sudden musical cues create constant tension. The licensed music choices are particularly strong, often playing at the end of episodes to reinforce emotional beats.
Voice acting is solid overall, especially Alan himself, who convincingly portrays a man slowly losing control. The narration-style inner monologues add depth to the storytelling and help maintain immersion.

✅ Unique Light-Based Combat Concept
The idea of using light as a weapon is genuinely clever. Burning away the darkness with a flashlight before dealing damage adds a layer of tension to every encounter. Managing batteries, flares, and environmental light sources keeps you alert and reinforces the horror elements.
When the system works, it feels thematic and satisfying, especially during early encounters.

✅ Strong World-Building and Lore
Remedy excels at creating a believable, strange world filled with unsettling details. Manuscripts that predict future events, recurring symbols, and cryptic characters all contribute to a rich lore that rewards attentive players. The game invites interpretation and discussion, which is part of why it remains so memorable.


What Could Be Better?

❌ Repetitive and Shallow Combat
Despite the clever concept, combat becomes repetitive very quickly. Most encounters play out the same way: shine light, shoot, repeat. Enemy variety is limited, and fights lack the depth needed to stay engaging throughout the entire game.
By the halfway point, combat feels more like an obstacle between story moments rather than an engaging system in its own right.

❌ Dated Gameplay and Movement
Movement feels stiff, animations are awkward, and shooting lacks impact by modern standards. Alan doesn’t feel responsive, which can make combat encounters frustrating rather than tense. The controls clearly show their age, even in the remastered version.

❌ Pacing Issues
The story is compelling, but the pacing isn’t always consistent. Some chapters drag on longer than necessary, filled with repetitive combat sections that dilute the narrative tension. The game would have benefited from tighter level design and fewer filler encounters.

❌ Limited Enemy and Encounter Variety
Most enemies are variations of the same “Taken” type, which hurts long-term engagement. Boss encounters are rare and often underwhelming, relying more on spectacle than mechanical challenge.


DLC and Expanded Content

Alan Wake received two story expansions—The Signal and The Writer—which continue and expand the narrative after the main ending. These DLCs dive deeper into Alan’s fractured mind and provide more context to the story’s ambiguous conclusion. While the gameplay remains largely the same, the narrative additions make them worthwhile for players invested in the story and lore.


Final Verdict

⭐ 8/10 – A Narrative Triumph Held Back by Its Gameplay ⭐
Alan Wake is a game I respect more than I enjoy playing. Its atmosphere, story, and sound design are exceptional, and its narrative ambition still stands out today. However, the repetitive combat, dated mechanics, and pacing issues prevent it from reaching its full potential as a complete experience.
As a psychological thriller, Alan Wake succeeds brilliantly. As an action game, it often falls short. If you value story, mood, and world-building over refined gameplay, this is an unforgettable journey worth experiencing. Just be prepared to push through some rough edges to reach its best moments.

Alan Wake brings a great narrative, but comes up a bit short in the gameplay department. While I did enjoy the darkness mechanic and having to clear the shroud off enemies before you can harm them, that's it as far as depth. 

The combat here, which is the only aspect outside of the narrative really, is very one note. Throughout the story you'll do the same thing over and over again. Shine your flashlight on an enemy, shoot the enemy, repeat. It maybe would have felt a bit better if they'd actually given you better weapons later in the game so that it's not such a slog (ex. heavy enemies are basically double damage sponges with how long you have to shine a light on them before you can damage them), unfortunately though the things that are supposed to be "upgrades" really aren't. For example, the pump shotgun is somehow an inferior version of the double barrel shotgun. You get an 8 shell clip, but the gun itself is weaker so you just burn through ammo. Same goes for the flashlights. Later in the game you can find better flashlights like a heavy-duty flashlight and lantern, except... they're not better than the standard flashlight. The appeal of them, I guess, is supposed to be that they have a higher capacity so that you can boost them for longer without having to load batteries, except they all recharge at the same slow rate and batteries restore the same amount of charge regardless of flashlight. It would've been great if the bigger flashlights actually dissipated the darkness faster because they ultimately all feel the same where you'll be shoving batteries in them over and over during ambush fights.

Despite its shortcomings I would still recommend this one. It's one to play for the story not the gameplay though, so I wouldn't shy away from just running through it on easy difficulty if you give it a go; will make the experience less tedious.

213619949.review:
If you're into supernatural thrillers or anything by Stephen King, It’s basically a playable Twin Peaks episode mixed with a Stephen King novel and this is a must-play.

The atmosphere in Bright Falls is top-tier. There’s a constant sense of dread when you're navigating the woods at night, and the way the shadows close in on you still feels genuinely creepy. The "fight with light" mechanic is such a smart twist on standard shooters—having to manage your flashlight batteries while being hunted adds a layer of panic that most action games don't have.

My favorite part has to be the storytelling. Finding the manuscript pages that describe exactly what’s about to happen to you creates this awesome meta-tension. It honestly feels like playing through a high-budget TV series, complete with "Previously on..." recaps and a killer soundtrack (Old Gods of Asgard tracks are still stuck in my head).

The only real downside is that Alan runs like he’s never seen a treadmill in his life, so you'll be managing your stamina as much as your ammo. But the writing and the mystery are so good that you won't really care.

Highly recommend if you want a game that’s more about the "vibe" and psychological tension than just mindless shooting.

This game is an exercise in frustration.  The story is ok, but it's basically a copy cat Silent Hill 1/2.  If you happen to play the game you'll be able to tell all the similarities to SH1 and 2.  But story wasn't my main problem, it was the gameplay and combat, which are extremely poorly done and is the very essence of archaic gameplay design; even for its time.  I love Remedy's early games, Max Payne 1 and 2(3 was developed by Rockstar).  I also love Control, but I absolutely abhor this game Alan Wake.  I absolutely hate Alan's movement and gameplay.  

1.  Moves and runs like someone who is mentally ill, or has some type of handicap.  This idiot just doesn't move around in a clean precise manner.  In Uncharted games, they designed Nathan to move more naturally so when he's moving around he sort of glides through objects in the environment; kinda like how we would move around obstacle naturally avoiding collision.  This idiot Alan, wasn't designed like that.  He moves like he was designed by some indie dev who just graduated from college.  He also runs for like 5 seconds before running out of breath, making you wonder why even run at all?  The slowdown from exhaustion after running almost immediately retracts the benefit you reaped by running for 5 seconds; in other words, they cancel each other out.  You might as well just walk the whole.

2.  You run and move around as if you had glue stuck on your shoes, yet the enemies catch up to you in seconds.  They also have this Mario Kart-like rubber banding effect where if you try to run away from them they will almost spawn behind you; like those ai karts that rubber band in Mario Kart games.  Their attack animation has this propelling animation to them and the moment they initiate the attack animation they like spawn behind you.  I don't know how to exactly describe it, but if you are unfortunate enough to play this game you'll see what I'm talking about.

3.  There are only like 2 - 3 types of enemies and you will fight them from the beginning of the game until the very end.  The game dumps a group of enemies on you, and that's pretty much the whole template for the entire game.  The same 2-3 types of enemy combinations thrown at you over and over again.  There two gimmick enemies aside from the main ones but they're not even worth mentioning since you don't actually fight them; you do some dumb maneuvers to 'combat' them, but it's technically not even real combat.

Save yourself whatever it costs to buy this game and just watch someone else playing it on Youtube if you HAVE play it before playing Alan Wake 2.  SAVE YOUR hard earned money.

212993563.review:
If you ever come across someone who believes that video games aren't art, just show them this game. I genuinely enjoyed this game from start to finish. I mean sure, the first ten minutes of gameplay felt weird to play in 2025: besides the graphics, the camera angle required getting used to at first, there was one or the other graphical bug or system freeze. But keep in mind, this game was released 15 years ago. 

Other than that, Alan Wake is a game that just excels in it's story telling and gameplay. The combat, although it gets heavily criticized, is one of it's major selling points to me. It gets frustrating from time to time, you might have to repeat the same section several times (looking at you episode 2), but it is so clever and unique, that I just can't hate it. You have to strategize, set priorities, make use of whatever your environment offers. What's even more funny to me is that only after finishing the game, did I find out that I have been playing this on hard difficulty the whole time.

The story telling? Chef's kiss, kept me hooked the whole time. TV shows, radio stations, manuscript pages that tell you how the story will continue? So many ways of narration that just keep you engaged, there is no other game that made me go "this is such creative story telling" this many times while playing like this game did. The price is already good enough and it's a steal on sale, totally worth it. I will be playing the DLC episodes, American Nightmare and Control next. Now after finishing the main story, I wonder how much better Alan Wake 2 must be then, considering how impressed I was already by playing the first game.

Update: I can highly recommend playing the DLC episodes. The first one was alright, nothing special in my opinion but the second one was great! It requires a lot of platforming but the implementation of walking into a sort of hamster wheel that depicts story moments is quite incredible. American Nightmare certainly is a nightmare as a game but it has some fun combat.

212939674.review:
I was really not looking forward to this. You're telling me that one of the best survival horror games of the last decade is a sequel to a 2010 action-adventure game that shares very little in its gameplay to this 2024 masterpiece? Hmm...

But I was very very pleasantly surprised. The real selling point of this game is it's atmosphere. The use of lighting, physics objects, narration, soundscape and camera effects all combine to a campy-yet-spooky feeling that meshes insanely well with the story and vibe of the game. But [i]Alan Wake[/i] has so much more going for it; combat with an ingenious flare to it (pun intended), memorable lead and side characters and satisfying inspirations that the game is not afraid to address.

Big fan.

212899703.review:
I am a 46 year old single father, probably one of the oldest people playing this game. My son, who is 15 now, begged me to buy Alan Wake because he said it was different from anything I usually play. He said it was scary, smart, and full of atmosphere. I bought it for him, thinking it would be something we could talk about in the evenings.

For weeks, I watched him struggle through the forest at night, trying to manage his flashlight, his ammo, and his nerves. He would tense up every time the Taken appeared, swing the beam too fast, panic, and get overwhelmed. Sometimes he would pause the game and stare at the dark screen, frustrated and a little shaken. I felt useless. I did not know how to help him. I was just the dad sitting beside him while shadowy figures closed in.

One night after he went to bed, I picked up the controller. Bright Falls felt eerie and empty, the kind of quiet that sits heavy on your shoulders. I walked down the lonely roads, listened to the trees creak, and tried to steady my hands when the darkness rushed at me. I learned to hold the flashlight steady, to wait for the moment the shadows burned away, to stay calm even when I did not want to. Hours passed without me noticing. Somewhere between the abandoned cabins and the flickering streetlights, I understood why the game had gotten to him.

By dawn, I made it through the episode he had been stuck on for days.

When he woke up, I showed him. He did not say much. He just looked at me with a mix of surprise and something close to respect. Not as the old man who avoids horror games, but as someone who had walked through the same dark woods and come out the other side. We sat together, and for the first time in a long while, we were not only father and son. We were partners facing the same nightmare.

Thank you, Alan Wake, for giving us a bridge back to each other.

212842065.review:
Alan Wake is one of the best psychological thriller games ever made. Remedy delivers an atmosphere that pulls you in from the very first moment. The story feels like a playable mystery novel, full of tension, twists, and cinematic moments that keep you hooked till the end.

The combat is simple but unique — using light as a weapon adds real intensity. Every encounter feels like a fight for survival. The soundtrack is incredible, the voice acting is top-tier, and the world of Bright Falls is hauntingly beautiful.

Even today, the storytelling and pacing hold up amazingly well. The game isn’t just scary; it’s smart, emotional, and memorable. If you love narrative-driven games, horror vibes, or anything from Remedy, this is absolutely worth playing.

✔ Great story
✔ Amazing atmosphere
✔ Unique light-based combat
✔ Fantastic music and voice acting
✔ Still holds up years later

❌ Some combat can feel repetitive
❌ Occasional clunky animations (older game)

Overall: 9/10 — A must-play psychological thriller.

212789217.review:
Alan Wake thrives in both its setting and atmosphere, making it feel like you’re in a season of Twin Peaks or a book written by Stephen King. It’s dark and eerie, like a nightmare where you just can’t put your finger on what is wrong with everything going on around you. 

The game follows its titular character Alan Wake as he frantically searches for his missing wife in the Pacific Northwestern town of Bright Falls. At every step you take towards finding the truth, the nightmare around you also gradually unfolds more and more. 

Alan Wake is also damn fun to play from beginning to end, despite a few shortcomings with its gameplay and mechanics due to its age. Overall it’s an incredibly solid horror-thriller, and even 15 years after its initial release that still rings true.

212706748.review:
Atmosphere, the game. Might be the worst Remedy game though.

Extremely repetitive game loops (reach object, fight “unexpected” wave of enemies, repeat), horrible fighting (light then gun gets punishing fast when dealing with 2+ targets), sensitive controls in combination with platforming of all things, shoehorned vehicle driving mechanics and so on and so forth. It’s frustrating.

The story is nothing special — a good portion of episodes is about reaching objects A, B and C to get a minuscule piece of information that could’ve been said by NPCs giving directions to A, B and C.

What saves the game is the atmosphere. It’s about as Stephen King and Twin Peaks as it can get without becoming a parody. Also it’s good to know what happens in this game to better understand DLCs for a much stronger Remedy game (Control).

Can recommend for educational purposes but brace for the horrible gameplay.

211666368.review:
Quite conflicted on this one to be completely honest. I like the atmosphere here quite a bit (though I do have some complaints there as well), but the game is not just the atmosphere. Played and 100%ed it at launch on the 360, but finally returned here after years to start going through the Remedy games.

Initially, my early thoughts were that the combat was just a bit clunky - which it is. There's zero skill progression in this, meaning after about two hours of playing, you have realistically seen every combat encounter the game will throw at you, they just get more and more repetitive as the game goes on. By the end, I was finding very little fun in interacting with it, and was mostly just invested in the story.

That being said, I have complaints with the story as well, haha. I think the broad strokes are certainly there, and the concept worked enough for me as a metaphor to keep me engaged - but I felt like it went nowhere. The ending felt extremely unsatisfying to me - I think they must have been going for a Twilight Zone-esque tragic warning, but I did not feel like they set that up at all. But that's fine! There's two more DLC chapters to resolve the story, so maybe they did that intentionally! ... .... The DLC chapters do not resolve the story any more or less than the actual game does lol.

Idk, I'm very willing to stay invested because I'm interested in learning where the Remedy-verse takes off from here, but on it's own I think this felt quite clunky, repetitive, and outdated. I could pick any aspect of the game and list about ten different negative things about it I'd like to change. HOWEVER, there's enough unique design here (especially in the final DLC chapter, which I think was the most interesting part of the entire game), to warrant playing through this at least once.

(written April 2025)

211008622.review:
When a gamer such as you and me think about a game that had a big effect on us.
It can be a lot of games... 

For me Alan Wake was this point which I remember as if it was yesterday. 
I knew about the game coming out, I did not have hardware to run it, and so I watched theRadBrad on Youtube as he covered it from start to finish. 
And that hunger to experience that game by my self stuck somewhere within me. 
So later when I was able to take control and experience it for my self. It was perfect to the very last detail. 

I covered it on my own Youtube channel and this year I even managed to get all the achievements for it finally. 

I really miss these kinds of games which took a basic story of wife being abducted and flip it on it's head into this amazing concept of Light Vs Darkness as Alan tries to fight the horror he was tricked to create in order to bring back Alice and the power of the lake using set story to try and stop him.

That sort of creativity is hard to come by these days. And even if the sequel is on Epic along with the broken ''remaster'' which I do not recommend. 

This version will run well, and give you a chilling experience until this day and make you keep checking over your shoulder as you walk home at night, making sure the lady of the lake is not coming after you.

It's not pure horror, it's not horror survival but it finds this special place coming close to titles like Resident Evil. Where you know, your time will be well spend.

The soundtrack is the finally cherry on top of the cake... 
Replaying that sequence where you protect your self on a sound stage while there are waves of enemies coming at you, makes you feel as if you were almost able to experience a live performance. 

I really recommend this title as it's a special gem and they don't make them like this anymore ;D

210021959.review:
It's a rather simple shooter, with a lot of the focus being on the story, but I'd basically call this a light form of "Action-Horror" (Think Dead Space, Resident Evil 4/5/8, etc)

Gameplay involves running around a creepy forest while enemies come out of the trees to surround you, with the only way to damage them being shining a light on them long enough for them to become weakened by it. It's an incredibly simple concept, but the game's weapons and level design push it just far enough for it to remain enjoyable as the overarching story and mystery slowly unravel themselves in front of you. 

Alan himself is fantastically written, to the point that his constant monologuing never becomes annoying.

There's also plenty of replayability with an entire manuscript hidden throughout the game's various levels, a bonus campaign and various difficulty options.

209518416.review:
Really wanted to like this one, but it really wore out its welcome across 8 playable episodes (averaging at least an hour each in length) and especially when going for collectibles and achievements.

Artistically, the graphics are the main selling point. Outside of every facial animation being really uncanny, the game has a great atmosphere thanks to solid lighting, detailed environments, and solid music and sound. Though, the remastered Alan Wake model only looks somewhat like his actor. My favorite details are all the cheesy sci-fi TV shows you can find hidden around and the fully produced songs from fictional bands in the game's world. The overall supernatural thriller aesthetic is pretty solid.

Story is meh. It starts out great, with an excellent mystery and great pacing in both tone and mechanics, at least for the first episode. Most of the writing is surface level and edgy, presenting itself as something much deeper than it actually is. Voice acting is a mixed bag, especially with the Taken. The pacing is drawn out way longer than it needs to be. Nearly all apartment scenes could have just been cutscenes. Ending was very inconclusive and unsatisfying.

Combat is annoying. Controls are slow, and sometimes completely unresponsive because of how animations are prioritized. Even on easy, you can get stun-locked and have your health mowed down by 2-3 enemies and there's often nothing you can do. And they throw a TON of enemies at you in later episodes. Enemy encounters get SUPER repetitive. You've seen pretty much all the game has to offer 3 episodes in. It has an obnoxious tendency to surprise you with enemies leaping out from behind doors, corners, and (worst of all) just spawning them directly behind you and never having the camera focus on them. Every "boss" is pretty much the same fight every time. Cinematic dodge only works some of the time.

Collectibles are a pain in the ass. Good luck finding over 100 pages, 100 coffee thermoses, and about another 100 miscellaneous collectibles without a guide. You will NEVER get them all your first playthrough. Would be better if the episodes were shorter or there were at least more chapter/checkpoint selections.

Two additions that would at least somewhat save the game for me (besides cutting down on lengthy animations):
1) Have a visible stamina meter. I don't mind having just a quick burst of sprint to use in tight situations, but there are only subtle character staggering and labored breathing cues which are very hard to keep an eye out for amidst the hectic combat. Most of the time, I'm just guessing at how much stamina I have and the cooldown is very punishing to your speed if you use it all up. A vehicle health meter would be nice, too.
2) Have the HUD compass/tracker in the top left track enemies (and possibly collectibles/supplies) instead of just the main objective. That would help tremendously with both how often enemies flank you from behind and where some of the more tucked away pages/coffees would be.

5/10 Has a lot of style without substance, and flashy show without solid gameplay foundation. Whoever put a platforming section in Special 2 needs to be slapped in the face. Hard.
