209453979.review:
I played Control and its Alan Wake expansion before this game.
I really like the story of Control and was curious about the story of Alan Wake since these two games share the same world.
I decided to give it a go, and the result was not what I expected.

First of all, the story of Alan Wake is good. The atmosphere, characters, and metaphorical elements are all well done.

But I don’t understand why the developers placed those manuscripts in locations we would never find unless we watched gameplay videos online. Those manuscripts are supposed to be a very important part of the story. They let players feel that everything is written or that some supernatural force is controlling events. Even if we, the players, don’t actively search every corner for them, I don’t think it affects the gameplay at all. In fact, I partially gave up looking for manuscripts halfway through my playthrough. They don’t give any gameplay rewards—just empty Steam achievements.

Besides the manuscripts, there are other collectibles such as coffee thermoses, TVs, radios, can pyramids, hidden chests, etc. Most of them don’t give any gameplay rewards, just empty Steam achievements, except for hidden chests, which provide ammo. From my POV, these collectibles disturb the gameplay. It’s like I’m in a very dangerous situation or need to hurry to save someone, but then I stop to check a corner for a coffee thermos—which doesn’t even give me anything.

My suggestion, especially for completionists: don’t worry about the collectibles. Just play the game and let the story carry you through your first playthrough. Even if you try to collect everything on your first run, you won’t be able to. Some items are hidden in higher difficulty levels. This game is designed to make you play multiple times if you want 100% achievements.

At last, I hope to see Barry in Alan Wake 3.

209163312.review:
Alan wake is relatively simple, but it's still somewhat hard to explain. A lot of negative reviews complain about it being a walking simulator, and I can't really see that argument. Sure, there are some walking segments but there are usually monsters to spice up the journey. Stamina is really bad tho

Combat is fun and creates for an intense experience, especially if you stay up late in a dark room due to a lot of flashing lights. The camera angle takes a bit to get used to, but by the end of the game I didn't mind it. 

The puzzles were also pretty simple and I didn't have to look up any walkthroughs or tutorials the entire game. This is also pretty easy to run, so if you don't have the strongest PC it's a good pick. I also like the picking up animations and all that

My biggest complaint is the dodging game mechanic. A lot of times the game swarms you with enemies and you can maybe dodge a few, but being hit stuns you for a good second and then a lot of enemies only need that second to damage you. I assume there is a cooldown to it, but the game never tells you so I have no idea. 

8.45/10

208848052.review:
Alan Wake Review Unless you like walking simulators skip this one.

WOW...I just finished Alan Wake and oh boy…this game has to be one of the most over hyped walking simulators that barely qualifies as a game.  Sure you get a flash light and weapons to battle supposed demons and it gives you some nostalgia for a much better TV Show called The Twilight Zone.  The game is structured as if you’re watching the Alan Wake TV Show and there’s a TV Show inside it that’s called Night Springs which is an extremely lame attempt at this game’s take on The Twilight Zone.  And what it took me about 12 hours to get through the entire story start to finish and this includes letting all the songs at the end of each episode play in their entirety.  If you skip the songs well you’re looking at 11 hours top on normal difficulty.  And this is like watching a bad movie where once you’ve seen it you don’t want to go back and watch it again.  I have zero desire to sit through all these cut scenes again.  And you can’t make any choices in this game it is pretty much go down the story path the writers want you to go down and there is no way to deviate from the path in any way.  This is IMO NOT a video game but a movie where you the ‘supposed player’ guide the main character through every part of the story and it unfolds the same way every time because it has to.

Unless you like walking simulators skip this one.

208701977.review: “Alan Wake” is a captivating game, and I can’t believe it took me this long to finally play it. I love the game’s moody atmosphere and Pacific Northwest setting, though a slightly less jittery camera would have enhanced the tension-building. Aside from this minor flaw, the overall direction is quite impressive. The story is engaging, though I sometimes wished it didn’t take itself so seriously. The real drawback lies in the controls, and in particular the running mechanics. I lost count of how many times I wanted to send Alan to game character boot camp for a proper cardio regimen. On the visual side, the levels are absolutely gorgeous. I frequently found myself pausing to take in the views during outdoor sections, despite the game’s age. The episodes and chapters offer a good balance of length and difficulty, and the level design is generally enjoyable, though I could have done without the clunky platforming elements in the second DLC.

It remains a fascinating concept, but the experience is likely less impactful now than it was at launch.

208231598.review:
[h1] Garth Marenghi VS The Shadow People [/h1] 

Alan Wake is a fun, but not in the least bit frightening action thriller with a clever metafictional twist.

You play as Alan Wake, an obnoxious novelist with serious writer's block who unwittingly becomes embroiled in a cosmic struggle between light and dark during a trip to one of those strange cursed villages that seem to just exist all across the Pacific Northwest. Pursued by foul-tempered shadow people, he must find a way to save his wife and uncover the secret behind a strange manuscript which he does not remember writing which seemingly predicts the events of the story.

The plot of Alan Wake takes a while to get going. The game starts out quite frankly badly, hacky and lame and focused on an unlikable pompous git of a protagonist. However, once it picks up and the plot begins to come together, it is genuinely compelling and very enjoyable in the manner of an airport paperback or a mid-00s thriller. It's a real page-turner.

Gameplay is also a lot of fun, although the enemy variety is pretty poor. In terms of early 7th gen Resident Evil 4 knockoffs, it's very well-done. The game looks very pretty for the time, too, with gorgeous and expansive levels and areas. I was occasionally reminded of Half-Life 2 in the way the game has you naturally hike and drive across it's huge, but linear maps, fighting enemies and stocking up on resources. The game is also decently challenging, with a couple of tough encounters.

Apart from the lacklustre enemy designs, my biggest enduring trouble with the game is that it is simply not scary. It's occasionally a bit tense, but mostly it's a fun game. The slightly hacky atmosphere of the early chapters never quite goes away, it simply evolves to be more creative and weird.

I had a blast with the game, though I'd never replay it. If running through the woods as Garth Marenghi while fighting off monsters who sound like Gilbert Gottfried sounds like your cup of tea, you'll probably love it.

207761738.review:
TL;DR
v cool story, a little janky and a little dated but still fun to play.


I played this while working through my pile of shame and thoroughly enjoyed my time with it. With a strong narative focus and very clever writing and an episodic structure that split gameplay into 90-120 minute digestible chunks, I found myself happy to take a break, and then excited to come back and pick up where I left off.

The story follows the titular writer, Alan Wake, on vacation in a small town in the Pacific Northwest in the US. Some supernatural events occur, and alan finds himself in a car crash he has no memory of, a week later, being stalked by murderous dark shadows. Many, many Twin Peaks references ensue.

While in terms of gameplay, Alan Wake is very much "of its era", with all of the jank and rough edges that characterise titles released in the middle of the PS3 and Xbox 360 era. At the same time, however, Alan Wake is also a game that knows exactly what it wants to be, and the game mechanics that are in the game do not overstay their welcome nor do they wear thin throughout the approximate 12 hour runtime. The player is offered up to 5 weapons at a time, and a flashlight to ward off the darkness. the weapons include a revolver, one of three longarms (a rifle, double barreled shotgun or pump action shotgun), a flaregun, flares, and flashbangs. to damage enemies, the player must shine the light on them until their mantle of darkness is disintigrated, whereupon they can be damaged by bullets. flares and flashbangs act as a "get the ♥♥♥♥ off me" button, while the flaregun is the period equivalent of like a bazooka or a grenade launcher or something. 

These weapons, combined with a relatively small roster of enemy entities, may sound like a sparse offering, however the iteration of different styles of encounter and different setups for level design make for consistently interesting and thrilling encounters. This is also enhanced by the constant threat of running out of resources, such as spare batteries, flares and, of course, bullets. while I only ever ran out of bullets once during my playtime, the knowledge that every shot I missed might have been very very important (particularly with the longarms, like the rifle) made the stakes feel appropriate each time I pulled the trigger, much more so than many other action games of the same period where I found I never needed to think about ammo until the gun was out of bullets at which point you would simply swap to another gun.

Overall, I enjoyed my time with Alan Wake a lot, I'm planning on moving on to the spinoff "Alan Wake's American Nightmare" shortly and then to Alan Wake 2 if, inshallah, it ever comes to steam

207254168.review:
[h1]Alan Wake Review [/h1]

[b]Alan Wake has a very unique premise. It's about a writer, who finds himself trapped in the story of his own written manuscript. (without giving spoilers of anything else that follows).[/b]

From start to finish, the story-line is intriguing, fresh, and very well-written. The game has a very dark atmosphere, with gloomy and dull environments to match the theme. I would consider Alan Wake to be a nice blend of mystery, suspense, and thriller. Not so much horror, but it does have some horror elements to it.

The animation and voice acting can be a little "off" during some parts of the game, but the cut-scenes bring a very cinematic feel to the experience. Alan Wake almost feels like a mix between a movie, and a game, which is quite refreshing to experience.

My only small complaint with the game is the gun-play. The combat often feels a bit repetitive, and almost tedious at times. Especially since the combat hasn't been designed with the "easiest" of mechanics. But it's clear that the intention of Alan Wake is primarily driven to its story-line, characters, and environments, rather than the action and gun-play.

Alan Wake will only take you about 10 - 15 hours to complete. But If you are looking for a good story-driven game, with a mysterious and dark undertone to it, then this game will give you that experience!

Overall it's a recommendation from me!

207194570.review:
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ Still Gives Me Chills After All These Years

Coming back to Alan Wake after a decade feels like revisiting an old nightmare — one I never wanted to wake up from. The atmosphere, the tension, the storytelling… it’s all still unmatched. Even after so many years, the game holds up incredibly well — both visually and emotionally.

The lighting mechanics still feel genius, the soundtrack hits just as hard, and that eerie, foggy town still gives me goosebumps. You can feel the love and detail Remedy put into every frame.

It’s rare for a game to age this gracefully, but Alan Wake isn’t just a game — it’s a story that stays with you.
If you missed it back then, don’t make the same mistake twice.

206877723.review:
A true classic. I adore Remedy games - I have yet to find one I didn't connect with on some level. 
Max Payne on PC started my love of action/story driven video games when I was 8 and played it at a friend's house. 

From there it just grew and remember getting Alan Wake as my first Xbox 360 game (apart from the game bundle with the console) after watching a gameplay trailer for it. 

Back then it was quite scary for me and I always played it at night with all the lights out. Later I got a friend to help me out - we would switch between stages or when one of us died. I remember our stress levels sky rocketing on sections where we ran out of ammo or batteries... The day sections felt so comforting and needed after a long night run. 

When Alan Wake II was announced I searched for the original on Steam and immediately purchased it to refresh my memory. Just got done playing the main story, as well as the DLC which I never experienced on the Xbox 360. 

Even now it is worth every cent. 

The combat/gameplay is stressful in a good way, the environments even now look really good - the genral look of the game. The lighting this game implements is still to this day breaktaking. The atmosphere this game achieves with the visuals, score and voice acting is second to none, and the way the story is told (tv series/episodic but also through the writings found) is pure genius. The way spoilers are introduced to not spoil but induce panic and the way past events are described in full to give you a better understanding of the bigger picture is sublime. 

I would recommend this game even today, competing with modern games. 

An amazing horror/psychological thriller.

9/10

206701748.review:
A very flawed game, but I wish there were more like it. The episodic structure always makes for nice, clean start and stop sessions. It's story focused and the gameplay is simple yet engaging enough to serve it well. Its also refreshing to play a game that's so centered around its artistic experience it's nice to not be burdened with a level progression or upgrades for your character. Alan is just Alan the whole game (well, sort of) and you only get the resources you need as they come.

Now the gripes: it is a lot of running through a forest, get to checkpoint, fight enemies or don't, rinse and repeat. Not helped by combat being clunky at times, and outright poorly designed sections. The two special episodes you play after the main six have some great environments that shake-up the scenery, but the gameplay becomes some of the worst up to that point. I think the story is a bit hammy and leans a bit too much into comic relief for me, and by the end of it the twists get very hit or miss.

The difficulty balance is very off too. I shamelessly played on easy and yeah maybe I just suck but I can't imagine how frustrating this could be on harder difficulties. And a real pain that some manuscript pages are exclusive to nightmare difficulty.

I struggle to play horror games, and I guess this is generally more of a "horror adjacent", but it made for a just my level of spooky for the season. A 7/10 but incredible value for the $1 I spent on sale. Looking forward to playing Control and Alan Wake II as I can see how the team that made this could have made superior games from this as a baseline.

In [b]Alan Wake[/b] you play a successful author who takes a vacation with his wife due to burnout.
You'll realize pretty quickly that Bright Falls has some dark and seemingly otherworldly sides.
But is all of this actually real? Or is it just in your head?

Alan Wake plays like a well written thriller sprinkled with a touch of horror.
Although the game has a creepy atmosphere (and a few jump scares), I would rate the horror as light overall, since you have weapons to defend yourself and enemy attacks are always announced.
My biggest criticism is that the combat became a bit boring and repetitive in the second half, 
but the story is interesting enough to keep you hooked and 
the memorable moments make it worth it.
I'm looking forward to part 2

206146097.review:
The only other Remedy game I've played is Control and I was surprised to form a lot of similar sentiments about Alan Wake. There's a really impressive physics engine here. At one point there was an explosion and a plank landed on top of a box, and I found I could get on top of the box and walk to the end of the plank and it acted like a realistic lever as I approached the end of it, tipping as my weight acted on it. That's just... really impressive for an Xbox 360 game. You can see the seeds of the sophisticated game engine that would become Control years later. The shooting is solid but unfortunately there's not a lot driving the gameplay other than, "I have to get to the next location" which almost always involves running through the Twin Peaks forest and fighting the same few enemy types (which too frequently teleport silently behind you) with the same few weapon types. Because most of the game takes place in the forest, almost all the environments look the same and the game lacks visual variety. 

Also like Control, I hate the story. The main character is exceedingly unlikeable. He stands around verbally berating other characters like a self absorbed celebrity, prattling on about his muse, with patches on the elbows of his tweed jacket and his mouth constantly agape like his jaw is broken (that has to be a problem with his character model). Not the most fun personality to hang out with for hours on end. Any engaging aspect of the story, locations, or characters was ripped shamelessly and clumsily from Twin Peaks (David Lynch would have hated this game) or from the most stereotypical Steven King tropes. On the bright side, I did really like the Twilight Zone vignettes scattered around the game, and the voice acting is also really good. 

Overall I think there's enough solid gameplay here to recommend, and the game is on the shorter side so it doesn't overstay it's welcome. And Barry reminded me of my friend Paul so that was fun.

206140321.review: This was a game that really plays into the cliche horror trope. It references Twilight Zone a lot. I especially love the creative direction of the voice acting. There is this overly melodramatic tone throughout the whole game that pokes fun at the late 2000's TV show drama/horror cliches. The story itself is fairly interesting as well. I like the idea of a writer being stuck in his own story. In terms of combat, it is somewhat lack luster. I think that things would be more interesting if there were more weapon options. Alan Wake's American Nightmare is sort of a noncannon(?) sequel which does have a more expansive arsenal, but it requires manuscript pages (optional collectibles) in order to unlock them. However this is a review of this game. I think it's definitely worth playing. I got it on sale for like a dollar and it came with Alan Wake's American Nightmare. Can't wait to get the second game. 7.8/10

206062848.review:
If you like Stephen King and his stuff, you’ll probably enjoy this. The combat can be frustrating and by now can feel dated. But it’s also quite unique with the flashlight and gun combo. Then there’s some elements like the poltergeists that fly where the wind takes them and they either hit you, drain your HP, or they miss by a mile and you’re free to go. 
The game is relatively straight forward, there are no puzzles, no real challenges. Nightmare mode more or less just buffs the Taken and doesn’t do anything else. There are collectibles which you’ll get ¾ of them in your first playthrough as they are not that well-hidden. Achievements can add challenges you normally wouldn’t go for, like going through a section without firing a shot or completing a section under 30 minutes. 
Regarding replayability factor. Unless you’re going for said achievements there really isn’t any. Like I said nightmare mode just enhances the enemies. The story doesn’t change, apart from a few collectibles that you can only get in nightmare mode. There are DLC’s. Both come with the game. 206042831.review:
Alan Wake is one of those rare games that feel like a perfect blend of storytelling, atmosphere, and gameplay. From the moment you step into Bright Falls, you’re pulled into a world that feels alive, mysterious, and deeply unsettling. The writing and narration are absolutely top-tier — it’s like playing through a psychological thriller novel.

The lighting and shadow mechanics still hold up incredibly well even after all these years. Every encounter feels tense, and the flashlight combat system adds a unique twist that keeps you on edge. The soundtrack and ambient sound design elevate every moment, especially during the darker chapters.

If you enjoy story-driven experiences with a cinematic touch, Alan Wake is a must-play. It’s not just a game — it’s a haunting journey that stays with you long after the credits roll.

206019330.review:
Another great game from Remedy. It's been out for a while. I played Control (on Epic, so no review here) and that was fun. Very Lost Room vibes. Apparently they take place in the same "universe".

Anyway, the movement is a bit janky on Alan Wake. You can only run in very short bursts (Alan is clearly out of shape). It tells you pretty much everywhere that you need to go, look etc.

The point of light from your torch is where you go, and not the way the character is facing (which is slightly off to the side). Okay once you get the hang of it, but can be annoying at times.

The graphics are very dated, but it's still okay. It's very much a linear path that you follow, but there are items and weapons caches off to the side from time to time.

You also lose all your ammo and weapons at the end of each "episode".

Nice atmosphere and a lot of fun.

Edit: Just finished. One of the fun things is getting the Steam achievements and seeing how many other players have got the same ones. 28.6%  of players got the Gatekeeper achievement, but 28.4% got Lady of the Light. You get both of those just by playing the game, and they are one after the others. Which means that 0.2% of players gave up in the middle of one section of the game lol

205203532.review:
Alan Wake is a flawed beast that went underappreciated in its time.

Made more than 13+ years ago by Remedy for the XBOX360, this game was triple-A for its time.
One can clearly tell the game feels very aged. Despite that, it has many enjoyable aspects as a horror shooter.

This game has a subpar combat system but excels thanks to its suspenseful story and mystery.
On the surface it feels like you are running alone in the forest at night while fighting off shadows, birds and poltergeists, but it manages to keep the player on edge.
The story is told in an episodic fashion like a TV show. With recaps and even ending songs!
Because items do not carry over between chapters, players are encouraged to use their entire arsenal instead of hoarding like the cowardly greedy loot goblins that gamers are. lol

I admit, I completed "CONTROL (2019)" and its Alan Wake "AWE" DLC first, which were absolutely stellar and served as an entry point into this para-natural world of basically SCP Foundation and the Backrooms.
While I compared that game to works such as "X-Files" and "Fringe",
Alan Wake on the other hand is akin to stories of Stephen King or the "Twilight Zone".

Alan Wake received a "remastered" version that is only accessible on EpicStore, but honestly it is a much INFERIOR version. With worse animations and a lot of bugs and glitches. Better to play this original version on Steam!
It also contains the DLC episodes (except American Nightmare, thank fk that standalone is an absolute TRAVESTY.)


EDIT: Alan Wake 2 is such a big game and it is a crime that Remedy made it an EpicStore exclusive! Regardless, I dusted my old but trusted black flag and set sail again!!
It took me around 26+ hours but I finished Alan Wake 2 and all its DLC.
Truly beautiful game of the year! A triple-A masterpiece.
As any game, some might nitpick the combat or other aspects, but that still wouldn't take away from the tremendous size and quality of that game!
The only shameful mark on this game, beside the EpicStore exclusivity, is that it is categorized as a Level 3 DEIDetected "woke" threat...
In the 10+ years of making, many clips about Saga Anderson have been made, actual canon videos! This white Caucasian actress that we already got to know and accepted as Saga was replaced and blackwashed by a Black actress for no particular reason but to virtue signal...
(If they wanted to have Saga be black, they should have done so ages ago, not get us used to a different actress and then pull the rug from under our feet when releasing the sequel. Dishonest and cowardly.)
Also, I have never met a white or black person with a name like "Zhang Xiu Ying" because that is clearly Chinese-coded name. As is "SAGA", there ain't an Asian or black person in the world with that name. So that also doesn't help the whole actress swap.
Last time they did it in "The Last of Us 2", nobody liked playing as Abby. Why repeat this same mistake???
Despite that, the game has watched its mouth and focused on the story and quality, except in the final chapters where Remedy felt the need to have Saga give that one annoying remark "not letting a white man decide her life"... WTF?
Doesn't help that DEIDetected categorized it as a Level 3 woke threat. Remedy sold out to Sweet Baby Inc.. disgusting.
YET despite all that, the quality of the game is superb and stands on its own, despite the flaws and politics trying to drag it down.
A perfect sequel and conclusion, that just needs to be mentioned here in Alan Wake 1.


This first game always had its flaws but has become iconic.
A product of its time. Beautiful but flawed. Fun? Definitely.
Now that Alan Wake 2 exists, this game will get the attention it so desperately deserved.

205122384.review:
You play as Alan Wake, a writer battling a sinister dark force using light as a weapon, trying to recover his missing wife and unravel the mystery of his own story. This is a horror thriller.

✅ Very dark atmosphere
✅ Strong, twist-filled narrative
✅ Good mix of action and horror
✅ Well-written plot and characters that keep us in suspense until the very end
✅ Graphically, the game looks phenomenal; in my opinion, it didn't need a remaster. It's from 2012 and is still impressive by today's standards (only the grass and bushes look bad).
✅ DLC included in the price (two special episodes that continue the main storyline).
✅ Varied locations (forests, campsites, a town, a power plant).
✅ Alan Wake's voice reading pages from the manuscript.
✅ Very good idea regarding equipment. We have a flashlight that blinds our enemies with its light, and additional items such as flares, flashbangs, and rocket launchers that provide light.
✅ Good weapon selection. We only have a revolver and two shotguns. I can't imagine having an AK-47 or M4 in this game.
✅ Very enjoyable shooting mechanics.
✅ Variable weather and atmospheric effects add to the immersion.
✅ Car driving motif has also been added.
✅ Great sound design that changes the tone of the game, keeping us on the edge of our seats. It fits perfectly with the atmosphere of the game.
✅ Dream sequences and hallucinations add an extra layer of mystery and unease.
✅ The artist's struggle with his work is a recurring motif throughout the game.
✅ Good character controls and fluid movement.
✅ Good menu options, including FOV adjustments.
✅ The game is divided into 6 episodes, plus 2 additional ones with great music after each one.
✅ The episodic structure gives it the feel of a great TV show.
✅ For fans of collectibles, the game has plenty to offer (106 typewritten pages, 100 coffee thermoses, knocking over 12 pyramids of cans, finding 30 hidden chests, 11 radio programs, 14 television programs, 25 signs, 10 alarm clocks, 6 cardboard silhouettes, and 10 games from the Night Springs series). There's plenty to explore, though I'm not a fan of this kind of content, so it's a plus for those who enjoy it.
✅ The game’s length is perfect; it's neither too short nor unnecessarily stretched out with an artificial, boring storyline.
✅ Three difficulty levels to choose from: easy, normal, and nightmare.
✅ No noticeable bugs.
✅ Very good price for such a high-quality game.
✅ Frequent save points throughout the game

✅❌ The game is linear, so, as with every linear game, everyone has to decide for themselves whether they enjoy that or not.

❌ Very weak puzzles. For example, the generator can be activated by double-clicking at the right moment. They could have introduced more complex puzzles or controlled them differently.
❌ Alan Wake's stamina is too low. He can run about 20 meters before getting tired, which quickly becomes frustrating for the player. Sprinting depletes stamina far too quickly.
❌ Jumping animation looks terrible.
❌ Poor enemy variety.
❌ The flashlight battery drains too quickly. The game could have allowed for longer battery life at the expense of fewer batteries scattered throughout the story.
❌ I don’t understand why the developers don’t allow players to start on “Nightmare” difficulty during their first playthrough.

✅✅✅ Verdict: [strike] Very Bad [/strike] , [strike] Bad [/strike] , [strike] Decent [/strike] , [strike] Good [/strike] , [strike] Very good [/strike] , [b] Excellent [/b] , [strike] Masterpiece [/strike]


If you have any questions about my reviews, feel free to ask. I'm happy to answer them. All reviews are my own!! Best regards, King Viking Bezimienny, Polish Game Reviewer.

204806040.review:
[h3]My name is Larendell, and I'm a writer (of reviews). [/h3]

After playing Control last year and both Max Paynes this year, I decided to finally remedy the fact that I still hadn't played Alan Wake. Luckily I had mostly forgotten what happened in Control's AWE, though the little that I remembered gave me some false preconceptions about Alan Wake – though that didn't really negatively affect my experience in the end.

I did my first playthrough completely blind, which led to the biggest horror moment in the game: seeing the statistics screen exclaim that there is a total of 283 collectables to be found!! But nonetheless, even though I realised that I had already missed one, I took a deep breath and strode forth. A good chance to knock my neuroticism down a peg, eh? Although it lead to me looking through every single straw of grass with a magnifying glass, only to find 93,64 % of everything there was to find in the end, but more on that later.

The visual presentation and graphics were very good considering that we're dealing with a title from 2010. The atmosphere, characters and voice acting really ease you into the world and make getting immersed easy – especially when you're playing alone in a dark room with headphones on. The story is great (some would probably say excellent, even), but the way it's presented in somehow elevates it even more: the episodic format makes you feel like you're dealing with an interactive TV-series. Even the episodes of the fictional in-game TV-series are captivating and macabre to watch.

The gameplay on the other hand is a bit more divisive. Frankly, I was relieved after what some people I talked to had said about it beforehand. The controls can certainly occasionally be a tad clunky, but the gunplay works well and is pretty fun. In the long run some might find it slightly repetitive, as the number of different enemies and guns is quite limited. What for most players probably starts out as survival horror gameplay can already by the second playthrough turn into more of a walking simulator as you stagger enemies and just skedaddle by them. At certain points the enemy AI also just gives up and let's you murder it freely, but for the most part it worked pretty much as you would expect it to.

Driving cars is probably something they should have cut off when they dashed the open-world idea – but at least it's not as bad as in Borderlands 2, as you don't have to shoot at the same time. The way the camera snaps back to facing forward after a second every time you try to look around just feels frustrating and unnecessary.

I also feel like Alan Wake gets a bit more tedious and not as exciting towards the end. The majority of the last episode feels so light on content and heavily combat-focused, which, again, is not the strongest point of the game. There's not even any new mechanics introduced to make it more interesting, like in the DLCs. After learning about the original plans for it to be an open-world game, I can see the vision that they were aiming for here. In its realised state though, it just doesn't feel super compelling compared to the preceding story-heavy parts.

In total, I played through Alan Wake somewhere around two and a half times – which was surprisingly fun. For most people I expect one playthrough to be enough, as an additional run on Nightmare difficulty only adds a minuscule bit of new perspective to the story in the form of fifteen new collectibes (for a total of 298). The replayability was greatly enhanced by being able to choose which episode to play, and most episodes also allow you to start mid-episode, usually from a point where you're stripped of your equipment (which happens quite frequently throughout the game).

Even though hunting the last [i]thermos[/i] when you're sitting at 99/100 and have no clue which episode you could have missed it in is quite the pain, the achievements were overall very doable and quite fun. Though I must admit that the DLCs' permadeath achievements that made you get through the whole episode in one sitting had my hands shaking a bit, but in the end I made it on my first and third attempt, respectively.

An honourable shout-out I feel I need to give goes out to [spoiler]Barry[/spoiler], who honestly might be the best sidekick in any game! Such a terrific feel-good comic relief character that sticks by you through thick and thin. Also, I've been listening to Poets of the Fall for over an hour while writing this, as their journey continues in step with Remedy's – and what a great collaboration!

All in all, Alan Wake is a superb atmospheric and spooky experience that works excellently as your first step into the Remedyverse. Personally, I can't wait for 2035 when Alan Wake II is finally released from Epic's grasp to Steam.

[h1]8,5 / 10[/h1]

204299881.review:
The Worst game for mordern hardware, I downloaded the and also checked the system requirements, they were pretty basic in front of my laptop so I thought it would easily be playable with high FPS, but man I was wrong.
When I first loaded up the game it said i don't have the "minimum requirements for the game" , I thought It might be just a bug , since  I have a RTX 2050 and i5-12450HX, so i thought it might be just an error, and when the first cutscene pulled up i was instantly getting above 500fps, so i thought it would be great playing this game but, when the first time i was able to play i wasn't even getting near 30fps, i was getting something around 16 to 26 fps even on lowest possible setting with native resolution. Then i searched the internet and found out, since it's an old game it runs on directx 9 with can't communicate properly with modern hardware and when the cpu cores are above 4core, the game doesn't run that optimised. So, the only solution was to Converts DirectX 9 calls to Vulkan, which modern GPUs handle much better, and set my cpu affinity to 4 cores. I just deleted the game and installed Rise of tomb raider (way better than this) . Since i didn't wanted to handle with all solution stuff. So if you are planning to play this game keep the above facts in mind.

204211436.review:
This game controls abysmally, keyboard and mouse or controller. I am not tense when I am floundering against controls like this. Confusing/actively painful controls works in REmake 1, Silent Hill 1/2/3, etc because they are still responsive and consistent. 

The combat and Alan are both slow as hell- while you are also expected to deal with zippy little bastards who can wombo combo you within a few seconds of starting a combat scene. It feels like pure RNG on if you will win with no skill at all or lose by no fault of your own. This is not tense and scary- this is frustrating. 

Combat means nothing past an annoyance because of frequent checkpoints and their AI sometimes deciding to kill you no matter what you do (or stand around doing almost nothing). The segments where large things are flung at you are also simply annoying, because there is a very good chance you will not see it coming in time to dodge out of the way (not that your dodge puts you low or far enough to actually dodge them even if you had the time). If these thrown objects even clip your leg after they have landed there goes at least 1/5 of your health, which they can do multiple times due to the funky physics.

My reaction to combat in a game should be "oh hell yeah" or "oh ♥♥♥♥-" not "ugh, this again?" no matter the genre.

Also, on rare occasions the game expects you to platform- which it does not at all have the controls to do competently, while in at least one section also having you platform on a janky physics object.

There is also a segment where you have to lure a large ball that is being thrown around by darkness, this is idiotic because of the aforementioned reasons- except this item, which is supposed to break down doors, then sits in the middle of the doorway and takes half your health if you breathe on it.

The vibes are on point, however. I also have to give it props as one of the few games worth playing set in my home state of Washington. The aesthetics have aged pretty well and the story is funky but it seems to be intentionally so (I haven't yet seen Twin Peaks, of note, so I don't know how much of an impact that would have on my understanding of the vibes).

Still, if you can't put up with the controls and combat by the time you beat episode 1 then it's best to just shelf this game and watch a playthrough or something to that effect.

Also, the game crashed every once in a while but I won't hold it against it because it's both 13 years old and I'm running it on linux.

204115988.review:
Alright... I'm going to give this game a little bit of a break for being older, but man this may be one of the least fun video games I've ever played. I wish I were exaggerating. This came recommended by some friends and content creators as a great story-focused game, and after playing I looked through the reviews. Am I missing something here? I feel like I'm the problem somehow given the widely positive reception this game has. That said, my thoughts are echoed in the negative reviews.

First off, the gameplay... man... this was just about the jankiest game of all time. Again, this isn't a game that you play for the combat, and this is an older title, but this was tough to continue. The entire premise is a bit ridiculous to begin with (flashlight/gun combat), and every step you take is janky, awkward, and overly time consuming. Most deaths are due to the awkward combat mechanics rather than some skill check. The actual skill you're developing in this game is to overcome the design itself rather than get good at the intended mechanics. I don't even know if this would have gotten a thumbs up from me for the times... games like FFX, Tales of Vesperia, Gears of War, Halo, etc all had combat miles better than this while having great stories. It's also not clear at all why these enemies are in the game. I understand it's fiction, but the whole thing is just odd.

Then the story... they really tried here. It is somewhat unique, though a miss for me. It's definitely the strongest part of the game, but still a negative. The first three episodes are a slog. Episode three alone almost made me quit. The plus side is after episode three, it gets way better. I actually enjoyed some of the final two episodes. The game is broken down like a mini TV series, and I thought that was pretty cool. It made me feel like I had a great stopping point each day.

Main issues I have here are the over narration. The "show, don't tell," philosophy is absolutely lost here. Every minute detail is narrated by Alan at some point; even the puzzles. Remember Dragon Age the Veilguard? Yea... pretty sure they copied this game. "I have to make my way up this mineshaft... maybe the machinery could help me!" (this is an actual quote during a puzzle when I was standing in front of the solution)

This one I will give grace on due to age, but the voice acting really caused immersion issues. During emotional points, everything was a bit flat. There are very rarely pauses in between the reading of sentences causing the feeling of rambling and lacking energy. It's tough to describe, but just not very high quality.

Regardless, I know a lot of people like this game. It would be hard to look up gameplay without spoilers, but if you can, definitely do that. I'ma give this one a 4/10.

204036648.review:
Haven't played the sequel (which I heard is pretty disastrously bad storywise in comparison) and haven't yet started the DLC, but I just finished a playthrough of Alan Wake on Normal difficulty and was very happy to have finally enjoyed this title. The game intrigued me back in ~2009 or 2010 when I read about it in game magazines but I never ended up getting a chance to buy it do to mixed reception and my impressions of being unsure it would fill the RE/Silent hill vibed games I was looking for. 

I have to say that even though Alan Wake isn't a particularly scary game, it genuinely doesn't have to be? What I found it to be was a slow burning, entertaining supernatural thriller with some horror elements peppered in. I enjoyed a lot of the collectibles, listening to the local radio broadcasts and watching episodes of an in universe horror anthology show that the main character had written episodes for. The atmosphere is top notch, really makes you feel like you're somewhere in the Pacific northwest, encountering quaint attractions, diners and ranger stations across the countryside.

I really enjoyed how the game went about its combat encounters and having to use light sources as weapons to weaken the enemies of darkness, there were a lot of good "a-ha" moments where you can use generators, flares, carlights, etc. to your advantage to trap/fry enemies in a satisfying way. Tossing flashbangs into Tormented mobs and seeing them incinerate is quite entertaining, and the gunplay is nothing amazing but totally serviceable, and makes sense considering the fact Alan is just an author and not some military veteran or special forces like Leon S. Kennedy in RE 4. 

I won't sit here and detail the narrative, there's plenty of people with better writing skills who could summarize it better for you, as even aspects of the narrative remain a mystery to me even having beaten the game (the ending finishes off with a sort of open-ended/cliffhanger finish that leaves you scratching your head a bit) but I loved the setup of Alan and his wife Alice taking a trip to a cabin to vacation and the events that unfold in rural Bright Falls continue to get stranger and stranger like an episode of Twilight Zone. I like that the game is somewhat restrained in its scares, very few jumpscares, a lot of good psychological buildup to crescendos, and the Poltergeist attack sections could be genuinely unnerving and scary, the game does a good job towing the line between making you feel badass in certain situations and somewhat helpless in other ones, keeping you on your toes and invested in what's going on.

The narrative can be a little hard to follow, pretty esoteric, but there's enough mystery tidbits being fed to you that it keeps you guessing and interested in where the story is going to go and to further explain the paranormal events that circle Bright Falls. The game does an excellent job of sucking you into the atmosphere and feeling like you're in the author's shoes as you're watching this twisted narrative develop, it definitely keeps you guessing and although you're not given definitive explanation as to everything going on at the conclusion, it was a very satisfying thing to play through, much like a really good season of a paranormal mystery thriller you'd see back in the 90s or 2000s.

I have a couple of gripes about the pacing/writing occasionally that aren't even really worth getting into, some segments seem to drag slightly too long, or finding myself being chased by enemies and not sure whether the game is expecting me to finish them off or charge ahead to get to a new Safe spot under a light. A few of the Poltergeist segments can be confusing/it was challenging to tell how to proceed, but I rarely had to reference a guide and for the most part could figure the game out as I messed around and played along. I highly recommend playing with a guide to at least find all the survival caches because those supplies will be a godsend in keeping the hoards away from you. 

If you like subtle horror in videogames and a plot similar to "what if the narratives that authors write in this town begin to transform it and come to life with horrible consequences?" sounds like an intriguing premise you'll probably enjoy this. It's a very nonstandard horror/thriller romp with unorthodox but satisfying combat, some reliance on "survival horror", an intriguing narrative and a creepy but at the same time cozy atmosphere that will suck you in, you'll probably have a blast with this. I haven't stuck with a game and enjoyed it so thoroughly in quite a long time, and it's been on sale for .99 cents! A complete and total steal!

My rating: 9/10

So, anyone looking for a nice solid game, with great story and an overall nice gameplay, I highly recommend it. There's no need to think twice.

PS: It's probably the only game where I played the whole DLCs. They are short and to the point. Very well done for DLCs, the way they are meant to me. You won't miss anything important, but they do give a little closure on the story and kind of set it up for Alan Wake 2.

PPS: Just noticed the tons of bonus content in the installation directory. It's worth a look.

The best description I can get to this game is that it is a mixed of things between Resident Evil and The Evil Within. The way of playing is technically the same as Resident Evil (the new ones, 3rd person) and the story is as weird as The Evil Within, it has a lot of twists, mid game you'll be asking yourself what the f is happening, but in the end it makes sense if you're paying attention to the game. For the price it is I think it's a grab, my only critique is that the enemy encounters can get a bit repetitive at the end, even though you get upgrades for your arsenal (not much, but some), there's some enemy variants but the way of fighting them are always the same.

Final rating: 7/10

203513275.review:
[h1] I dont understand how this is considered a Masterpiece [/h1]

I really dont like this game and I cant understand all the positive feedback for it. 
To get this out of the way: for me a good game has to be fun and/or entertaining for it to be considered good, but this game is mostly just boring. In general I love the type of game that is promised here: a nice story-heavy horror/thriller game that draws you into it's own world.

It also starts of pretty strong. Intresting cutscenes with a cool way of storytelling which really picks up your intrest and leaves you wanting more and a visually apealing scenery(probably really good for the time of release, but still fine to date imo). 
Then the gameplay loop is intresting concept where you first need to "blind" your enemys before shooting them with various normal weapons. 
I thought "hey thats an intresting idea, lets sea what fun stuff the devs will make out of this" and the awnser is: Fing nothing. You are playing at least for 8 hours or so against the same 3 enemys with the same 3 weapons(plus 2 useable 'nates) in almost the exact same scenario: You walk into an opening in a forest a wierd slow-motion cutscene foretells the ariaval of some bad boyos which you are supposed to fight. Again and again and again. The rest of the gameplay consists of narrow tube-like levels that will lead you to a generator or a switch where you have to press E on it to open up the way to the next tube-level and here and there you will be molested by some birds or random objects (like a steam-train) which u need to shine your torch at. Every now and then you will come across a sheet of paper which gives you some insight or some subtle foreshadowing of the story. This was really intresting at the beginning, but you find many pages and you are reading much of those so called manuscripts and they objectivly badly written(which is wierd conosidering its supposed to be written by a goated writer) so they will turn annoying sooner or later.

There are no real puzzles, not a single second where you have to use your brain, you are just straight up running(Alan is by the way slow as f and gets out of breath after running 4 meters) down the levels waiting for the next bit of story.
For me that was fine for about one third of the game. In that time i was really fired up by the story and endured the mid tier gameplay in hopes that shurely there will be some change ups but no. It certainly doesnt help either that most locations just look exact the same, its either forest or industrie-like-building in the forest(with the exception of one level that plays for one half or so in the city)

And in terms of horror: There is no horror
The Atmosphere rather feels like "Dear Esther" soaked in ink with a slide anoyance after every 20 steps.
The longer the game goes the more it feels like a cheap loot-shooter without all the intresting parts of a loot-shooter and the gamplay gets so boring that after a while that I didnt even fought those shadows anymore but I started to just run while spamming my 'nates, flares and light-torches everytime I saw a slightly more open space to just reach the next checkpoint asap. It didnt matter if you used many consumeables because in almost every checkpoint you would be drowned with new Items and at the end of every level, the game just took away all your stuff. 
With that Item management fell completely out the window which made the already stiff gamplay even less impactful.
And if you say now that Alan wake is a story game and i shouldnt be focus so much about the gamplay and fighting aspect, then why the hell do I have to endure this excremental bs for at least 8 hours straight?

And regarding the Story Its good for one half of the game, but after that it gets progressivly worse. It gets either boringly predictable or absurdly random and maybe that is meant as symbolism for Alan Wake's mental state since he is writing his own fate, but he is supposed to be a goatet writer so he at least should be able to write his story a bit solid?
And the Characters... OMFG even a DIN A4 has more depth. In comparison, Silent hill 1 from 1998 is able to bring the charakters closer to you heart in only like half a minute of really bad synchronized cutscenes.
In this game there are some big sequences where one of the characters is running after you for about an hour and I still couldn't care less about them.
Just try to get this situation in your Head: 
you are searching for your well beloved, kidnapped wife and there is the real threat of her beeing killed if you dont find her fast enough, you are on your legs night and day trying to find her then your friend suddenly suggests: "Hey lets go drink our brains out". AND YOU F.ING AGREE AND DRINK YOURSELF INTO COMA?! WTF?
This game has many more story aspects that imo are just bad but if I would go to write them all now I would be sitting here till Dawn.

By the way, if you are searching for a good Story-driven Horror game, go and play Until Dawn
but if you expect something from a Story-game or a game in general, do yourself a favor and 
dont buy this.

Müsst ihr Wissen

203405531.review:
Graphics and Atmosphere
The game looks very decent, especially after the remaster.  but the style and atmosphere are simply magnificent. The forest at night, the fog, the beams of your flashlight cutting through the branches and illuminating objects flying at you—it all looks very cool and feels cohesive. The atmosphere of dread and the unknown is conveyed 100% through the visuals.

Combat and Mechanics
The main gimmick of the combat is "shine and shoot." The enemies are covered in darkness, and to damage them, you must first weaken them with your flashlight (or another light source) and only then finish them off with your weapon. It sounds simple, but in practice, it's very intense and engaging. You are constantly monitoring your flashlight batteries, your ammo, and searching for safe havens of light.

About the Bosses (No Spoilers)
This is perhaps the main drawback for some players. The bosses here aren't drastically different from regular enemies; they are just bigger, have more health, and have a few more tricks up their sleeve. For the most part, the strategy against them is the same—you shine your light and shoot. If you're expecting unique gameplay design for each boss, like in some Souls-like games, you won't find it here. They function more like very powerful and dangerous enemies that add to the scale of the threat.

203344808.review:
The game centers on Alan Wake, a bestselling author caught in a supernatural mystery in the eerie town of Bright Falls. The Pacific Northwest setting, foggy forests, misty lakes, and a creepy small-town vibe, creates a chilling, immersive atmosphere. The story unfolds like a TV series, with episodic pacing, “previously on” recaps, and collectible manuscript pages that deepen the narrative without overwhelming. 

Gameplay is where Alan Wake shines, blending third-person action with a clever light-based mechanic. You weaken shadowy enemies using flashlights, flares, or environmental light before finishing them with firearms. This ties into the game’s light-versus-dark theme, making combat intense and strategic, with limited ammo and batteries adding tension. Exploration rewards you with collectibles and environmental storytelling, though the linear design keeps the focus on the narrative. Controls are tight, with dodging and cover adding a cinematic edge to fights. 

Visually, the game holds up for its era, with moody lighting and detailed environments amplifying the eerie tone. The sound design is stellar, moody music, chilling effects, and strong voice acting (especially Alan’s narration) pull you into the world. The episodic structure feels fresh and unique.Strengths include the gripping atmosphere, innovative light-based combat, and engaging, layered story with horror-genre nods. Weaknesses are minor: combat can feel repetitive in longer stretches, and the linear structure limits exploration. Enemy variety could’ve been more diverse.Alan Wake is a must-play for fans of atmospheric action and psychological thrillers. Its unique mechanics, haunting setting, and cinematic storytelling make it a standout on Steam. Highly recommended, despite occasional pacing hiccups.

I'm Alan Wake, a writer, and this isn’t an average story I’m here to tell. Something strange happened, and I still can’t wrap my head around it. It was another dream—almost—but it felt real. Too real.

It was a world dense and black, with no human life left. It felt like an altered future. I awoke in a forest with towering trees that blocked the sky above me. Around me were fallen and charred trunks, as if a forest fire had broken out—or even a battle. I walked along a path the trees had created and came upon the acrid corpse of a creature. It was so disfigured I couldn’t tell what it had been, but it was a monstrosity, like something just released into the world to devour everything.

If I had to guess, I’d say it was a dragon—one that had grown immensely, grotesquely in size. But with how massive it was, there’s no way it was purely organic. It had to have been summoned by something.

I had to find a way out of the dream I was trapped in, so I continued my journey until I heard a voice from within the forest. I froze in my tracks, scanning the treeline for the source—until I saw it: a pair of red eyes staring back at me.

“Come here, boy,” the voice called, clearer now.

I was frozen in fear, my eyes locked onto the thing calling for me. Stupidity took over, and I called back:

“Are you God?”

The eyes grew brighter as the trees around me toppled, revealing a thin-framed robot with a metal brain. The figure began to levitate into the sky as it spoke again.

“I am true perspective,” it said, the words rattling my body like a toy being shaken by a toddler.

The figure kept rising—until it suddenly stopped in midair. I watched in horror, waiting for the next words to emerge from its body.

The ground behind me split open, and I was thrown into the hole before a wooden door slammed shut above me, sealing me into the earth. The darkness was blinding.

Then I saw myself—from an outside perspective. I was inside a coffin.

I started to hyperventilate as the ground began to shake again. I feared my life was ending. Those same red eyes appeared inside the coffin, and I let out a scream of terror—

—and awoke in the real world, in my bed.

I don’t know what caused the dream. Maybe my job as an author has opened up my brain in ways even I didn’t expect. Whatever it was, though... it was over.

This is honestly one of those games that just make you say 'Wow!' It's a slightly older game now and the graphics do show, I imagine the remastered version improves on the graphical aspects, but the game ran very smoothly, the combat was like no other game I've played and I enjoyed almost every second of it, the only parts that I didn't adore were some of Episode 3 which dragged on a bit, but still means more Alan Wake! And also Episode 6 felt a bit tedious and repetitive.

Edit:

*SPOILERS*

I discovered after writing this that there were 2 DLC Episodes included, if you weren't aware you access them from the Episodes menu and select which one you want

The first is a bit rubbish in my opinion. I enjoyed Alan Wake because it had a great story and innovative combat, but this chapter just felt like 1 unnecessarily large combat section after the other with little story, I found myself just running to the next light source as it was so tedious!

However, the second was a pleasant surprise! After the first Bonus Episode I was dreading playing this, but it was great, just like another chapter of the regular game, balanced, spread out combat sections, with puzzles and cool environments, wow! 


But with highs like when you fight loads of enemies on the concert stage with the awesome music playing and some of the fun puzzles in the second bonus chapter, I don't think a DLC chapter and 30 minutes of still very good gameplay taints the experience!

Main Game: 10/10
DLC: 7/10 (The second DLC was my second favourite part of the game, but the first DLC brought it down 3 points!)
Overall: 9/10

203087783.review:
Alan Wake, developed by Remedy Entertainment and released on Steam in 2012 after its original Xbox 360 debut, is a unique blend of psychological thriller, action-adventure, and supernatural horror. From the very first moments, the game wraps players in an atmosphere of tension, mystery, and unease, fueled by a story-driven experience that emphasizes narrative immersion above all else. Taking cues from the works of Stephen King, David Lynch, and classic horror films, Alan Wake builds a compelling world where the line between fiction and reality is increasingly blurred, and where the protagonist’s own written words manifest into terrifying threats.

You play as Alan Wake, a bestselling crime novelist who travels to the remote town of Bright Falls with his wife, Alice, hoping to overcome a stubborn case of writer’s block. But things quickly spiral into the surreal when Alice disappears and Wake begins to find pages of a manuscript he doesn’t remember writing—pages that describe horrifying events that are beginning to come true. As Alan searches for answers, he faces shadowy enemies known as the Taken, manifestations of a dark presence corrupting the town. The narrative unfolds in a TV-style episodic format, complete with cliffhangers and “previously on” recaps, which gives the game a distinctive storytelling rhythm and a feeling of playing through a tightly structured psychological drama.

Gameplay is rooted in third-person action with a unique combat mechanic that sets it apart from other survival horror or shooter games. Enemies are shrouded in darkness and must be weakened with light before they become vulnerable to conventional weapons. This results in a strategic layer to every encounter—you’ll use flashlights, flares, and other light sources not only to expose enemies but also to create safe zones and control the pace of combat. It’s a clever metaphor that supports the game’s central themes: light versus darkness, clarity versus madness, and creation versus destruction. The tension created by limited resources, the need to manage batteries and ammunition, and the ever-looming darkness keeps players constantly on edge.

The environments in Alan Wake are gorgeously atmospheric. Bright Falls, though seemingly idyllic during the day, becomes an oppressive and unsettling place at night. Forests, cabins, abandoned buildings, and foggy roads all contribute to a pervasive sense of isolation and dread. The dynamic lighting system, one of the game’s technical achievements, plays a huge role in establishing mood and gameplay, with shadows that twist and flicker realistically and enemies that can appear from any darkened corner. The game’s sound design is equally impressive, with eerie ambient effects, sudden bursts of noise, and a haunting musical score that reinforces the game’s emotional beats and rising tension. Voice acting, particularly from the lead characters, is solid and helps bring the narrative to life.

What makes Alan Wake especially memorable is how tightly interwoven its gameplay and story are. The manuscript pages scattered throughout the world not only serve as collectibles but also as foreshadowing devices, giving players hints of what’s to come—or misleading them entirely. This narrative device builds suspense and engages players on a psychological level, prompting them to question whether Alan is losing his mind or trapped in a nightmare of his own making. The story moves between grounded reality and surreal horror seamlessly, often leaving players unsure of what’s real and what is fiction—a fitting experience for a game about a writer haunted by his own creations.

Despite all its strengths, Alan Wake is not without some shortcomings. The combat, while initially fresh and engaging, can become somewhat repetitive over the course of the game. Enemy variety is limited, and later encounters can feel more like endurance tests than meaningful confrontations. The pacing also suffers at times, particularly in the middle chapters, where the game occasionally leans too heavily on fetch quests or drawn-out traversal. Additionally, the controls can feel stiff, especially during platforming sections or sudden combat sequences, which can lead to frustrating moments when precision is needed. However, these are relatively minor issues in the broader context of what the game achieves as a narrative experience.

Alan Wake also benefits from strong replay value, especially for players interested in uncovering every piece of the story. The game offers multiple difficulty levels and collectible items that flesh out the lore and universe. Two DLC episodes—The Signal and The Writer—expand on the story and offer even more psychological depth and visual creativity. These additions deepen the sense of mystery and are almost essential for those who want the full experience.

In conclusion, Alan Wake is a standout narrative-driven game that expertly blends suspenseful storytelling with innovative gameplay mechanics. It’s a rare example of a game where the narrative tone, environmental design, and gameplay systems are all aligned to serve a cohesive artistic vision. While it may not hit every gameplay note perfectly, its atmosphere, originality, and commitment to storytelling make it a must-play for fans of psychological thrillers and supernatural mysteries. More than a decade after its release, Alan Wake remains a haunting, thought-provoking journey into the mind of a troubled writer—and into the darkness that lies just beyond the reach of the light.

Rating: 9/10

202993862.review:
After finishing the main story and the DLC (aka the Special Episodes) Alan Wake is a unique experience. At first, the story is confusing and hard to follow, but after a specific episode ([spoiler]for me its Episode 3[/spoiler]), it suddenly starts to make sense, and that’s when it gets really interesting. Remedy’s signature style shines throughout this game. The atmosphere is thick, the writing is clever, and the whole “thriller/horror meets action” vibe works really well.

[b]Gameplay[/b]
[b]-[/b] Gameplay-wise, it definitely has that Max Payne DNA all over it (Watching TV heck yeah Night Springs, Environmental physics, etc.), but with a twist, light as your main weapon. 
[b]-[/b] The combat system, burning away darkness with light before finishing enemies with conventional weapons—fits thematically with the story, even if it becomes repetitive after a while. Enemy variety is limited, and eventually the encounters feel like the same loop: flashlight, weaken, shoot, repeat.
[b]-[/b] Even though the combat can feel intense and cinematic, especially with the soundtrack backing it up, it does get repetitive. Enemies lack variety, so the loop often boils down to “flashlight, weaken the Taken, shoot, repeat.” 
[b]-[/b] The environments are atmospheric but start to feel copy-pasted after a while.
[b]-[/b] When the story picks up, it’s worth pushing through. The soundtrack is excellent, the atmosphere is moody, and the game delivers a unique blend of action and horror that you won’t find anywhere else.

[b]Story[/b]
[b]-[/b] Alan Wake is one of those games that doesn't really get you hooked right away. The opening episodes are confusing and almost like a fragment, you’re thrown into a surreal mystery where nothing makes much sense. But that’s exactly the point: the game wants you to feel lost, like Alan himself. Once you push through the game, the story finally begins to connect, and that’s when it becomes something special.
[b]-[/b] Remedy has always been great at blending storytelling with gameplay, and you can see the DNA of Max Payne 1 and 2 here. But instead of gritty crime noir, Alan Wake leans into psychological horror and supernatural thriller territory. 
[b]-[/b] The game shines is atmosphere and writing. The town of Bright Falls feels both alive and unsettling, even if you notice that certain assets and environments get reused. The narration from Alan, manuscript pages, and dialogues slowly peel back the layers of the mystery, rewarding you for sticking with it. By the midpoint, the surreal narrative and shifting reality start making sense, pulling you deeper into Alan’s struggle.

[b]Sound Design[/b]
- The songs at the end of each episode give it a TV-series vibe, I love it. *ehem* PREVIOUSLY on Alan Wake. That never gets old.
- The soundtrack during combat heightens the tension in ways few games manage. Gives me the chills every time.

[b]Graphics[/b]
- I’m not a big critic on graphics because I don't care very much, but the lighting design in Alan Wake is fantastic. It’s not about high detail, it’s about atmosphere, and the way light is used really sells the tension and mood of the story and Alan himself.

[b]Verdict[/b]
- If you enjoy story-driven games and don’t mind a bit of repetition in gameplay, Alan Wake is absolutely worth your time. Alan Wake is worth the ride, especially if you're a psychological horror and supernatural thriller fan. I could go on with the story, but I think its best you experience it for yourself.
- I'm a big fan of story-driven video games, so my rating is gonna be higher.

[b]Score[/b]
[h3]9/10[/h3]

- DSR does no longer work
- Direct aiming is broken
- Some graphical effects are missing
- Enemy death ragdolls are broken
- Epic Store exclusive

I admit textures and models look better though I bet some people might not like some of the characters changes. The biggest win are the cutscenes since the original had 720p ones with really low audio quality. 

But all this doesnt matter at all since the remastered version is literally unplayable with the constant graphic errors. It is beyond me how this got through testing and was released in such a way and worse, they NEVER fixed it. The game only got one update in January 2022 and thats all she wrote.

They could have easily made a remaster dlc for the original PC version but instead opted for Epic exclusivity and a developer who obviously has never touched PC hardware in his life. Great.

Alan Wake has quite possibly the most interesting approach to narrative immersion I have seen in a game, and that's including Remedy's later works. I think it has the perfect amount of meta-infused ambiguity to be compelling while staying focused and keeping to a more linear storytelling approach. 

I love the game's supernatural eeriness, the psychological examination, and how well its noir elements fit within the symbolism in the story. Its biggest influences might be Twin Peaks and The Twilight Zone, but it also passes as a psychological horror, with genuinely unsettling encounters and a tendency to have you greeting nightfall with some trepidation. Combat is suspenseful and the dodge system is smooth and satisfying, with slow-motion animations that never feel distracting. It's a fun and fascinating experience from start to finish.

[b]Hard to believe it’s this under-reviewed.[/b]
A twisty, tightly wound plot. Atmosphere so thick it pulls you straight into foggy forests and your favorite sleepy town—if it were written by Stephen King. First the fog rolls in, then the shadows whisper, and before long you’re swinging a flashlight at murderous IKEA furniture. The dodge-flash-shoot combat loop never gets old—there's something deeply satisfying about burning away the darkness before unloading your revolver. And when you catch your breath between encounters, KBF-FM drops absolute BANGERS that hit harder than they should—and it heavily complements the Bright Falls atmosphere. Peak gaming—aged like fine wine.

just picked Alan Wake 1 up for the first time. I got it because I adore Control for its thrilling narrative, complex environmental story telling, expansive imagination, intriguing mysteries, and a combat system that's buttery smooth. Control's Brightfalls AWE DLC peaked my interest in Alan Wake; it was tense, different, and interwoven with the Oceanview Motel, a point of personal fascination. It even solicited a few genuine jump scares out of me.

Alan Wake 1 is... none of these things. Sure, its an older game now so I wasn't expecting seamless gameplay but the off-centered, janky camera makes me motion sick, the combat is about as exciting as a 12 hour day in the DMV, the dialogue is barely survivable, the only variance in fighting is when the occasional heavy enemy appears which just equates to "shine the flashlight at them longer" and the whole experience feels like a last minute undergrad project.

The most frustrating thing is, I watched the Brightfalls web series, a prequel to Alan Wake, where I saw them create dramatic tension, inspired cinematography, and gripping plot hooks so I KNOW THEY'RE CAPABLE of producing a solid plot with immersive horror elements. But in this game, they just chose not to for some reason.

It's so astonishing how awful Alan Wake 1 is compared with the Brightfalls mini series, All the horror and tension are removed by the wacky rantings of the Taken:
"Oh boOOO LOoK oUT, ALaN WaKe! I'M cOming tO gEt yOu! BoOoOo!" 

It's just so damn cheesy.  And I really dislike that they decided to personify the darkness with an old funeral lady. It was scarier in Brightfalls when it was a faceless, supernatural epidemic. Idk what the game writer's were thinking, clearly they weren't....

The real nail in the coffin is Wake's monotone monologuing. He over-explains EVERYTHING, like we can't be trusted to understand a C- horror plot that was written at a 6th grade reading level.

The Brightfalls mini-series and the Alan Wake lore in Control were tons more interesting than AW1 itself... How did they ♥♥♥♥ it up this badly?

I guess you gotta cook a few lousy meals before you become an expert chef. And if Alan Wake helped Remedy evolve into the studio that made a masterpiece like Control, then it was absolutely worth it.

Also, regarding Wake vs The Kidnapper, who the f*ck throws a punch and sends themselves off a cliff? Such utter contrivance... -.- I'm glad Remedy grew up.

201873038.review:
📖 Story & Atmosphere
- Narrative: Alan discovers pages of a manuscript he doesn’t remember writing—each one predicting terrifying events. The story unfolds like a psychological thriller, with strong influences from Stephen King and David Lynch.
- Setting: Bright Falls is a hauntingly beautiful Pacific Northwest town, filled with foggy forests, abandoned cabins, and supernatural tension.
- Presentation: The game is structured like a TV series, complete with episodic cliffhangers and “previously on” segments.

🔦 Gameplay Mechanics
- Combat: Enemies are shrouded in darkness and must be weakened with light before they can be harmed. Your flashlight is as vital as your gun.
- Exploration: Players navigate linear environments, uncovering clues, manuscript pages, and collectibles.
- Pacing: Alternates between tense combat and quiet, atmospheric moments. Occasionally repetitive, but always moody.

🎨 Remastered Edition
- Visuals: Enhanced textures, lighting, and 4K support. Cutscenes have been improved, though some animations still feel dated.
- Performance: Smooth 60 FPS on modern consoles and PC. Minor bugs reported in some ports.
- Includes DLC: “The Signal” and “The Writer” expansions are bundled in, offering closure to the main story arc.

✅ Pros & Cons
| Pros | Cons | 
| Gripping, mysterious story | Combat can feel repetitive over time | 
| Unique light-based mechanics | Lip syncing and animations still show age | 
| Atmospheric world and sound design | Limited enemy variety | 
| Strong narrative pacing and voice acting | Some remaster bugs reported on PC | 



🧠 Final Verdict
Alan Wake is a masterclass in mood and mystery. Its blend of psychological horror, cinematic storytelling, and light-based combat creates a uniquely immersive experience. While its gameplay loop may show its age, the remastered edition makes it more accessible than ever. If you enjoy narrative-driven thrillers with a surreal edge, this is a must-play.

201497910.review:
Alan Wake Remastered revitalizes the original third-person shooter, blending action and psychological horror in a captivating narrative. Set in the eerie town of Bright Falls, reminiscent of Twin Peaks, the game follows author Alan Wake as he searches for his missing wife, armed with a "Flashlight of Death" and a small arsenal. The remaster enhances the moody environments with 4K graphics and a smooth 60 frames per second on the PlayStation 5, and includes all DLC, making it a worthwhile purchase for fans and newcomers alike.

The storytelling is exceptional, featuring surreal plots and memorable characters, but the gameplay is where the title falters. While the mechanics are easy to master, the repetitive enemies and lack of unique challenges can lead to boredom, especially by the third chapter. Critics have noted that much of the game takes place in spooky forests, which, despite some complaints, are among the best parts of the experience.

Characterization is a strong point, with Alan Wake portrayed as a flawed yet relatable protagonist. Supporting characters like Sheriff Sarah Breaker and the eccentric Odin and Thor Anderson add depth to the narrative, though Barry Wheeler, Alan's agent, can be grating.

Visually, the game is stunning, with beautifully crafted environments that enhance storytelling. The backgrounds effectively convey the game's atmosphere, reminiscent of titles like Fallout 3. The remaster also includes two bonus episodes, The Signal and The Writer, which expand on the game's mechanics, though the overall narrative remains ambiguous. Additionally, the removal of product placements from the original version is a welcome change.

In conclusion, Alan Wake Remastered feels like a polished reissue of a classic horror novel - visually appealing but fundamentally unchanged. While it has its flaws, it remains a cult classic worth the price of admission, with its haunting atmosphere and clever storytelling making it a worthwhile experience for newcomers to the series. Fans eagerly await future installments from Remedy Entertainment.
