201377536.review:
The most tedious zombie game I've ever played.

Yes, it's a zombie game. The enemies are called "The Taken" in this one, but their function and behavior is identical to those in any zombie game. And there's really only one type of them. You fight the exact same "Angry Zombies With Axes" over and over for the entire game. Actually, that's not true. Sometimes they have machetes.

The first half of the game is spent running through a dark forest at night. Just miles and miles of forest. The repetition is bad enough, but to make it worse, you can only run for about four seconds before you start breathing heavy and slow down to a light jog. Alan Wake is the most out-of-shape video game character I've ever played. It's probably true-to-life with regards to middle-aged writers, but when your endless night forest levels are this large, maybe you could've had your main character do some cardio on his off days...?

In fact, in one fight scene, I literally died because my character was bent over with his hands on his knees huffing and puffing instead of aiming his flashlight where I was pointing. Kudos to the game makers for including this animation, though; I actually appreciated the extra detail.

One bizarre decision the creators made was the angle at which Alan Wake walks. It's 3rd person, so the camera is behind the character, but instead of walking forward, he points slightly to the left (and heads that direction when you press forward). This is mostly a minor annoyance except during some platforming sequences which require crossing a thin board or tree. Having to find the perfect angle to point the camera so Alan Wake doesn't just walk off the side every few steps seemed like an unnecessary annoyance.

Another strange decision was CONSTANTLY taking away your weapons and flashlight. This sometimes happens multiple times in the same episode (there are six episodes total in the main game). You never get to use all the weapons and ammo you amass before having to start from zero.
 
Now, you do occasionally reach civilization between lightly jogging through forests. These scenes are mostly just walking simulators, where you listen to someone deliver exposition before finding an excuse to run off into a forest again. The plot isn't terrible, but it relies too much on overused and generic tropes. The main enemy is an amorphous "Darkness" that can basically do anything it wants because A Wizard Did It. 

Perhaps this was a Good For It's Time™ game. For reference, however, it came out in 2010. Left4Dead 2, a very similar (and far better) game gameplay-wise, came out in 2009.

Overall, Alan Wake feels like a number of other games from that era, where perhaps what the creators really wanted to do was make a movie, so they came up with some generic "lightly jog to the next location and kill some guys" action scenes to break up the story-delivery cutscenes.

200967475.review:
A pretty fun story and setting let down by atrocious combat. It's extremely dated and if you don't like the combat two hours in you might as well refund it because it doesn't meaningfully change for the rest of the game.

GOOD:

+ Premise is pretty fun, the story of a writer whose story is dictating a supernatural nightmare happening around him.

+ Visuals are okay for an old game. Looks like a 360/ PS3 era game for sure, but in a good way.

+ Aiming is responsive, shots are easy to land even without a reticule. Guns feel nice and accurate.

BAD:
- Combat is just awful. 

- Dodging is awful, you'll get stunlocked easily if enemies catch up to you which is entirely possible because you can only sprint for ~5 seconds and then it needs to cool down. It's really unresponsive and also tied to sprinting, which just feels awful.

- But also, combat isn't hard. It's the kind of tedious combat where it's super easy and repetitive until you slip up or the game hits you with a cheap shot that melts you. I got through pretty much 99% of the encounters on hard without dying.

- Enemy variety is awful, they're mostly all some sort of variation of 'dude sprinting at you'. Literally no enemy in this game is fun to fight, they're either too easy or too annoying. Enemies like sprinters are literally best dealt just standing still until they stop running around you. Crows will literally clip through the environment.

- Exploration is tedious and the supplies you get from exploring are meager. You're often just rewarded with a hatchet in your booty cheeks from an enemy spawning directly behind you.

- Boss fights are basically just normal enemies but more bullet spongy.

- Combat is pretty much nonstop and the pacing is exhausting, the funnest part of the game is when you're running around with an ally that can literally do the fighting for you because you finally get an actual break from the crappy combat.

- Level design is subpar and uninteresting. Environmental "puzzles" amounts to basically pulling a switch or lining up a hanging object. Every once in a while it'll open up to allow you to find collectibles but you'll mostly be trudging through samey woods, logging camps, and cabins.


MIXED:
~ Some characters are fun and interesting, like the talk show host and the sheriff, some are annoying like the dinner girl and Alan's "best friend" Barry. Some characters have pretty underwhelming performances like Alice Wake, Barry, and Alan himself.

~ Manuscripts are interesting but it's kind of weird to have a collectible that literally spoils the game?? The story is literally the only strong part of the game, yet some manuscript pages will spoil scenes and twists that have yet to happen. They needed more thought put into their placements.


NITPICKS:
~ Game is presented in an episodic format, but doesn't prompt you to exit between episodes. You might as well just cut out the awkward "End of Episode _" or just have it play linearly.

~ I didn't like the control layout. B to pick up items? I have to tap to load each bullet? I have to HOLD L3 to look at points of interest? I can't aim in without also boosting the flashlight? Not great, but you can get used to it.

~ Game wasn't scary at all, but honestly it promotes itself more as a thriller.


Dead Space and Resident Evil prove you can have a horror game with a lot of action and combat, and that fun and scary can go together. Alan Wake proves that if you can't do good combat you're better off not having it at all. It's let down by the fact that not one part of the game is outstanding enough to carry the rest, it's a bunch of a underbaked parts that don't add up to reach it's full potential, and it did have potential.

Control and Alan Wake 2 are highly acclaimed games that I was interested to play. However, in order to get to those, I was obligated to play through the first entry in the remedy-verse. I'm not too good with horror games, however this game is more like a action thriller. The ambience was unnerving and I had to take lots of breaks to put myself back together to continue playing the game. Story is good (although a little confusing), but the gameplay kinda sucked and combat was frustrating for the wrong reasons. It was fun to loiter around when things were happening, like watching episodes of the night springs tv shows or simply listening to people talk. Followed a guide near the end so I could simply tell where i was at in the episode and how long was left of the episodes cause they felt dragged on. Anyways, I'm glad I at least finished the game instead of dropping it halfway, despite being tempted to do so multiple times. I'm not going to bother with the dlc episodes as it looks like just less story and more challenging gameplay which doesn't sound enjoyable so not worth playing.

Played a bit of the first episode for this version as well. Honestly it holds up and this is probably a perfectly acceptable way to experience the first title.

Haunting me.

200661100.review: The story becomes bloated because of it's own ambition. It leans hard on mystery without always earning payoff. Revelations are uneven and the pacing is dragged down by overwritten dialogue and monologues that feel more indulgent than insightful. Remedy's writing wants to sound literary but it often falls into "forced" symbolism and melodrama. Combat is very shallow and very very very repetitive. Walk through spooky forest > Flashlight to stun enemies > Shoot them with a Revolver or a Shotgun > Find Batteries or Ammo and repeat for about 8 to 12 hours. Enemy variety is poor (mostly shadowy axe-wielders). Flashlight as weapon is clever but mechanically it wears thin quickly. You're fighting the same enemies in the same arenas with the same tools and the same mechanics for the majority of the game. No real system progression, no stealth and no meaningful puzzle-solving. For a "psychological thriller", this game rarely challenges your mind. Remedy really nails the tone. The atmosphere is masterful. From misty forest to flickering light, it captures the feeling of "you" being hunted and isolated for the rest of the game. If you're looking for a strong, mysterious story and creepy atmosphere with a slower, story-first experience then this is the game that you should buy, otherwise don't.

⭐ 8/10 – I Just Got Here, But I’m Intrigued ⭐

🕵️‍♂️ Alan Wake so far feels like a mix of Stephen King, Twin Peaks, and a flashlight battery commercial 🔦📚.

💖 The Good Stuff:
🌲 Spooky small-town vibes — fog, forests, and “something’s out there” tension 🌫️
📖 Narration + story hooks you fast (even if I’m still figuring out what’s going on)
🔦 Flashlight combat is actually pretty fun and unique
🎵 Sound design makes me constantly check behind me 😰
🎨 Atmospheric visuals — even dated, they work for the mood

💀 The Bad Stuff:
🔋 My flashlight eats batteries like it’s sponsored by Duracell 🔋😂
📦 Movement feels a little clunky compared to modern games
🧠 I’m still confused… but maybe that’s the point?

💡 Why it seems promising: Even a few hours in, I can tell this is a story worth seeing through. Creepy, weird, and just cinematic enough to keep me clicking “Continue.”

👍 Recommended? Yep — even if you’re new, it’s easy to get hooked 🎯
👎 Nope — if you hate reading notes, hearing narrators, or being in the dark 🌑

200331933.review:
Alan Wake would seem to be a game I would thoroughly enjoy. I adore linear, tight, narrative focused games. But Alan Wake fell short for me in some key areas that makes this game really hard to recommend playing it versus just watching a Youtube let's play instead. With that being said, I still recommend it. Because despite its flaws, it's original. It's captivating. The story, while not stellar, was a fun ride and certainly worth the $2 I paid for it.

To start, gameplay wise this game aged like milk. 90% of my issues with this game stem from the gameplay. It is so incredibly bland and at times frustrating. There are 3 or 4 enemy types in the game. All of the them except one are dealt with exactly the same way. Point a flashlight at them then shoot them 3-5 times. Their attacks are not very unique, besides one having a charge attack and another being able to throw axes at you. I kid you not this is almost the entire game. The "puzzles" are pitifully easy, and there are really only small variations to the "walk to new area, flip switches, start generators, get jumped by a bunch of enemies, move on" gameplay loop presented here. Alan controls pretty poorly on mouse and keyboard. When I give a dodge input, it takes 7 to 9 business days to actually start the animation. Sometimes it doesn't even register. Aiming is extremely annoying as well since the only thing you have to aim is a flashlight beam which sometimes doesn't even line up with where you're trying to shoot.

You're probably thinking now "Oh well if the gameplay is not great why don't you just run past enemies?" I would have loved to, except they are impossible to outrun and if you let enemies stack up on you you're essentially going to get stun locked to death. It doesn't help that Alan can only sprint for a VERY short period of time before slowing down to a snail's pace. Flares and flashbangs are good counters but you don't nearly have enough to just run past all the enemies. Most of the time you're stuck in an area till you kill them all anyway. You don't have to worry about ammo or inventory management, since the game dumps ammo and batteries on you every 4 seconds. No health items, no inventory. You rarely even pick anything up that isn't ammo or weapons. Besides flashbangs and flares I literally never ran out of ammo once in the entire game. Yikes. I understand this game isn't a survival horror, but why even have gameplay at this point? It's like the developers put all their eggs into the story and graphics (which looks decent for 2012) basket and did the bare minimum on everything else. Atmosphere was alright, but nowhere near the levels of Silent Hill 2, Signalis, and RE4 in the creepiness/mystery factor.

The levels are extremely linear, I was essentially on rails the entire game. No alternate routes, very little collectibles or upgrades. 0 replay value besides playing the game on nightmare difficulty for the extra manuscript pages. As far as I could tell, collecting thermos' did nothing except give you some Steam achievements. By following some signs you can get to caches, but all it gave you was a little more ammo (which you already didn't really need). I found some of the optional TV and radio show encounters pretty cool, since I wanted to experience the story in it's entirety. With that being said, it was done better in Control. Otherwise, there isn't much else here. Frankly, this gameplay loop sucked for a 2012 title.

I'm aware that the game is supposed to be narrative focused, but at the end of the day its a video game. I could easily argue to someone that you shouldn't even play the game and instead just watch a let's play on Youtube. You really aren't missing much on the gameplay front at all. Speaking of story, it was… good? I have played Control, and I found the story in Alan Wake to be slightly better imo. I enjoyed the Twin Peaks / Twilight Zone-esque vibes the story was inspired from. With that being said, it felt slightly corny at times and was pretty predicable. It was full of cliches and references to other inspirational media. Not a bad story, I'd put it slightly above average compared to other games I've played. I didn't find myself really liking any of the characters, and most of them felt pretty flat besides Alan himself.

TL;DR: Buy the game on a deep sale, or just watch a let's play on Youtube. You get the same solid story while not having to deal with the horrendous gameplay.

200054386.review:
Interesting game. I'll tell you the good, the bad and whether it's worth it to buy it and for how much.

Starting with the good - the overall story is interesting, like, what exactly is happening, are you living through a nightmare or is this real life? I haven't completed the story but guessing as the game is called Alan 'Wake', I have an idea.
The locations are well-done and good looking. 
There's okay weapon variety, nothing impressive but gets the job done.

The bad:
A little bit of a spoiler but the game can't decide whether the main hero should be killed or allowed to write his book. Like if the evil wants me to complete the book, it doesn't make sense to want to also kill me. So which one is it?
The story doesn't make much sense if you think about it for 2 seconds. Why does FBI want to immediately execute me? Even if they thought I killed someone, I'm walking around the city normally, like, no one else thinks I killed anybody. Why would the Darkness attack the police? If they kill me, that would accomplish the same goal as it wants which is also to kill me. So they're on the same team but it attack them for some reason and stops them from killing me.

The main hero is obnoxious. It seems like a self-insert from the main creator of the game but it also shows his flaws which is he overestimates his writing ability (which is funny in itself). He treats everyone including his girlfriend like dirt. He gives her a broken switch from his childhood as a gift LMAO. He beats up a random doctor who offers to give him psychological help. There's way too much monologue, instead of the game explaining your surroundings, they should let you immerse in the atmosphere. The character should only speak with himself if he offers something interesting to say rather than just reading the script of the game which you can also play yourself. 

Way too many on-the-nose references. Obviously they thought they were smart copying Steven King and using otherwise normal unsuspicious things as enemies. First, it's on the nose, secondly, flashing light at barrels is not the gaming prowess of game design they think it is. The second reference is the Twilight Zone, I mean I appreciate the series but subtlety and immersion in the atmosphere is in my opinion more important than just saying that you guys watched the series. Also the Twilight Zone always has that dread going in the background, not just that something bad will happen and out of nowhere but that everything can fall apart in an instant. For example, humans can be sent to a foreign planet to be served as food. The otherwise nerdy bookworm breaks his only glasses and is in a world that's completely destroyed with no one to help him get new ones so he'll starve and he won't be able to even read in his final moments. This game has no dread, it's just a few guys sometimes jumping you which you can kill with a granade or slowly with your pistol or shotgun. This game is also not horror so you're not scared even. 

This game tries to be a mix of horror and action adventure but the only series which has done it do a good extent is Resident Evil and the best games out of that series are ones which involve horror as the driving force with your character slowly improving and the game becoming more action-oriented as it goes on. This game doesn't have horror at all. If they wanted to do that, they had to remove the monologue and make the enemies real rather than a bunch of shadows. What if the whole thing wasn't a dream, what if there was an evil force at all times. No switching between dreams and reality where reality is a nice place to be. The game should slowly become more and more dreadful to play. If it wanted to be more action-oriented they had to give us massively better weapons to mow down shadows which sounds ridiculous because it is, so it doesn't work as an action oriented game at all. 

Would I pay the usual price of 13 euros? Nah. 10 euros? Buy a RE evil game instead of you're into horror. If it costs 5 euros or less? Maybe. As I said, it's a good looking game. The voice acting is generally well done. The combat is decent but unimpressive. The switching between dreams and reality is an interesting mechanic so long as you don't think about it too much what exactly are the goals of the Darkness as it stops making sense. 

Where should I put a game I paid 1-2 euros for? I guess I have had decent fun for that money which is basically the cost of 1 coffee. Is this game worth that money? Yeah I suppose but don't pay full price. As such, leaving a positive review but that's because this game is so cheap and has a few good qualities to it.

199805082.review:
You ever watched a great thriller television show? Breaking Bad, Dexter, even Twin Peaks? Here's Alan Wake, a game proudly boasting itself as a psychological thriller and which absolutely earns that title. 
Playing through what is presented as a T.V show-structured video game is kind of a unique thing (and frankly, is a lot better than Remedy's newer title, Quantum Break). Alan Wake takes the concept of light versus darkness and makes it into a fantastical (and terrifying) concept. The game has you running through the woods at night in Washington armed with a gun, a torch and maybe some flares, surviving against creatures of darkness taking the form of shady men. 

A lot of people seem to think that the lore and the game's plot is far too 'deep' for what the gameplay really boils down to, but this is a boring and uneducated take on the game. This game is fun, pure and simple. It's no surprise that Remedy, absolute masters of their craft, loved this game to a point that they actually seemed hesitant to try and follow it up with a worthy sequel, although I hear that has a lot to do with Microsoft's licensing agreements. The sequel, (ignoring Alan Wake's American Nightmare) finally did arrive thirteen years after THIS game's release, but sadly won't come to Steam. The game is very professionally refined, with virtually no crashing/glitches and not to mention a very fancy engine that makes the game look so pleasantly mesmerising I've actually considered visiting a Washington national park myself. Defeating the 'taken' is fun and satisfying, and the slow-motion dodging is extremely cinematic, and I feel was likely inspired by Max Payne's gameplay. There's action, decent puzzles, a plentiful helping of collectables, and some really fascinating environments to explore. 

The D.L.C comes free on Steam since this port is from 2012, two years after the game's so-called Xbox 360 exclusive launch. Having played Alan Wake on both platforms, it's a wild ride in equal terms. The one issue with playing on a mouse and keyboard is that you can no longer aim your gun/torch without draining your batteries, since the Xbox 360's pressure-sensitive triggers made it possible to 'aim halfway'. Strangely, the game's difficulty options on Steam are different too. What used to be simply Normal, Hard and Nightmare has become Easy, Normal and Nightmare. So players, be warned that for whatever reason, the names were changed for seemingly no reason, and that when you are selecting Normal on P.C you're actually playing the Xbox 360's Hard mode. Very strange. 

But even if you aren't a fan of the game's action and astonishing ambience (not that I can imagine why) then the game's plot should be enough to hook you. Each episode is structured like an episode of a T.V, making Alan Wake feel kind of like an interactive mini-series or short-lived show. Where Max Payne was a comic book, Alan Wake is bigger and better with drama, thrills, plot twists, and sheer mystery. The game was heavily inspired by Twin Peaks, and had an unprecedentedly long development period. In the end, the wait was really worth it, and I perceive this game as a fixture of the Xbox 360's legacy. Turns out, it's great to play on a computer too.

199708960.review:
An incredible story about a popular writer [spoiler] whose works start becoming reality[/spoiler]. I didn’t expect this game to be so good... During the tutorial, when the mechanics were introduced, I was skeptical about how they’d hold up later on, but it turns out the mechanic of lighting up enemies until you can kill them became quite satisfying.
I also didn’t expect the ability to drive a car in a linear game. It appeared rarely and only in moments where you had to get somewhere far away. The only annoying thing was the camera while driving.
The game has an amazing, unique atmosphere that immediately brings to mind the console where Alan Wake was initially exclusive — the Xbox 360. I have personal experience with Wake on that console as well.
There are moments with TVs where you can watch short “Night Springs” episodes, or radios where you can listen to conversations sometimes related to the story, or just enjoy the perfectly chosen songs that appear at the end of each episode (like “War” by Poets of the Fall — though I knew the band before, now I’ll only associate them with this game).
The game is a “playable series” divided into five amazing episodes, each one more intense and immersive than the last.

199708519.review:
Great Premise, Stumbling Execution

Alan Wake is one of those games that sounds amazing on paper — a psychological thriller about a writer trapped in a surreal nightmare, battling darkness with a flashlight and a revolver. The atmosphere is drenched in Twin Peaks and Stephen King vibes, and for the first hour or two, it’s genuinely intriguing. But sadly, the more I played, the more the cracks started to show.

The biggest issue is how repetitive it all becomes. Every encounter plays out almost identically: shine the light, shoot, repeat. At first, it’s a cool mechanic — using light as a weapon — but the game never evolves it in meaningful ways. There’s little variation in enemy design, and combat quickly becomes a chore, especially with the sluggish movement and awkward dodge system.

The pacing is also a problem. While the episodic structure is a neat touch in theory, it often drags in practice. Long stretches of walking through forests or empty roads, broken up by clunky combat, make the experience feel padded rather than purposeful. Instead of building suspense, the game just spins its wheels.

The story tries to be deep and mysterious, but it’s often vague to the point of confusion. It constantly hints at bigger ideas — metafiction, trauma, loss — but rarely explores them with any depth. Much of the narrative unfolds through cryptic manuscript pages, and while they add flavor, they’re not enough to make the plot feel coherent or emotionally grounded.

And while the visuals and lighting effects were impressive at release, much of the level design is uninspired — just endless woods, cabins, and dimly lit roads. It starts to blend together long before the game is over.

I wanted to get lost in Alan Wake’s strange world, but it felt more like I was just trudging through it. There’s an interesting core here, and I can see why it has its fans, but as an overall experience, it’s repetitive, uneven, and too often feels like style over substance.

A strong concept weighed down by dated mechanics and shallow storytelling.

199676801.review:
Back to Bright Falls with Alan Wake Remastered — and the nightmare still shines.

I played the original on Xbox 360, so firing it up on PC was pure nostalgia. The core loop is intact (and still fun): burn away shadows with your flashlight, then finish the Taken with revolvers, shotguns, rifles, or the ever‑satisfying flare gun. Flares push back crowds; flash‑bangs erase them outright — perfect when you’re swarmed in a dark forest at 2 a.m. It still feels like Max Payne meets a Stephen King episode.

Atmosphere is where this game wins. Six TV‑style episodes deliver classic small‑town‑horror vibes: misty pines, humming streetlights, and that lonely Cauldron Lake. The story of a blocked writer searching for his kidnapped wife is strong, but you only get the full picture by collecting manuscript pages, tuning in to radios, and watching in‑game TV. Completionists will love that; casual players might miss key beats.

Visuals are sharper and lighting is gorgeous, yet the decade‑old animations peek through, and combat can feel repetitive once you’ve mastered the flashlight‑then‑shoot rhythm. Still, at a sale price of a couple of dollars this is an absolute steal: a moody action‑thriller that holds up surprisingly well.

Verdict: A cozy horror classic worth revisiting — especially if you’ve never walked the shadowy trails of Bright Falls.

199316441.review:
After spending about 40 hours playing and replaying this game, religiously collecting every piece of the Manuscript and every single Coffee Thermos (Which I still  have absolutely no idea what they are even used for), I can safely say that I am obsessed with Alan Wake! 

It’s not exactly the aged game mechanics that keeps dragging me back - because let’s be honest, why should anyone expect a writer to be good at combat or running from possessed Psychos! - but it’s the story and characters that kept me there. It’s the awe, it’s the primal fear of the dark, the unknown, the little details and the music all together. It’s the genius of Sam Lake and the Remedy game creators that finally made me see what a powerful medium video games can be for interactive storytelling. And no matter how immersive you want the experience to be, there is always something for you to find out on your own by connecting the dots - preferably while highly caffeinated on Oh Deer Diner high quality coffee and blasting Old Gods of Asgard/Poets of the Fall.

Playing Alan Wake, I am Alice going down the rabbit hole/Lake that is Remedy’s Connected Universe and I don’t believe I will ever Return from. Because [i]"it's not a Lake, it's an Ocean."[/i]

198902879.review:
The game's atmospheric design is truly second to none, immersing players in a richly detailed and chilling world that stays with you long after playing. Set in the eerie and enigmatic town of Bright Falls, the environment itself becomes almost like a living, breathing character. Every corner of this small town—from the dense, whispering forests surrounding it to the weathered, abandoned cabins and dilapidated buildings—has been carefully crafted to evoke a sense of isolation, mystery, and creeping dread.

Bright Falls feels authentic and lived-in, with subtle environmental storytelling woven into its design. Fallen leaves crunch underfoot, fog drifts lazily through the trees, and distant animal calls echo eerily through the night. The attention to detail invites players to explore every nook and cranny, rewarding curiosity with unsettling discoveries that deepen the overall sense of unease.

What truly elevates the atmosphere is the game’s dynamic lighting system. Shadows stretch and shift unpredictably, casting haunting silhouettes that seem to lurk just beyond the edge of vision. This interplay of light and darkness transforms even familiar locations into unpredictable and sometimes terrifying spaces. The lighting is not just cosmetic—it actively shapes the mood, heightening tension and suspense with every flicker of a lantern, every flash of lightning, and every glimmer of moonlight piercing through the thick canopy overhead.

Sound design works hand in hand with visuals to reinforce the mood. The creak of a floorboard, the distant howl of the wind, or the sudden snap of a twig can make you jump, reminding you that danger might be lurking just out of sight. All these elements come together to create an immersive experience that keeps players constantly on edge, feeling vulnerable yet compelled to uncover the dark secrets Bright Falls hides.

In sum, the game's atmospheric design is a masterclass in world-building. It creates a hauntingly beautiful yet oppressive environment that perfectly captures the tension and mystery at the heart of the narrative. Players don’t just walk through Bright Falls—they live it.

198422592.review:
First playthrough review time.

The game has very good visuals that help create a dark and mysterious mood. The lighting and shadow effects look nice, and many small objects have high detail. Forest areas, hills, and old buildings look natural and fit the tone well. Light on water, glass, and wooden surfaces looks realistic. Sometimes, when driving fast, objects appear late on the screen, but this does not reduce the overall quality.

Sound is also very well made. Steps, water, and movement sounds are clear and match the moment perfectly. The voices fit the look and behavior of each character, and the dialogues support the atmosphere strongly. Music plays at the end of each section and connects well with what happened before, making every part feel complete and meaningful.

In conclusion, the game builds a very strong atmosphere through its graphics, sound, and realistic movement. It uses known ideas but does so in an excellent way. The unclear events, lack of maps, and dark setting create constant tension throughout the story. Even with some parts that are hard to follow, the game’s design holds the whole experience very well. For those who like detailed settings and slow, tense stories, this game is a full and very well-made title.

198331934.review: The main selling point is the story and I think it was great. The episodic, television series, nature was a neat change of pace and fit well with the writer/storyteller theme. It is linear, from an age before open world everything, with standard off the beaten path caches, so you can pretty much plow straight ahead; it certainly doesn't overstay its welcome. You could replay it or really take your time to find all the collectibles, but I didn't focus on that so I can't say if it is worthwhile. The only negative is the combat, which has a cool idea of wearing the baddies down with light before you can kill them, but that is hampered IMHO by the difficulty in seeing what is happening (possibly a design decision since the focus is on darkness) and the cinematic nature of your dodge that disorients you even more. 8/10.

A decade-old game still holds up to this day; some parts could be better, but even then it is enjoyable and has a unique storytelling. Set in the Remedy Universe, Alan Wake is the first installment. Well, how was it? Good. It did become a cult classic for a long time and got its long-awaited sequel in the year 2023.

First of all, making a middle-aged man the main protagonist makes the character slow; those who have played this game would understand what I'm trying to say. Alan Wake's character can be interpreted in many ways; you could say he is at times untrustable as a narrator, but that belongs to a whole different discussion. That being said, the story was engaging. I wouldn't say that it was mind-blowing, but it was kind of close to it; the twists were great.

I did really like the overall setting and atmosphere of the whole game; the dark and eerie atmosphere was great, though I wish the Taken, aka the enemies, were a bit more diverse. But the enemy wave sometimes felt dragged out, blocking the path. It was super annoying at first, but by the end I adjusted.

The gameplay was decent; there is nothing too unique or new in here. The execution was good; it fit the narrative well. Though the stamina bar could be a bit better, it was way too low. The map feature felt kind of useless to me; they could've just used an arrow on the upper screen. One thing I loved was the feature to pause the game at any moment. I cannot stress enough how good of a feature that is!

198255247.review:
TL;DR at the bottom.

So I finally finished this game on my third attempt, and did the two bonus chapters. I quit this game back on Xbox 360 over a decade ago because the gameplay was so boring on account of it being too simple and repetitive, and I still stand by that. The gameplay and combat is the textbook definition of repetitive, but I can normally handle repetitive, and often throw myself into the same gamplay loops in other games for hours on end and have a blast. Where Alan Wake truly fails is its lack of depth to it's combat. It's always some form of use flashlight and flares, then shoot weapon. The flare gun and flash bangs also exist as quick aces up your sleeve, and there's a few driving sections where you can run over The Taken. (Side note, I wonder if Bungie grabbed that enemy idea of shadowy stolen people called The Taken being used by The Darkness to attack the player. If not, it's a funny coincidence.)

The reason I'm giving this game a positive review is for it's story. Something that was intriguing to me as a college student, but I couldn't get past the gameplay back then. And the only real reason I even bothered returning to this game to finally finish it, was because of Remedy Entertainment's other game in the same universe: Control.

Knowing this game takes place in the same continuity as Control, and having had a much more fun time with Control's gameplay, and I mean that by miles and miles, I was finally interested to see what Alan Wake's story was, and it is a VERY interesting story having come from the context of Control first.

I have no earthly idea if Alan Wake 2 will be enjoyable from my perspective, of greatly disliking Alan Wake 1's gameplay, and absolutely LOVING Control's, but I expect I will be waiting a while, since Epic plans to keep it as a PC exclusive. My only hope is that Remedy's other projects earn them enough money for them to buy out their contract with Epic and finally get Alan Wake 2 on Steam.

TL;DR: If you can stomach extremely repetitive gameplay with almost non-existent depth or complexity, then you will be treated to a fascinating supernatural horror story that fits in nicely with the game Control. If you can't stomach that, just watch a playthrough on YouTube, but with all the licensed music muted due to copyright claims.

Also, play Control.

197450439.review:
I highly recommend this game. The narrative is where this game truly shines. It's simultaneously one of the craziest stories and most entertaining and interesting stories I've ever played in a game. I never felt bored of the story, and I was constantly interested to know what was really happening to Alan Wake and the town. 

My only issue with this game is that it unfortunately felt a few hours longer than it actually was, and I blame that primarily on the gameplay. The gameplay is fun for sure, but its novelty lasts for only a few hours. Soon enough, the enjoyment I received from the gameplay wore off and I was left with several hours of doing the same thing to much dismay. What you're shown from the opening of the game is what you end up having to do over and over again for 10ish hours. It got to the point where by the end of the game I really just dreaded any encounters with any taken, because it really is just the same gameplay loop again and again with no real variety between encounters, and the enemies almost never change either. I too often just tried to run away from groups of enemies to get to the next safe haven. Also, while all of the environments are extremely beautiful, a lot of it ends up being spent running through very similar-looking forests, which becomes a little monotonous eventually. However, I don't want that to distract from the fact that the game is still great, even if the gameplay became really old really quickly. I'm very happy that I played it, and I bet most people would be. 

Now, it would be nice if poopy Epic Games would allow Alan Wake II to be on Steam!

[b] 8.5/10 [/b]

196955464.review:
"A Psychological Thriller That Will Haunt You Long After the Credits Roll"
🌟 Rating: 9/10

Alan Wake isn’t just a game—it’s an experience. A beautifully crafted blend of psychological horror, cinematic storytelling, and atmospheric exploration that grabs you from the very first minute and refuses to let go.

🖋️ Story & Narrative:
You play as Alan Wake, a best-selling writer caught in a surreal nightmare where his own words come to life. Set in the eerie town of Bright Falls, the story unfolds like a gripping mystery novel—layered, suspenseful, and full of brilliant twists. Every episode feels like an episode of a psychological thriller series, complete with cliffhangers that’ll keep you saying, “Just one more hour…”

🔦 Gameplay Mechanics:
The flashlight is your greatest weapon. In a world where darkness is your enemy, combat is more about light vs. shadow than bullets vs. monsters. The unique mechanic of burning away darkness before dealing damage keeps every encounter tense and strategic. It’s simple yet incredibly effective at building dread.

🌲 Atmosphere & World:
Bright Falls is hauntingly beautiful—misty forests, empty cabins, and flickering streetlights create a world that feels both dreamlike and disturbingly real. The environmental storytelling is masterclass; even walking through the woods can make your skin crawl.

🎵 Soundtrack & Voice Acting:
The soundtrack, combined with excellent voice acting and haunting narration, builds an atmosphere that’s dripping with tension. Songs from Poets of the Fall and the episodic structure make this feel like a playable TV show. The pacing is perfect.

🧠 Conclusion:
Alan Wake is more than a horror game—it’s an unforgettable psychological journey. Whether you’re here for the story, the suspense, or the thrill of fighting the darkness, this is one of those rare games that stays with you long after it’s over.

💡 Are you brave enough to step into the dark and face the nightmare written in your own words?

I have my fair share of complaints. Game is FAR from perfect. Still tho, I had a very good time. The atmosphere was ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ INCREDIBLE. I feel in an alternate universe, we got a Remedy Slenderman game instead of this and it woulda been the greatest creepypasta game of all time. Alan Wake himself is an AMAZING main character. His narration and character development where awesome. Also, side characters like Barry, the Old Gods brothers, and Sheriff Breaker were super nice. The flashlight mechanic was also PEEEEAK. The way it always forced u to be on the backfoot of fights at the start with the immediate turntables as soon as u fully lit up enemies was super cool. Another thing was the Poltergeist enemies were easily my fav enemy type in the game. Any time enemies have physics involved in their attacks, its sick af. Finally, the last major positive is the story DLC for the normal game is kinda peak??? If the game before was already psychological horror, the DLC takes it even further with some of the coolest visuals and set-pieces in the whole game. Episode 3 and Special 2 are some of the highest peaks in the game.

The main negatives I got tho starts with the lack of enemy and weapon variety. This is my biggest problem with the game. Outside of the poltergeists and crows, there is essentially one more enemy type in the game. Because they all work the same, just with all having varying health pools and speeds. Which is boring imo. There is only one of them that has a unique mechanic, and its the one that runs REALLY fast. However, he only runs fast to avoid your flashlight, not to actually attack u. So usually whenever I faced it, I just stayed still until it tried to lunge at me, which, ironically, he did that SLOWLY. So that enemy was a miss imo. As for the weapons, 5 of them in the whole game is kinda abysmal tbh. If the game was survival horror, id get it slightly more, but its action horror and action games rely more on having varied enemy types and weapons to keep their combat fresh. Somehow tho, American Nightmare fixes ALL OF THIS??? The weapons and enemy types double in that DLC, which is insane imo. They 110% shoulda put all that stuff into the base game and it woulda fixed that issue i had.

My other slightly less major complaint is Episode 5 is a bit of lull in the plot imo. This is mainly cause the very title of it spoils the reveal at the end. And that's an issue i have with the collectibles too tbh. I like the idea of them teasing future events, but i kinda wish they went more the route of them being ominous teases, instead of straight up telling u what happens. Some of the pages did try to be a bit ominous and it was really cool.

My last even less major complaint is technical, and its that u can tell they used the Max Payne engine for this game and its being pushed to its ABSOLUTE LIMIT. Texture work is what has mainly suffered in this case. A lot of it seemed to have to be done low quality so everything worked could run, and even now, at least the PC version, still has those textures on some stuff. Def should have gotten an upgrade by now tbh. Animations also feel like they coulda been better in some aspects. Makes the game feel more dated than it should. Especially cause stuff like the flashlight mechanic was kinda ahead of its time ngl.

All in all tho, I know I just talked a lot of bad, but that's just cause the bad usually requires more explaining. My time with the game was great and i couldn't be happier i played it. Also, playing through AW1 and knowing how it kinda flopped when it came out has showed me how much of a miracle it is the fact it got a sequel, let alone a sequel as good as AW2 (from everything i heard at least). Genuinely cant wait to get to it.

196799991.review:
I played this game because it was listed as recommendation by DDG's AI, and I paid the hard way.
I was hoping for a game like RE4HD, but my experiences playing this game made me so disappointed of even trying---that my brain just went poof hearing every psychological concepts in this game. Then, a lot of references towards other people's work... huh?! Why would you add other people's ideas and reference from them when you should be creating your own narrative in this very game... I'm appalled. I won't judge.

Another gripe I have for this game would be: For a game that is probably the first installment to the series, there's barely any commitment of educating the players when it comes to moving to another location. Too much mystery.
GAME: "[i]Try to learn how to play my game, idiot[/i]." lol (that's just bad in many ways)

Playing this game seems a rather enjoyable game if you're into psychological warfare and really have yet tackled the very truth of things in the world. As someone who have the experiences of nightmares, the concept of "burning the dark with your flashlight" was cringe at best. But even then, the story in the prologue kept giving me ♥♥♥♥♥♥ psychological nonsense and I can't even stomach hearing...

Graphics: 3.5/10 (RE4HD's is much more crispy, and this one is much more like a simulation)
Sound: 2.5/10 (For a game that's barely commendable imo, the sound is decent.)
Concepts: 0.5/10 (Psychological Horror? More like Psychological garbage)
 Characters: 2/10 (Sorry, Alan, but I dislike your character. For someone who has a wife, his attitude towards his wife when she's comforting you... ugh, I can't stomach people like him, as I see my past self of being ungrateful to other people. Such character in a game isn't ideal, especially when you give these kinds of game to your loved ones, and I'd rather not watch any depravity from this character or I'll fall again in depravity.)
Controls: 0/10 (F yeah, this one is quite annoying. The speed of camera is annoying when using a gaming mouse, and the crosshair is in the fluffing flashlight... lmao?!)

All in all, I wouldn't recommend this to any sane people who have their consciences enlightened after reading the [url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pYZw0dfLmLk][b]>Bible<[/b].[/url]

If you want to play this game to know what FEAR really is.
Don't.
[url=https://youtu.be/fcqxzXMRGoc]Watch this instead.[/url]
With love.

196238914.review:
While some mechanics like the shared dodge/run button and the low stamina can sometimes be annoying especially when trying to flee enemies, the overall gameplay felt much smoother and engaging than how others have described it. While there is no crosshair, aiming wasn't as hard as perceived from gameplay videos, thanks to some sort of bullet magnetism being present. Early on. The game was supposed to be open world but that idea was ditched by the final release, however this still impacted how the world felt thanks to how the locations were interconnected in a meaningful way.
The game's strong atmosphere, compelling characters, and  strong writing deliver a well crafted story experience although some of the  more interesting background information was left out of the game and instead presented in side materials such as comics, a web series, and novels.
Despite both DLCs being strong narratively, the second one suffered in gameplay sections that required some platforming to be done, due to the more realistic way Alan moves and controls. This made some jumps harder to land, resulting in having to reload the last checkpoint (or restart the whole episode if you want to get the achievement for finishing the DLC without dying).
8/10

196236411.review:
Alan Wake is mainly a horror-shooter game that is based on the paranormal story of a writer (Alan Wake himself). Story of the game begins with Wake going on a vacation with his wife to a remote lake town. When they reach the town, his wife mysteriously disappears and he begins to search for her.

You go to many different places in the search for your wife. In the game, you mainly fight with the "dark presence", which is a kind of evil energy that possesses people and objects and make them attack you. You mainly use guns and flashlight in order to fight with enemies. Enemies who are possessed by dark presence is also protected by it, so you need to use light to destroy the protection barrier on enemies before you kill them with a gun.

Playing mechanics are pretty simple and easy to understand. Guns, flashlights and ammo are available in certain areas, you just need to explore the environment a bit. The game perfectly mix the story with the gameplay. Although fights and behaviours of enemies begin to be repetitive and predictable after some time, story always manages to keep you focused on the game and the dark and haunting atmosphere always keeps the tension alive and the scenary is great. The game is also very good at describing the psychological situation of a protagonist who sometimes finds it difficult to differenciate between reality and dream and it is also an important factor that keeps your interest during the game.

196053132.review:
Alan Wake – Отзыв человека, который слишком много читал Стивена Кинга перед сном
Подзаголовок: "Фонарик – мой новый лучший друг, а тьма – бывший."

Привет, любители пощекотать нервы и покричать в подушку! Сегодня мы поговорим об Alan Wake – игре, которая доказывает, что:

Писатели – самые несчастные люди на свете (особенно если их жёны пропадают, а вокруг бегают тени с топорами).

Жить в лесу – плохая идея, особенно если этот лес находится где-то в штате Мэн… то есть, простите, в Вашингтоне.

Фонарики – undervalued (спасибо, игра, теперь у меня в каждой комнате по три штуки).

📖 Сюжет – "Я писатель, у меня кризис жанра… и жены"
Вы – Алан Уэйк, успешный писатель с лицом типичного голливудского актёра, который слишком много курит и мало спит. Ваша жена пропадает, вы оказываетесь в городке Брайт Фоллз (который, вопреки названию, совсем не яркий), а вокруг тьма, паранойя и страницы вашей книги, которые вы… не помните, чтобы писали.

Сюжет – это микс "Секретных материалов", "Сияния" и вашего самого странного сна после пачки энергетиков. Персонажи говорят загадками, злодей похож на мистера Хайда из дешёвого театра, а главный антагонист – сама Тьма (и да, она явно обиделась на вас лично).

🎮 Геймплей – "Беги, свети, кричи"
Механика проста:

Враг появляется из тьмы (как нежданный гость в 3 ночи).

Вы светите на него фонариком (как будто говорите: "Нет, я не сплю, уходи!").

Когда он "освещён" – можно стрелять (потому что пули, очевидно, боятся темноты).

Проблемы начинаются, когда:

Батарейки садятся (спасибо, Duracell, вы могли бы спонсировать Алана).

Врагов больше, чем патронов (классика survival horror).

Вы бежите к свету, а он оказывается галлюцинацией (тьма явно троллит).

🌲 Атмосфера – "Твин Пикс, но без чертовски хорошего кофе"
Графика местами устарела, но лес ночью всё ещё жутковат, особенно когда:

Ветки хватают вас за ноги (или это просто куст?.. НЕТ, ЭТО НЕ КУСТ!).

Из радиоприёмников доносятся обрывки новостей (и да, все они про исчезновения и смерть).

Где-то вдалеке играет рок-н-ролл (потому что даже в аду должен быть саундтрек).

Саундтрек – это Pet Shop Boys vs Nine Inch Nails, если бы они записали альбом в заброшенном мотеле.

😱 Страхи – "Почему я играю в это ночью?"
Игра не пугает скримерами, но зато умеет нагнетать паранойю:

Тени двигаются, когда вы не смотрите (как в "Докторе Кто", только страшнее).

Предметы летают сами по себе (тьма явно не любит вашу мебель).

Каждый второй диалог звучит как предсказание смерти (спасибо, местные, теперь я не сплю).

👍 Плюсы:
✔ Атмосфера как в хорошем триллере (если любите Кинга и Линча – это ваш must-play).
✔ Отличный саундтрек (особенно треки Poets of the Fall).
✔ Сюжет с неожиданными поворотами (хотя некоторые из них – как удар табуреткой по голове).

👎 Минусы:
❌ Бойцовка иногда клинит (Алан бежит не туда, патроны кончаются в худший момент).
❌ Повторяющиеся враги (да, мы поняли, тьма любит лесорубов).
❌ Концовка оставляет вопросы (но это же Ремеди, они всегда так делают).

💯 Вердикт:
Alan Wake – это как если бы Стивен Кинг написал сценарий для "Форсажа", но потом передумал и решил снять хоррор.

Если вы любите атмосферные истории с налётом безумия – игра для вас.
Если же боитесь темноты – ну… купите ещё один фонарик.

Оценка: 8.5/10 (потому что 10/10 будет, когда Алан наконец выспится).

195791855.review: Alan Wake is a total mind-bender, blending creepy storytelling with some seriously spooky vibes that will have you on the edge of your seat, clutching your controller tight. The game’s dark, atmospheric visuals and eerie sound design create a legit horror movie feel, making every shadow feel like it’s hiding some dark secret. It’s a wild ride that’s perfect for horror fans who love a good scare, with plenty of jump scares and creepy moments to keep your heart pounding. Overall, it’s a must-play for anyone looking to get their scare on and dive into a mysterious, chilling world.
